### Josh — 2014-11-29T01:54:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-148742>

[http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v5/n9/full/cddis2014364a.html](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v5/n9/full/cddis2014364a.html)

> Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a devastating condition of CNS that often results in severe functional impairments for which there are no restorative therapies. As in other CNS injuries, in addition to the effects that are related to the primary site of damage, these impairments are caused by degeneration of distal regions that are connected functionally to the primary lesion site. Modulation of the endocannabinoid system (ECS) counteracts this neurodegeneration, and pharmacological modulation of type-2 cannabinoid receptor (CB2R) is a promising therapeutic target for several CNS pathologies, including SCI. This study examined the effects of CB2R modulation on the fate of axotomized rubrospinal neurons (RSNs) and functional recovery in a model of spinal cord dorsal hemisection (SCH) at the cervical level in rats. SCH induced CB2R expression, severe atrophy, and cell death in contralateral RSNs. Furthermore, SCH affected molecular changes in the apoptotic cascade in RSNs – increased cytochrome _c_release, apoptosome formation, and caspase-3 activity. CB2R stimulation by its selective agonist JWH-015 significantly increased the bcl-2/bax ratio, reduced cytochrome _c_ release, delayed atrophy and degeneration, and improved spontaneous functional recovery through ERK1/2 inactivation. These findings implicate the ECS, particularly CB2R, as part of the endogenous neuroprotective response that is triggered after SCI. Thus, CB2R modulation might represent a promising therapeutic target that lacks psychotropic effects and can be used to exploit ECS-based approaches to counteract neuronal degeneration.

[http://www.nature.com/neuro/journal/v15/n4/full/nn.3053.html](http://www.nature.com/neuro/journal/v15/n4/full/nn.3053.html)

> The mammalian brain is one of the organs with the highest energy demands, and mitochondria are key determinants of its functions. Here we show that the type-1 cannabinoid receptor (CB1) is present at the membranes of mouse neuronal mitochondria (mtCB1), where it directly controls cellular respiration and energy production. Through activation of mtCB1 receptors, exogenous cannabinoids and _in situ_ endocannabinoids decreased cyclic AMP concentration, protein kinase A activity, complex I enzymatic activity and respiration in neuronal mitochondria. In addition, intracellular CB1 receptors and mitochondrial mechanisms contributed to endocannabinoid-dependent depolarization-induced suppression of inhibition in the hippocampus. Thus, mtCB1receptors directly modulate neuronal energy metabolism, revealing a new mechanism of action of G protein–coupled receptor signaling in the brain.

### Josh — 2014-11-29T04:32:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-148745>

[http://www.hindawi.com/journals/sci/2013/796715/](http://www.hindawi.com/journals/sci/2013/796715/)

> **Abstract**
> Significant loss of bone due to trauma, underlying metabolic disease, or lack of repair due to old age surpasses the body’s endogenous bone repair mechanisms. Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) are adult stem cells which may represent an ideal cell type for use in cell-based tissue engineered bone regeneration strategies. The body’s endocannabinoid system has been identified as a central regulator of bone metabolism. The aim of the study was to elucidate the role of the cannabinoid receptor type 1 in the differentiation and survival of MSCs. We show that the cannabinoid receptor type 1 has a prosurvival function during acute cell stress. Additionally, we show that the phytocannabinoid, -Tetrahydrocannabinol, has a negative impact on MSC survival and osteogenesis. Overall, these results show the potential for the modulation of the cannabinoid system in cell-based tissue engineered bone regeneration strategies whilst highlighting cannabis use as a potential cause for concern in the management of orthopaedic patients.

[http://www.medicinalgenomics.com/wp...nges-in-respiration-of-brain-mitochondria.pdf](http://www.medicinalgenomics.com/wp-content/uploads/2011/12/Cannabinoind-induced-changes-in-respiration-of-brain-mitochondria.pdf)

> { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }
>
>
>
> A B S T R A C T
> Cannabinoids exert various biological effects that are either receptor-mediated or independent of receptor
> signaling. Mitochondrial effects of cannabinoids were interpreted either as non-receptor-mediated
> alterationofmitochondrialmembranes, or as indirect consequences of activation ofplasmamembrane type
> 1 cannabinoid receptors (CB1). Recently, CB1 receptors were confirmed to be localized to the membranes of
> neuronal mitochondria, where their activation directly regulates respiration and energy production. Here,
> we performed in-depth analysis of cannabinoid-induced changes of mitochondrial respiration using both
> an antagonist/inverse agonist of CB1 receptors, AM251 and the cannabinoid receptor agonists,
> D9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), cannabidiol, anandamide, and WIN 55,212-2. Relationships were
> determined between cannabinoid concentration and respiratory rate driven by substrates of complex I,
> II or IV in pig brain mitochondria. Either full or partial inhibition of respiratory rate was found for the tested
> drugs, with an IC50 in the micromolar range, which verified the significant role of non-receptor-mediated
> mechanism in inhibiting mitochondrial respiration. Effect of stepwise application of THC and
> AM251 evidenced protective role of AM251 and corroborated the participation of CB1 receptor activation
> inthe inhibitionofmitochondrial respiration.Weproposed amodel,which includesboth receptor- andnonreceptor-mediated
> mechanisms of cannabinoid action on mitochondrial respiration. This model explains
> both the inhibitory effect of cannabinoids and the protective effect of the CB1 receptor inverse agonist.
> ã 2014 Published by Elsevier Ireland Ltd.

### Josh — 2014-11-29T04:42:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-148747>

> Figure 1. Then, because of these widespread properties of cannabinoid
> compounds, targeting the ECS could represent a unique and
> reliable opportunity to advance toward an effective therapy against
> the AD. Moreover, cannabinoids might represent a safe low-cost
> therapy, with their natural origin and low side effects profile. From
> our point of view, the success of cannabinoid-based therapy in
> AD could be increased taking into account two important aspects:
> (i) the use of a combination of compounds that cover the whole
> spectrum of therapeutic properties described for cannabinoids,
> i.e., combination of CB1 and CB2 receptors agonists plus CBD,
> which presents interesting neuroprotective properties spite of its
> mechanism of action remaining poorly understood, and (ii) the
> early initiation of the treatment in the neurodegenerative process,
> which ensures the integrity of the ECS target components and
> increases the possibility of curbing the exponential degenerative
> progression toward dementia.

> **Abstract**
> The limited effectiveness of current therapies against Alzheimer's disease (AD) highlights the need for intensifying research efforts devoted to developing new agents for preventing or retarding the disease process. During the last few years, targeting the endogenous cannabinoid system has emerged as a potential therapeutic approach to treat Alzheimer. The endocannabinoid system is composed by a number of cannabinoid receptors, including the well-characterized CB1 and CB2 receptors, with their endogenous ligands and the enzymes related to the synthesis and degradation of these endocannabinoid compounds. Several findings indicate that the activation of both CB1 and CB2 receptors by natural or synthetic agonists, at non-psychoactive doses, have beneficial effects in Alzheimer experimental models by reducing the harmful β-amyloid peptide action and tau phosphorylation, as well as by promoting the brain's intrinsic repair mechanisms. Moreover, endocannabinoid signaling has been demonstrated to modulate numerous concomitant pathological processes, including neuroinflammation, excitotoxicity, mitochondrial dysfunction, and oxidative stress. The present paper summarizes the main experimental studies demonstrating the polyvalent properties of cannabinoid compounds for the treatment of AD, which together encourage progress toward a clinical trial.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24634659](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24634659)

### Josh — 2014-11-29T05:05:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-148749>

Looking into a bit, I am guessing that the response to cannabanoids, endogenous and exogenous, will be an issue without reliable fatty acid metabolism for both formation and degradation.A positive response might be indicative of issues related to handling Omega 6 and Arachidonic Acid precursors and or the production of the enzymes for degradation.

> The characterization of CB1 and CB2 receptors permitted the discovered of endocannabinoids or cannabinoids produced and released by nerve cells. Endocannabinoids are lipid compounds of the eicosanoid family derived from the degradation of membrane phospholipids ([Piomelli, 2003](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3942876/#B85)). The most representative are arachidonoylethanolamine (AEA), also named anandamide, and 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG), although several others have also been identified, such as 2-arachidonylglyceryl ether (2-AGE), virodhamine, and _N_\-arachidonyldopamine ([Piomelli, 2003](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3942876/#B85)). Endocannabinoids act as neurotransmitters since they are synthesized and released by neurons, are able to bind and activate membrane receptors, and are inactivated by reuptake and enzymatic degradation within the cell. However, endocannabinoids have two fundamental characteristics that differentiate them from other neurotransmitters: they act as retrograde messengers and they do not accumulate in the interior of synaptic vesicles ([Wilson and Nicoll, 2002](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3942876/#B109)). For several years, endocannabinoid compounds have been supposed to be exclusively synthesized on demand to act on cells located near their site of synthesis, and then to be rapidly inactivated by the action of specific degradation enzymes. However, recent studies have shown intracellular stores of anandamide in places other than synaptic vesicles as in adiposomes where it is sequestered and concentrated to higher levels than in the extracellular space ([Hillard and Jarrahian, 2000](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3942876/#B49); [Maccarrone et al., 2010](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3942876/#B64)). Moreover, an active intracellular binding of anandamide to TRPV1 and PPARs suggest an additional role of anandamide as a second messenger in intracellular signaling ([Maccarrone et al., 2010](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3942876/#B64)). This new scenario contemplates the possibility that anandamide may act as an autocrine/paracrine ligand of CB receptors but also as an intracellular ligand to TRPV1 and PPARs; moreover, the presence of extracellular anandamide transporters would point anandamide as an endocrine messenger ([Maccarrone et al., 2010](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3942876/#B64)).

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3942876/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3942876/)

The PPAR binding catches my eye as well...

### Josh — 2014-11-29T05:11:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-148750>

I recall Dr. Kruse mentioning that Cannabanoids might be useful when redox is poor...

> CANNABINOID ACTIONS ON MITOCHONDRIA ACTIVITY: OXIDATIVE
> STRESS AND ENERGY METABOLISM
> Mitochondria are vital cellular components essential for ATP
> production and calcium homeostasis. The relevance of these
> organelles in neurons is even greater than in other cell types
> because neurons are highly demanding energy cells mainly dependent
> on aerobic oxidative phosphorylation, due to their limited
> capacity for glycolysis. Long axons require energy transport
> over long distances, and synaptic transmission depends on calcium
> signals. Mitochondria are abundant in presynaptic nerve
> terminals where they provide energy for sustained neurotransmitter
> release. Therefore, defects in mitochondrial activity can
> have severe consequences for the cell, including energetic failure
> associated with decreased ATP production and apoptosis
> resulting from the release of death factors and impaired calciumbuffering
> capacity. Moreover, alterations in the protein complexes
> of the respiratory chain located in the inner mitochondrial
> membrane lead to electron transport leakage that enables the
> production of ROS, which may overwhelm the capacity of the
> anti-oxidant systems existing in cells to counteract free radical
> damage, with the subsequent oxidative damage produced to
> proteins, DNA, RNA, and lipids. Numerous studies have linked
> mitochondrial dysfunction to neurodegenerative diseases, including
> AD (Ferrer, 2009; Ankarcrona et al., 2010; Burchell et al.,
> 2010). Altered mitochondrial function appears early in time
> in AD, even preceding the characteristic Alzheimer pathology
> in mouse models, and ultimately leads to exhausted neurons
> as a result of the convergence of reduced energy production,
> increased energy demand, and excessive oxidative stress (Ferrer,
> 2009).
> The anti-oxidant properties of cannabis derivatives, notably
> CBD, were demonstrated early in cell cultures exposed to toxic
> glutamate levels (Hampson et al., 1998, 2000). In line with these
> findings, CBD prevented ROS production and lipid peroxidation
> in PC12 neuronal cells exposed to Aβ, as well as reduced
> apoptosis from reduced caspase 3 levels, and in counteracting
> the Aβ-induced increase in intracellular calcium concentration
> (Iuvone et al., 2004). CBD also reduced, in similar conditions,
> the levels of nitrite (NO), a potent oxidant reactive molecule, as
> well as the expression of iNOS, one of the enzymes responsible
> for the synthesis of NO (Esposito et al., 2006b, 2011). Moreover,
> other cannabinoid compounds exhibited anti-oxidant properties
> in animal models of AD. Thus, the selective CB2 receptor agonist
> JWH-133 reduced hydroxynonenal adducts, derived from lipid
> peroxidation, and enhanced the levels of the superoxide dismutases
> SOD1 and SOD2 in the vicinity of plaques in APP/PS1 mice,
> indicating the role of CB2 receptors in reducing oxidative stress
> damage and to promote responses against such damage (Aso et al.,
> 2013). A reduction of free radicals and mitochondrial activity
> was also suggested in a mouse model of tauopathy exposed to
> [www.frontiersin.org](http://www.frontiersin.org) March 2014 | Volume 5 | Article 37 | 5Aso and Ferrer Cannabinoids in Alzheimer
> chronic treatment with the Sativex® mixture of 9-THC and CBD
> (Casarejos et al., 2013).
> An additional topic deserving attention is the putative role
> of cannabinoid receptors in the regulation of neuronal energy
> metabolism. The little information available to date supports the
> direct control of CB1 over neuronal respiration and energy production.
> One study, using anti-CB1 receptor antibodies, revealed
> CB1 receptor protein localization in approximately 30% of neuronal
> mitochondria, which when activated by exogenous or
> endogenous cannabinoids reduces the respiratory chain complex I
> activity and oxygen consumption, likely through cyclic adenosine
> monophosphate (cAMP) and protein kinase A (PKA) signaling
> (Bénard et al., 2012). These findings are in agreement with previous
> observations showing that AEA, 9-THC, and HU-210, all
> of them partial CB1 agonists, significantly decreased oxygen consumption
> and mitochondrial membrane potential (Athanasiou
> et al., 2007). However, these data must be interpreted with caution
> as commercial anti-CB1 receptor antibodies also recognize
> the mitochondrial protein stomatin-like protein 2 and that the formerly
> described effect of WIN 55,212-2 on mitochondrial complex
> III is in fact not detectable in isolated mitochondrial preparations
> (Morozov et al., 2013).

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24634659](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24634659)

### Josh — 2015-01-15T18:37:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-152882>

Cannabinoid and Pregnenolone curiosity....I ask myself why at a quantum level is this so? I imagine it will force me to go many places?? Interesting that Pregnenolone affects the Cannabinoid receptors in the brain???

[http://www.eurekalert.org/pub\_releases/2014-01/ind-mdt010214.php](http://www.eurekalert.org/pub_releases/2014-01/ind-mdt010214.php)

> Two INSERM research teams led by Pier Vincenzo Piazza and Giovanni Marsicano (INSERM Unit 862 "Neurocentre Magendie" in Bordeaux) recently discovered that pregnenolone, a molecule produced by the brain, acts as a natural defence mechanism against the harmful effects of cannabis in animals. Pregnenolone prevents THC, the main active principle in cannabis, from fully activating its brain receptor, the CB1 receptor, that when overstimulated by THC causes the intoxicating effects of cannabis. By identifying this mechanism, the INSERM teams are already developing new approaches for the treatment of cannabis addiction.

### sjoshua — 2015-02-04T19:25:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-154733>

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=148752)
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4209357/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4209357/)

> CB1 receptors in VMN neurons provide a molecular switch adapting the organism to dietary change.

I read this as saying, CB receptor activation assists the system in being metabolically flexible. Considering it is naturally a fall harvest plant, it would make sense to utilize seasonally at the transition into winter in order to assist with switching to a winter-like diet (i.e. 'eat like a shark' - when minimal carb availability is more typical, dependent on location).

So, bearing in mind the current state of the surface of our planet (regarding nnEMF saturation in/around poulated areas and exponentially greater with increases in populus), and the manner in which society has shifted towards perceiving 'endless summer' (unlimited light exposure potential, blue light into the night hours sitting in front of TVs, phones, computers, etc.; moving between indoor, climate controlled environments in a A/C 60Hz field), it would make sense that 'the people'/voters are latching onto cannabis as an useful medical/recreational tool, since in a way it is delivering the 'seasonal change' signals most do not even receive anymore...

> Conversely, under high-fat diet, lack of CB1 in VMN neurons produces leptin resistance, blunts peripheral use of lipid substrates and increases adiposity.

And, it appears to have a regulating effect on leptin when 'eating like a shark'.

\-----

The recommended dose is **500-600mg of CBD acid**. This is probably best by drinking a raw smoothies with a set portion raw cannabis leaves.

However, the CBD to THC ratio also makes a difference in therapeutic outcomes. In general, cannabis contains anywhere from a 2:1 to 1:2 ratio of CBD:THC-acid. Personally, I have not done enough research on the ratio to comment. Perhaps I'll start reading now.  (will need to understand cannibinoid receptors, the NMDA pathways, and possibly the nicotinic receptors)

You're right that heating THC-acid turns it into the psychoactive THC. It takes just **10mg** of THC to get really really high (which is most strains will only get you anywhere from 5-20mg of CBD). Hence the recommendation is to juice the cannabis raw, which does not cause any psychoactive effects, and is extremely therapeutic.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-02-08T23:29:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-155074>

I also want to bring attention to this paper '**Phosphene phenomenon: A new concept**' --[http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.377.2281&rep=rep1&type=pdf](http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.377.2281&rep=rep1&type=pdf)

I have made an excerpt summary here -- [http://qhwiki.com/study:phosphene-phenomenon](http://qhwiki.com/study:phosphene-phenomenon)

This talks about how Reactive Oxygen and Nitrogen Species can stimulate weak biophoton emissions at reaction sites

It then talks briefly about how psychedelic compounds induce formation of these ROS and RNS. We can make an argument that one of the reasons "harmful psychedelics" are harmful is because they flood the body with too great a ROS/RNS signal.

"Good psychedelics" provide for controlled ROS/RNS signalling, which gives rise to both the quantum-mechanical entanglement-signalling that Jack has talked about, but also provides for weak biophoton emission at the site of sick cells. I wouldn't be surprised if we found out that THC-induced ROS gives rise to emission of the "380nm healing wavelength" of light at precisely the right intensity , though I have no proof as of yet.

Tensegrity #14 tackles the 380nm wavelength -- [http://jackkruse.com/tensegrity-8-communication-breakdown-gut/](http://jackkruse.com/tensegrity-8-communication-breakdown-gut/)​

\----

**OPINION on use of CBD, THC, and other ROS-inducing compounds**

These compounds are likely stimulators of ROS, with cascades into the rest of circadian signalling.

There is obvious seasonality to such compounds. eg: cannabis is traditionally a fall harvest herb -- precisely at the time when ROS signalling is most required due to seasonal changes (see this post -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/reverse-electron-flow.12744/#post-154292](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/reverse-electron-flow.12744/#post-154292))

Use of such compounds DOES NOT add energy to the system. They help re-establish signalling between components. Energy-adding behaviours like eating a good epi-paleo diet, grounding, etc.. must be performed in conjunction with these compounds. Though when used in conjunction with optimal health strategies, they act as a great aid to re-establish communication between components of the body.

ROS induction through non-dietary pathways is something that I view is useful in the treatment of almost any disease state whereby there is communication breakdown (almost all disease states).

I think this is the most helpful with gut-mediated disorders like excessive leaky-gut-induced autoimmuny, or with something like a candida infection. This these cases, you do not want to introduce ROS-producing carbohydrates to the diet, which can hurt the disease state even more.

While I did mention seasonality, I think such compounds are useful in all seasons when diseased (for the reasons above, and with the appropriate caveats). They are likely not needed in a healthy individual, whereby oxidative signalling is already under control.

Overdosing on such compounds is basically an ROS overdose, with all the known side effects.

An implication of that paper I shared even suggests that all the psychedelic effects are just a by-product of the huge burst of biophoton activity that you get when you take these compounds.

Eventually, I'd think it's possible to be able to shoot bio-photons directly to the appropriate tissues using technologies like targeted low-intensity lasers. Whether or not these will have "psychedelic effects" likely depends on the technological implementation. ie: whether or not you can target say just the prefrontal cortex, without affecting any of the visual or auditory pathways that lead to hallucinations.

The natural method of using such psychoactive substances will continue to be relevant of course, and are at your disposal today (not in Australia where they are illegal as fuck )

> One thing I'm still not understanding about seasonality is that we can re-create our environment rather easily. For instance, after I move, I'm going to get into perma-culturing, regular ol' gardening, aqua-culturing, growing mushrooms, perhaps spirulina, and more. So, I can eat those all year round in that respect, and thus seasonality for me would be drastically different than one who is in the same location as myself, but not growing their own foods. This would be further augmented if I had a chicken coop or something.

"Seasonality" here broadly means any external factors that change over the natural course of a revolution of the earth around our current Sun and the rotation of the Earth on its axis, which end up affecting our circadian biology.

So far, we lack practical manipulation over stuff like magnetic and electrostatic (gravitational) variations across the seasons. Both are stronger during the winter time, and affect our circadian biology massively as I laid out in the 'reverse electron flow' post -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/reverse-electron-flow.12744/#post-154292](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/reverse-electron-flow.12744/#post-154292)

We could possibly re-create those environmental factors eventually, even without understanding the direct impact on biology, so long as we can directly measure and mimic exactly the conditions that earth would provide. (This is a pre-requisite for safe space travel)

We definitely have not even begun to study how exactly our body measures these forces using the various SQUIDs in the body (which work at different scales, at the mitochondrial level, at the extracellular level in AQP4 gates, etc ....)

It's a decent assumption that evolution and natural forces have been yoked pretty tightly, which means that plants such as cannabis are ready for harvest under a particular set of conditions, brought about by natural seasonal changes. We cannot claim to understand why this occurs yet, though we can get one perspective from qualitative measures of ROS signalling (which we cannot quantify )

### Inger — 2015-02-09T08:43:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-155109>

bio-fractal-soul-self,
how are you going to cultivate your veggies etc.? I think as long as you do not use a green house it will always be somehow seasonal. In Finland many uses greenhouses in their garden to be able to grow more stuff and longer.... but I would never use that. I think it is stupid. The plants need real sunlight to be real healthy. That way they will be more seasonal too. I have read it makes quite a difference if a plant gets real light or filtered through glass/fake light.....

I am a fan of wild foods... I think it is way more practical to pick and eat wild plants, berries and mushrooms and eat, than have to do lots of work with gardening
Somehow the wild stuff makes me feel amazing too. I love the whole procedure of searching for it... eating it... being in the wild..... I prefer that over gardening anytime... I sometimes think, why do people pick away the weeds from their gardens.... all those healthy wild edibles.... full of nutrients... that are maybe way healthier than the carrot they grow? So much work. I never understood this. Then comes a deer.. and eats the veggies.. all the hard work was for nothing. or other issues..... too dry.. too wet.. you cant control the weather. But the wild stuff is so hard core it always survives. Here are wild greens that survive under the snow! Amazing! Must be full of life force

That said... I do love to munch on some home grown cucumbers or dirty carrots....  I just would always grow them naturally to get natural lights and temps, no greenhouses

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-02-10T06:12:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-155181>

Well, I will firstly have a garden, as I have some minor experience gardening and feel like picking up a few new skills as it is; it would be great for CT, grounding, light, active meditation, etc. as well. I know things are different in different parts of the world, but in the Midwest people often plant carrots in the winter, often in sand, and they continue to grow quite easily. There are also berries, edible acorns, grasses and shrubs, and much more, in the dead of winter, just under the fresh snow. If I chose to eat these foods alone as sources of carbs, it'd be easy to get around 70 grams of carbs each day, even in the coldest days of winter. The coastal inuit tribes did very similar - they never ate under 50 grams of carbs, and often ate 70+ in addition to hundreds of grams of protein everyday - not even close to a ketogenic diet. Technically speaking, dairy isn't so hard to find in winter, either (I'm speaking of local, grass-fed/raw dairy). If I ate a more 'Optimal Diet' like Peter at HyperLipid, who prefers saturated fats especially from dairy, well, I'd easily be able to get 70-100+ carbs a day in the winter if consuming full fat raw milk, fermented cream and yogurt, matsoni, etc. There is strong evidence that plant foods were readily available during the ice ages in winter as well, such as tiger nuts, and various shrubs and _fatty_ plants. So, I'm not necessarily worried about eating certain foods that someone does or doesn't think is appropriate for winter, so much as I'm concerned with optimal beta oxidation, mitophagy, and so on, especially during winter. CT and a high fat diet alone can accomplish this; DHA, AA, and tons of saturated fat, iodine, selenium, and sulfur from eggs, sardines, etc. alongside some PQQ and resistance training can enhance these adaptations via transcription factors such as PGC-1a and increased activity of some PPAR's.

Further, it's very easy to grow herbs, spices, and other plants indoors. Oregano, ginger, avocados, and more, can be easily nurtured inside even in the winter. Spirulina and mushrooms seem rather easy to grow, too, all year round. This isn't even including things such as aqua-culturing and utilizing the work of Viktor Schauberger and others to drastically increase plant growth sans additional food (using water and minerals alone), which can completely alter what is "normal" in any given environment. There is a show called Bizarre Foods with Andrew Zimmern, where he travels the world eating, well, "bizarre" foods. Interestingly, they're often just traditional/indigenous dishes. Recently I saw his episode of the Farroe Islands in Europe where things like fermented sheeps head, blood pancakes and blood pudding are frequent dishes. What else did they eat? Massive amounts of raw parsnips, potatoes, and winter fruits, in the dead of winter; and these weren't imported foods... they were naturally occurring for people in small towns in the hills with almost no access to the main cities nearby. So, seasonal foods = relative to what you are looking at, and also, what you aren't looking at.

This isn't to decline the validity of your approach or ideas - someone as youthful as yourself, well, I can't argue with those results, and I definitely think fat burning pretty much all year round is optimal, and in Winter, I see no reason to not be in pretty deep ketosis (30 or less carbs, 100 or less grams of protein, maybe 50-70 if dealing with neurological/psychiatric issues); but in the event that my various experiments work out (with food production), there is no way I'd decline some fresh berries, shrooms, home grown avocados, etc. just because there is some trace evidence that in some remote location some time in history those foods weren't available. **Oh, also, I plan to mineralize all my soil with sea veggies and the shells of shellfish**

>
> One more question for those thinking outside the box on this
>
> @[Josh](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/8791/) @[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/) @[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/)
>
> How would COMT, MTRR and other dopamine/serotonin snps impact the THC/CBD response, as well as Pregnenolone levels?

As usual, I am not 100% certain on the mechanisms of CBD oil

Regarding migraines, I personally subscribe to this mechanism -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...-csf-semiconductors-in-brain.7564/#post-89605](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/migraines-a-deficiency-in-current-of-dha-csf-semiconductors-in-brain.7564/#post-89605)

So basically, I think migraines are an upset in the water balance within the brain, and depending on which part is upset, you'll get more water into some parts of the brain more so than the others. Malfunction of the water balance system (AQP4 gates) is likely the root cause, so the next question is why do AQP4 gates fail?

A quick review is recommended of some of the notes I made on the May 2014 Webinar -- [http://tanyewwei.com/notes/jk-webinar-may-2014](http://tanyewwei.com/notes/jk-webinar-may-2014)

Some extra technical notes on AQP4 function -- [http://tanyewwei.com/notes/grotthuss-aquaporin-4-conductance](http://tanyewwei.com/notes/grotthuss-aquaporin-4-conductance) . Note really needed for this discussion though​

When it comes to CBD oil and migraines, we've got to look at the CB1 receptor and its effects on the Central Nervous System ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cannabinoid\_receptor\_type\_1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cannabinoid_receptor_type_1)). Activating this receptor does a lot of downstream ACTIVATION of the neurotransmitters in the brain. Both CBD and THC will activate this receptor pretty strongly.

Where I think the COMT SNPs come into play, is that COMT is needed for DE-ACTIVATION of several neuronal pathways. So having a COMT defect basically means that you cannot de-activate overly-excited pathways very well. This is purely based of theoretical speculation, but if that holds true, then CBD/THC dosing in people with such defects will have to be much lower; You'll still get the benefits of CBD/THC, but the required dose will be lower than in people with these defects.

With migraines, I still the best treatment is going to be to restore AQP4 function, aka: re-zip collagen. Red light to the brain seems like the best solution. Intra-nasal red light therapy, or possibly red-light LLLT is probably the best bet. (In the operating room, doctors would pump saline solution into the brain to get the same effect )

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=159043)
>
> Another follow up. how does cycloset, when coupled with DHA and Sunlight upon rising, bind to the same receptor sites? or is it just the stimulation of Dompamine that affects the SCN.

IMO yeah, it's just the stimulation of the D2 receptor, which happens to mimic a lot of the downstream effects of Leptin. Both the D1 and D2 receptors will have good downstream effects on NPY and CRH (this guy is mentioned heavily in CT4 and CT6 -- "bypass the thyroid and make CRH directly").

Leptin affects both the D1 and D2 receptors, while Cycloset will only affect D2, and actually reduce activation of D1 slightly (though not enough to reduce the overall effect).

So I basically view it as a boost to the Leptin Reset protocol.

....

There may be persistent intermediate effects along the detoxification/biotransformation pathways that have their own effects as well, otherwise known as a "hangover". etc...

Consider some of the factors involved in reliable H+ tunneling that effect enzyme function/pharmacokintics by factors of up to 10 to the 25th power.

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-03-21T08:46:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-159096>

As I alluded to before, one also has to be careful with what "CBD oil" they're getting. The 'legal' stuff made from hemp is crap and doesn't work, and there is in fact a large group on facebook that has been calling out differen companies selling "CBD oil" for quite some time. Not only is there often barely any CBD in the oil, it is typically filled with potentially allergenic impurities. That alone can cause migraines, body aches, etc.

It seems interesting that most people on various forums get NO beneficial effects - and sometimes adverse effects - from CBD oil, yet, when people go and see a MD who then prescribes them medical marijuana and they get medical grade CBD made from cannabis, not hemp, they respond positively.
'

@[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/)

I think one of the key things to addressing the AQP4 is the proper Pg:E2 ratio. You can be rocking everything perfectly, etc but if at any point in the hormonal cycle across both the 24-hour, monthly, and seasonal that ratio is off, the gates don't work.

Usually for me that's two points in my cycle - day 5/6 or day 26. these are the days I usually have issues with migraines (thought not every time)

This cycle - I got hit with a killer migraine on cd3. not normal. I think the geomagnetic storm induced the migraine.

I also think that based upon what Jack has commented about women having a more sensitive epigenetic antenna - that its possible that if we lived in a world with just native EMF, the balance issue at AQP4 is required to inform egg release as well as influence fertilization and pregnancy. If the balance is off, AQP4 might actually result in the inability to sustain a pregnancy given environmental factors.

Yet when you live in a world flooded with nnEMF and you have balance issues at AQP4 - that epigenetic antenna sets you up for failure as system faster. the only signals might actually be the migraine, or the inability to get pregnant or carry a child to term w/o medical intervention.

As for the COMT I've never thought about its inability to turn off the neural pathways. but that would make total sense. Especially given someone who has PTSD. I've always looked at COMT from my perspective recently of not generating enough Dopamine and supporting the Liver detox pathways of P450. but it makes sense. Especially given my COMT status as +/- . I should be able to make enough dopamine, and turn off the hyper-activation of the neural pathways, but I get stuck. courtesy of the good old bipolar issues. when the switch flips, its hard for me to turn it off... when the switch is off its hard for me to turn it on... I would assume that something similar is happening in individuals with PTSD/TBI as well. @[Josh](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/8791/) & @[QuantumRadical](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/165/) alert.

much more to ponder here.... I really need to get signed up for my anatomy and Physiology and biochem class.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-29T01:30:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-159690>

Posting here some of the stuff that I posted in this QH Facebook Group thread: [https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/978212452191649/](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/978212452191649/)

\----
(**excerpts commence**)
\----

Rubin linked to this paper: [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9472423](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9472423)

Important to note that the study involved psychoactive THC, introduced into the body after it has been heated. Non-heated THC is non-psychoactive, which probably means it doesn't excite particular neuronal pathways.

\----

Whether or not CBD or TCH this is useful for something like PTSD will IMO depend heavily on the mechanism being used when taking something like medical marijuana.

My opinion is to do it raw (never heated above 40C)

PTSD is basically over-excitation of the brain's emotional centres. I personally don't think exciting it further with psychoactive THC is helpful

Non-psychoactive CBD in combination with Non-psychoactive (non-heated) THC, could potentially be a good way to re-establish cellular signalling. Binding to the cannabinoid receptors also help with the downstream dopamine pathways, which ultimately feedback to Leptin function (improving it).

\----

IMO cannabis works by producing Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) in the appropriate cells to re-establish cellular signalling, while decreasing ROS production in other cells (see papers below). This would normalise the ROS response to "tune the system to work together".

Chemical reduction of ROS induces biophoton emission at the site of reaction, which transmits information both at this site of reaction, and at the original ROS generation site (due to quantum entanglement)

\- Control over Macrophage ROS production -- [http://cardiovascres.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2009/07/31/cvr.cvp240.short](http://cardiovascres.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2009/07/31/cvr.cvp240.short)

\- Reduction of Oxidative Load on rat PC12 neural cells -- [http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1471-4159.2003.02327.x/full](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1471-4159.2003.02327.x/full)

\- Killing malfunctioning cells with ROS over-production -- [http://molpharm.aspetjournals.org/content/70/3/897.short](http://molpharm.aspetjournals.org/content/70/3/897.short)

IMO cannabis was designed by Nature as a plant to aid with this ROS signalling during the Autumn transition period, when light levels are decreasing, and it isn't cold enough for matter to be condensed.

The body will need to find more ways of generating signalling molecules, and cannabis is one of those ways.

Overview -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/reverse-electron-flow.12744/#post-154292](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads%2Freverse-electron-flow.12744%2F#post-154292)

\----

^^ Implication of the previous section is that I do not think cannabis is useful for people who already have good cellular signalling, other than to have a good time  (which I fully support)

It is designed as a stress mitigation strategy, and can be useful acutely for that purpose (again, I believe raw is best), or chronically in sick populations until health is restored.

Don't forget that the stressor still needs to be removed  Using cannabis as a band-aid is a losing strategy.

....

@[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/) @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) Do you know what mechanisms could trigger spasmophily/tetany seizure when someone smoke or eat weed? (very cold hands and anxiety/hypertension/tachycardia/palpitations)?

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-05T10:41:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannibinoid-curiosity.12247/post-163336>

> [Arborescence said:](/goto/post?id=163277)
>

Cannabinoid receptors will excite certain neuronal channels. I'm sure this can cause misfiring of certain circuits.

# Thread: Experiences with Fish Oil
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/experiences-with-fish-oil.12251/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2014-11-29T21:17:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/experiences-with-fish-oil.12251/post-148780>

So I've been taking Green Pastures fermented Cod Liver Oil and I'd like to know I'm not just wasting my money. I don't have much..working at Walgreens 10.90 an hour after 3 raises and I've been working 3 years so about 1 a year.

It feels like I've been spending tons on supplements...and I'm not even sure of their efficacy.

[http://www.walgreens.com/store/c/fi...ry-supplement-softgels/ID=prod6108946-product](http://www.walgreens.com/store/c/finest-nutrition-fish-oil-pearls-dietary-supplement-softgels/ID=prod6108946-product)

14 cents a serving for these easy to swallow pills that use Wild Caught fish.

[http://www.greenpasture.org/public/Products/CodLiverOil/index.cfm](http://www.greenpasture.org/public/Products/CodLiverOil/index.cfm)

37 cents for the liquid and 50 cents for the capsules.

The price per serving amounts aren't that much different but it adds up in money, and I'm wondering if it really makes a big difference. Has anyone had any experience trying different kinds and actually noticing differences either in lab results or even just how you feel?

Part of me wants to take the Green pasture capsules because its easier, but its still supposed to be high quality fermented cod liver oil which I've heard so many good things about. Are they just as good as the liquid though...they basically are just putting the liquid in a capsule right? The ingredients in the capsules don't sound bad.

Also how much better is it if at all than the Fish Oil pearls...they are supposedly also wild caught fish so is it any different. I don't need the pearls for their size I have no problem swallowing large pills, but the cost difference though seemingly small when you look at the per serving cost when you look at the cost as a whole its quite a big difference.

9.99 and thats not even taking into account that its buy one get one free so its actually 4 cents a serving. That doesn't actually matter if its not absorbed and used as well, but if it is than I'm just paying for nothing getting the Green Pastures.

Of course there is also the issue of product quality..how much nutrient capacity..how it varies between products and I would think there was a higher amount in the more expensive one, but I have no clue on that..

If anyone has any experience taking these different types etc..or any advice please let me know or any advice on supplements in general. I don't currently do the shopping for my house and I currently have several different supplements I've bought to fill the gaps I may have in nutrition.

I'll post all my supplements in a bit.

Thanks

### CTforlife — 2014-11-29T22:15:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/experiences-with-fish-oil.12251/post-148788>

Food is your supplement. Not a pill! Food is your supplement, rewiring this into your brain will take time, but we have an awful lot of that here ! Don't ever look at a multivitamin and think that's how you get nutrients vitamins etc.... Food is the supplement package , and WE all know supplements are additions , not the source.
Look at the definition of a supplement I'm being quite literal throughout this post .

You will begin to see why I call food a supplement, because it helps, but it's not the end all be all. (Read emf 2!)

Right now you're thinking about what you can add to food, that's wrong .
Think about what food you can supplement to your new lifestyle to improve your n=1 game. (Becoming optimal)

I'm talking about emf 2, food is the supplement not the answer. Meaning it helps the new lifestyle you are seeking. See why I said and why I said it?

You are trying to supplement your food.

No!!! Use food to supplement your lifestyle!!!!!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/experiences-with-fish-oil.12251/post-149870>

I took green pastures for some time and results were mixed and it hurt the wallet. I also have tried many other supplements to fight a lifestyle that I was paying a heavy metabolic price for. I spent a lot.... a lot of money on supplements over the years, thousands! In october I did an experiment where I ate about 400 oysters over the course of a month. I felt better than I had on **any** supps I had tried. Now you may say, that is a lot but I proved a point to myself, I can't outsmart mother nature. These foods already have DHA, iodine, magnesium, niacin and other cofactors.

The food gets the DHA in the right places because of it being in the SN2 position. Be smart about it, eat oysters, buy whole fish and save the bones and make broths and eat some of the organ meats. Ask your local fishmonger for some fish heads, sprinkle some paprika in there go to town.

Do some CT to optimize uptake and avoid blue light to avoid breakdown. Think about everytime you cheat you are tossing money down drain. You have gotta look at your resources and innovate...

### Kristi Lambert — 2014-12-04T01:57:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-149153>

[Well, here we are at Day 1 of the LeptinRx. I made my video for the LCHF Youtubers and here it is: http://youtu.be/bqCpklQRfOI. Here are my stats for the week:](http://youtu.be/bqCpklQRfOI)

FBG 126
Evening BG 113
Morning Ketones 1.2
Weight 237.8
Released this week -8.4
Total released 23.6
Avg release 1.82
Hip/waist/bust 55/49/52.5

I was really happy to see my evening BG. I will get in a Colpak on my upper back tonight and start face dunking tomorrow. Gonna look into compression garments asap. I bought one of those gizmos for the tub listed in Dr K's store to keep the tub from draining. I'd really like to get a stock tub and put it out front (will see if I can have one for Christmas).

My food for the day was:

Breakfast (7:15 AM EST):
3 5 oz cans of pink salmon with a splash of MCT and some mayo
decaf coffee with 2 tb coconut milk (just the cream part from the top)

Lunch (11 AM EST):
2 heads romaine with mct/mustard/ACV dressing
2 TB Bubbie's sauerkraut

Dinner (4:15 PM EST):
2 c mushrooms in coconut oil
1 5 oz can pink salmon with splash of MCT oil
decaf coffee with 2 tb coconut milk (just the cream part from the top)

I woke up to pee at 5 AM then went back to bed until 6:18 AM. Kept sleep mask on except for when I went to pee (put blublockers on for that). When I woke up at 6:18 AM I put my breakfast together and took care of Mom's meds and Mom and hubby's breakfasts. I kept my blublockers on until official sunrise (7:06 AM I think). Then I ate my breakfast by 7:15 AM and walked across the street and did about 5 minutes of sungazing. Did my evening sungazing through sliding glass door today but will go out on the porch going forward as I do not know if the glass door has any coatings.

I am not sure if my morning routine is correct. Tried to stay in bed as long as I could without impacting getting my breakfast into my pie hole before the end of the golden hour (as defined in the iPad app called SOL I installed that shows it as being that first hour or so after official sunrise):

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I am wondering if it is workable for me to get out of bed about 40-45 minutes before official sunrise and eat breakfast starting right after sunrise, finishing before the end of the golden hour.

I will list my supplements later. I am hoping that my dry flaking skin will improve after having read Dr K's comments on other people's questions about it (regarding Vitamin D and cricadian mismatch). Anyway, will add the youtube video after I get Mom to go to sleep later.

Ate breakfast at official sunrise at 7:07 AM and did sun gazing for about 5 minutes at 7:31 AM.

FBG: 126
Evening 8:27 PM BG: 108
Ketones: Did not measure - will use Ketonix tomorrow - blood test strips once a week

decaf coffee with 2 tb coconut milk (just the cream part from the top)

Lunch (11 AM EST):

I am very excited about it!

### Kristi Lambert — 2014-12-10T22:59:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-149751>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=149748)
>
> Good morning Kristi .....just on my way to Sydney for some Christmas shopping ...I will be back!
>
> please re read the leptin reset again ...don't over think things! sometimes we miss things the first or second time thru - I know I do ....or I didn't quite understand the importance of something.
>
> It is all about the three legged stool ......
>
> my initial thought is you are getting a lot of protein ......
>
> Are you hungry for lunch still? How much good fat are you having?

Thanks Caroline! Ooh sounds fun, I love to see all the decorations in the city this time of year. I like to watch the young girls take ice skating lessons at the mall to get me into the Christmas spirit.

I went back through the leptin RX, brain gut 6, and the leptin faq and from what I am gathering from three combined at this point is:

-   **Does breakfast need to be just protein, or can there be vegetables or fruit? What about fat?** If you are overweight, avoid fruit. _Veggies are fine as long as they are not nightshades and have a low glycemic index_. If you are underweight, you can eat fruit, and I suggest blueberrries, blackberries, and grapes over all others. Fat is great. The obese can use [coconut oil](http://store.nutiva.com/aff/7563933154C0D4013687DE48F5051500/index.html) as their “number one diesel.” If you don’t like coconut oil or Nutiva coconut manna, you can use heavy cream, but you won’t lose weight as quickly.
-   Try to eat as soon as possible upon rising in the morning, ideally within 30 minutes of waking. _Make sure that breakfast has little to no carbs (less than 50 grams), and has a lot of protein and fat. I use as a general rule 50-75 grams of protein with most patients. Some patients can use less and some need more. The key point of knowing how much is right for you is your hunger later in the day. If you remain ravenous throughout the day, you need to eat more protein in the morning. If you can hold off eating until dinner you probably are at homeostasis for you. If you can skip both meals you likely are overdoing it at breakfast. As for sources, I suggest pastured or organic eggs first, served with left over dinner scraps of grass fed meats, poultry, or fish. A third option, although less ideal, would be whey protein or protein shakes._

-   _Any time I eat carbs_, I use liberal amounts of butter, heavy cream, [coconut or palm oil](http://store.nutiva.com/aff/7563933154C0D4013687DE48F5051500/index.html). I do not recommend other oils initially such as olive oils or industrial seed oils. I would also avoid nut oils at the initial stages. My personal favorite is [coconut oil](http://store.nutiva.com/aff/7563933154C0D4013687DE48F5051500/index.html) because of the great metabolic effects of MCT, and how it helps heal the guts of LR folks.

From these points it sounds like I can optimize my reset by moving what I eat for lunch to breakfast and adjust the protein by first making it 50-75g using eggs (mmm a spinach/mushroom/bacon frittata sounds great to me) as the first priority for breakfast so that lunch will not be needed. I am eating at least 6 TB of coconut and mct oil and 2-4 TB coconut manna daily, but need to move those to breakfast and dinner from what my feeble brain is gathering. I am thinking that will continue to hit the bone broth with arame and kelp in it, probably with dinner.

Cheers

Breakfast (7:15 AM EST):
3 5 oz cans of pink salmon with a splash of coconut oil
1.5 heads romaine with mct/mustard/ACV dressing
blonde starbucks coffee with 2 tb coconut manna
2 c mushrooms in coconut oil

Dinner (4:15 PM EST):
Applegate Farms salami (32g protein)
2 TB Bubbie's sauerkraut
decaf coffee with 2 tb coconut oil
1.5 heads romaine with mct/mustard/ACV dressing

Tried to sungaze after dinner but the sun had sunk too low behind the trees (hopefully I got some rays through the trees). But I was delighted because two Sandhill cranes decided to put on quite a show for me dancing around, jumping and throwing twigs and leaves up in the air!

I hope everyone had a great day!

### louise — 2014-12-12T17:34:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-149907>

hello Kristi, you must be happy with the FBG.... it has dropped considerably
I have never had my BG tested as im lazy and haven't been to docs for over 15yrs but when I started this stuff I knew I had problems cos I was always carb hungry and would feel shaky after 3hours of not eating----I can easily go all day now and at last the carb cravings are gone
you went 9 hours today without food so its working....fabulous.

Good evening y'all!

Missed my face dunking and Colpak today, but went to the Crystal River Archaeological State Park and sat on a rock by the river's edge in my bare feet and no jacket in 48 degree weather, and drove with the windows down. Woke up at about 6 AM, got my sungazing in both in the morning and evening. Picked up 10 more yellow bug lights from Home Depot.

FBG: 96
Evening BG: 91

Breakfast (7:15 AM EST):
3 5 oz cans of pink salmon with a splash of coconut oil
1.5 heads romaine with mct/mustard/ACV dressing
blonde starbucks coffee with 2 tb coconut manna
2 c mushrooms in coconut oil

Dinner (4:15 PM EST):
1 5 oz can pink salmon
2 TB Bubbie's sauerkraut
decaf coffee with 2 tb coconut oil
1 heads romaine with mct/mustard/ACV dressing

Spent the afternoon working on changing my network around so that I can turn off wireless at night and monitor Mom via a wired ethernet camera. Got all the pieces for changing my ipad and iphone over to wired ethernet, but found that I will need a genuine original usb-to-ethernet adapted for each of them to make it work. Ordered them from Apple. I did get my laptops onto ethernet and turned off their wireless. So, some progress made.

Have a good one ;-)

\-----------------------------------------------

Evening BG 8:20 PM: 96

### Kristi Lambert — 2014-12-27T04:12:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-151033>

Hi lovely peeps!

Well yesterday was not optimal reset-wise. I ate my BAB 15 minutes before sunrise, got no CT in and ate Christmas dinner a little after sunset. Got outside for some sun though, went to the wildlife park with family and enjoyed it immensely! I ate my usual BAB. I overindulged on prime rib and had a small serving of green peas for the Christmas meal (I mostly overindulged on the fat around the outside though, and wow did it taste great!). Had a thin slice of turkey.

Today I was back on track (almost - gotta face dunk tomorrow):

FBG: 114
Evening BG: 104
Ketones: 2.9 (cool!)

3 5 oz cans salmon
decaf coffee with coconut manna and coconut oil
1 can smoked oysters

Dinner:
1 5 oz can salmon
1 head romaine with mct/acv/mustard dressing
decaf coffee with coconut manna and coconut oil

Did my sungazing after breakfast. Did my Colpak tonight. Had a 3 hour nap this afternoon after family left.

I hope you all had a lovely two days! Cheers

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-03T18:06:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-151471>

Howdy lovely peeps!

Here is my update for Friday 1/3/2015:

Still waking a little too early, but sticking to having breakfast at sunrise so that I only eat during daylight. Went to Ft Island Beach (froze my face with AC on the way there for about 30 minutes) and got into that 59.5 degree water for 15 minutes up to my belly button. Wanted to go deeper but things were getting mucky on the bottom and I feared I would lose my balance. I used the IR thermometer to check my skin temperature when I got out and it was 61 degrees. I should have stayed in longer because it actually felt great, but I couldn't wait to check my skin temp, lol! The air was cold and windy and the sun was barely peaking through so I got some good grounding and sungazing in while I sat on the beach for a while. Very peaceful, practically had the place to myself.

FBG: 90

Breakfast:
3 5 oz cans salmon
decaf coffee with coconut oil
1 can smoked oysters

Dinner:
1 5 oz can salmon
1 head romaine with mct/acv/mustard dressing
decaf coffee with coconut oil
2 c mushrooms with 2 tb butter
two forkfuls of Bubbie's sauerkraut plus two swigs of the brine

I am happy to see my FBG come back down. The only visible change is that I stopped the vanilla extract. Will keep working on the sleep. Will post today's entry later tonight. I think I will start setting up my portable tub. Hoping I'll be ready to do it soon, need to do the ice bags this week.

Cheers

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-05T12:58:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-151676>

Maybe this will help, I found it on a forum:

**[brassandzin](https://discussions.apple.com/people/brassandzin)**Jan 29, 2013 11:28 AM
[Re: Can't play YouTube videos on Safariin response to darkhorse85](https://discussions.apple.com/message/18674880#18674880)
I had the same issues!! Here's the fix! Found on the Gooogle/YouTube discussion boards.
The issue is caused by YouTube's HTML5 test not working in Safari.
To fix this go the the [HTML5 test page](http://www.youtube.com/html5), scroll down to the bottom, and click the "Leave test" link.
If this doesn't do it or you're getting this issue in another browser try clearing your cookies and restarting your browser. That usually solves it.

Just a shot in the dark...

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-07T01:47:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-151908>

Had my first session with bagged ice today. Got 2 10 lb bags and started with lying on the floor, but my back gave out so I moved to a sort of reclined porch chair and held the bag in a tall kitchen bag against me for the rest of the time. It was 56 degrees outside which helped. I lasted an hour - makes me so happy! I checked my skin temp with the IR thermometer often. I got some areas to 49 degrees and some to 52 degrees. I have set my new portable tub where I think it will work for me in anticipation of using it perhaps tomorrow. I am in the meantime searching for a "chiller" that I can afford (unless I get really lucky on an aquarium chiiler - it's a very small portable tub). Been looking at peltier, chill plate, immersion wort chillers, counterflow wort chillers, ice probe chillers, etc. trying to see if I might be able to cool my outdoor tap water enough throughout the year. Aaah, but lots of time to figure that out - will search for the cheapest ice bags around here ("Wally World" charges $2 per 10 pound bag). Got my epsom salts, MSM powder and H2O2 ready to go.

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-09T05:02:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-152191>

Howdy y'all! Well, it was 32 degrees with a bitter wind so I did my CT in the bathtub today. Got a 20 lb bag of ice from Publix for about $3.70 and reclined in the tub with my swim cap, dive socks, and compression gear. Started off with the ice on the compression shirt, then decided it was time to do the next step, so I exposed all my belly and iced my belly directly. I believe the whole session was 30 minutes. Got skin temp down to 49 degrees. Interesting, got a couple purple faint bruises. I read in another thread that Dr K mentions perhaps too much omega 3, which actually thrills me since I have been pounding the fish. I have a pork roast warming for tomorrow so I can rebalance O3/O6 a bit, as well as get more cysteine. Looks like that direct icing test was a success because I saw no cold urticaria, so I am thinking I shall move onto the next step with water plus ice with socks, cap and gloves.

On another note, I think I figured out why the weight loss has been lower these past two weeks, or at least a contributing factor - silly me, I added the oysters and forgot to check how many grams of protein I was effectively adding to my BAB, so I have been eating a whopping total of 82 grams total for BAB. So I will drop the salmon down to 2 cans (40 grams) with the 22 grams from the oysters and see if that improves things.

Oh and thanks to Yew's makerel food porn post  I checked out Publix and found makerel in water for $1.99 for a 15 oz can, so I will be shifting to that (the salmon is $1.49 for a 5 oz can).

Just ordered the faraday disposable tent from lessemf.com to try to lessen the smartmeter effect at night.

I hope this has been a blessed day for all of you lovelies

Thanks so much Jude, I really appreciate your feedback   I find your posts very inspiring and educational

Well, I bought one on amazon and it turned out to be too loose, but I found a cheap spandex generic garment (ClimateRight is the brand - [http://www.walmart.com/search/searc...l0=e&wl1=g&wl2=c&wl3=45038760815&wl4=&veh=sem](http://www.walmart.com/search/search-ng.do?search_query=climate+right&adid=22222222220214754401&wmlspartner=wmtlabs&wl0=e&wl1=g&wl2=c&wl3=45038760815&wl4=&veh=sem) ) at walmart and I got it a couple sizes too small. It's kind of stretchy but seems to hold tight enough and is easy to get on. When I can afford it I plan on getting an Under Armour shirt or a Spanx. Thanks to Caroline's suggestion I pretty much wear it all day long now.

I will absolutely update you on the tent. I am hoping it will buy me some more sleep time

Cheers, Jude

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-12T18:56:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-152559>

Just a quick update on CT. Found myself a nice little spot for CT on Fridays where I can ground and I can kneel to get waist deep and sit to get all the way in. Friday the air temp was 42 degrees and the water as probably in the 60s (hubby's guess) - I forgot the thermometer.

Today I finally used my new portable tub with epsom salts. I did not get enough ice in so the temps was 68 degrees. Got my skin to about 69 degrees after 45 minutes. It was a last minute decision to try it. This was the first time I have put myself in the water up to my neck. Wore my compression shirt and shorts and dive socks. Having the treadmill to hang onto made getting in and out quite easy. This is exciting. I know 68 degrees is wimpy but I am happy to make any progress I can LOL! I may have to keep buying ice bags until I can clear enough room in the freezer for ice jugs and packs.

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-16T00:58:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-152921>

Aaaah, much better. Air temp was 40 degrees and water was 56 so I borrowed Mom's living room lamp and used the heat lamp bulb so I could keep hands, nose and head warm as needed. My feet are still freakin' cold even with compression socks, dive socks, and althletic socks layered, but it was okay because I was able to warm my hands when I got too cold. I added my knit cap over my swim cap too. Mom's gloves did the trick for the hands. This cold weather right now is good since I only had to add one bucket of ice to get to 56F (waiting on the immersion chiller). I was happy that the lamp did not heat up the tub at all. I made it 40 minutes this way. Still wimpy, but some day I hope to grow up to be a badass CTer like you lovely peeps!

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-18T01:11:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-153200>

Had a good two days of CT. Yesterday I lasted 60 minutes in 56 degree water and today I lasted 50 minutes in 53 degree water. The air temps have been around 40 degrees so the heat lamp got me through since I could warm my nose and hands when needed.

Added two raw eggs to my fatty coffee along with the coconut oil plus coconut milk, nutmeg, and Ceylon cinnamon.

My FBG was 90 this morning - ICE IS NICE! LOL!!!

I was just watching this youtube channel - addicted2cold.com is their site. Saw this video about inner fire meditation (tummo) and now I am seeing many related videos - very cool (pun intended)

All you badass CTers, I have the utmost admiration and respect!

Blessings

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-20T21:00:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-153450>

Howdy, lovely peeps!

Here is my weekly update:

FBG: 104
Ketones: 2.1
Weight: 225.8
Release for week: -0.2
Total released 20 weeks keto: 35.6
Keto weekly avg: 1.78
Hips/waist/bust (deltas): 54/47/51 (.5/1/0)
Total released 7 weeks Leptin Reset: 12
Leptin Reset weekly avg: 1.71

Even though I went up .2 lbs I lost an inch in my waist and .5 inches in my hips, so I am thinking the CT is doing it's thing to my body composition!

Drinking arame water - thanks, Inger!
Adding kelp granules to my fish, along with turmeric and pepper
Using makerel 15 oz can for BAB - yum! skin and bones taste great
Will have bacon/pork, to get my cysteine, and oysters for supper
Been egg yolks raw to my optimal coffee - will eliminate this week and see if I might have been reacting

CT is coming along well. I am still too wimpy to go without socks, gloves and hat. Air temps have been low enough that I can just tolerate things with the IR lamp close by. Lesson learned on the socks, layering and using materials other than wool made my cold feet almost intolerable, but today I used just a single layer of a wool blend sock and that made things alot better. My water temps have gone from 59 down to 52 today. Once my wort chiller arrives I am hoping to get it between 45-50F. I am warming up a bit faster now by wearing a pair of wool socks after I dry off.

Cheers, all!

The shape and touch says more.

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-26T14:05:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-153890>

> [Spanishjeff said:](/goto/post?id=153880)
>
> Hey Kristi,
> your looking really composed there doing your CT!Going to try the arame water too,so thanks for the tip.
>
> Mmmh mackarel,yes beats salmon for me too!
>
> I'd love to know how you felt when you started ct in your tank and how the sensations change,for example the first time I tried on friday it was really intense and left me exhausted and weak feeling,yet the second time yesterday was much better.
>
> Keep at it Kristi!!

Hi, Jeff! I am so glad you like the arame water (thanks so much for that, Inger!)

I love the way they can the mackerel so that I can break it up myself, and the skin is yummy!

LOL, I am not so composed when I first get in, but I found that having the treadmill to hang onto helps, so that I can stand, kneel, then sit, which I do within the first minute.

I found the CT very intense and exhausting too. I am fine with my torso but still find the shoulders, upper back, and arms a little intense. The lower body was easier once I realized I should only use wool for my socks in one layer. Now my feet last the whole hour. I am finding, as Dr Kruse describes, that water closer to 50 degrees is actually much more tolerable than water closer to 60 degrees. In fact 59 degree water on Saturday was a little miserable. Yesterday 49 degrees was much better. As others have described, I found that some days I take forever to warm up afterward - weird, some days I warm up fairly fast. I usually need a nap within a couple of hours - I guess I still have loads of tired mitochondria

You are doing so awesome! Thanks again for your inspiration and encouragement

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-26T14:13:34+0200

LOL, thanks, Jude! I think that was mid morning. Thanks for the beanie feedback, as I have always been so self conscious about my "humungoid melon" size 7 3/4 inch head in hats

I have been doing this out on the back porch, which I know is a no-no for the first month, but I have been using the IR lamp to control the air temperature on my upper body. The air outside yesterday was around 40 degrees F and it was fine. I am actually glad because it's keeping the tub cold. It will become challenging once spring and summer arrive

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-27T18:27:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154004>

Thank you, Jeff  That means a great deal to me  Well, my skin temperature is not yet where I would like it but's getting closer now. The last two days the water started at 49 degrees according to my rubber ducky thermometer. I only made it to 45 minutes these last two days. By the time I finish rubby ducky says it's 56 degrees. My skin temperature under the compression garments as been 59 degrees, and bare skin 57 degrees. So today after kneeling I pulled my compression shirt up to my armpits and my shorts down to my hips so I could expose my torso. At least now my torso is getting closer to the 55 degree goal for apoptosis. I wonder if the heat lamp is causing the water to get to 56 degrees or just my body heating the water. I keep the lamp pointed so that it's not down toward the water. I couldn't make it outside in that weather without having the heat lamp to warm my head and hands though, and hopefully I am getting some fournier effect benefits as well. I have such a long way to go that I won't rush things. I am finding that by bedtime I am a furnace and want a fan on me, so maybe I will pack my swimcap with cold packs and get some fournier effect in the other direction at night and see how that works.

Thanks so much for the wet boots idea! I have neoprene dive socks but the water goes right into them and then my feet feel even icier so I quit wearing them. Now with the wool socks I keep my feet folded under me and that helps me last longer.

You are inspiring me as well! Yes, this biohacking is very intriguing and fun  Blessings!

### nicld — 2015-01-27T19:01:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154011>

Your own body heat will increase the water temp as well as the lamp. I have an overhead heater that I use and my the time I am done my water temp goes up about 3-4 degrees.

You are doing great, keep it up

### Spanishjeff — 2015-01-28T12:42:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154090>

Perhaps put some cool blocks in to help maintain the water temp Kristi or some ice,My water temp doesn't really change and perhaps try without the clothing,My skin thermometer got lost in the post so I don't know my own temps but I do know the water temp and the closer you can keep it to ideal the more consistent your skin temp should be.
Are your neoprene boots a good fit?They need to be snug as they work by trapping a layer of water then your body temp warms it up,if they are a poor fit this won't happen,but if you are fine with wool socks,no problem.

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-28T21:20:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154124>

Howdy, lovelies! Here is my weekly update:

FBG: 91
Ketones: 2.1
Weight: 227.8
Release for week: -2
Total released 21 weeks keto: 33.6
Keto weekly avg: 1.6
Hips/waist/bust (deltas): 54/47/51(0/0/0)
Total released 8 weeks Leptin Reset: 10
Leptin Reset weekly avg: 1.25

Here is the food I had this past week (I am soooooo boring, same shtuff each day):

about 21g carbs 3.2%
about 100g protein 20.4%
about 174g fat 76.4%
about 2000 calories

Breakfast:
1 15oz can makerel
decaf coffee nutmeg/cinnamon/coconut oil

Dinner:
oyster stew with:
one can oysters
1/3c coconut milk
1/2 shallot
2TB kerigold butter
1/4c water
2c mushrooms sauteed in bacon grease topped with 2TB butter
3 slices bacon
decaf coffee nutmeg/cinnamon/coconut oil
forkful of sauerkraut
seaweed water

45-60 minutes CT water temp 49-53 start 56 end, added epsom salts, MSM powder and H2O2

lugols 1/2 dropper
magnesium malate

stopped lugols today

Will try cutting out all carbs this week (am thinking that with CT my body says, hey it's winter, no carbs, please) and see if that kicks things up for me.

Blessings to all

### kovita — 2015-01-28T21:21:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154125>

Kristi, I am no CT hero, no chances i can even remotely compare with many on this forum. I do CT because i enjoy it and I discovered i enjoy it most in the nature. There are so many wonderfull places where you can just get into the water and breath the magic of the surrounding beauty. I actually find it much more energizing than CT in my bathtube. I often sit naked on the shore of the lake or river and feel a bit longer wonderfull... For my partocular personal health story "the feelings" are proving to be as important as DHA. The health and the beauty comes hand in hand with the harmony and happiness. It all works better for me if i am finding joy and passion in what I do, I threw the thermometer and stopwatch away some time back and go by feelings. I think it is being important part of my "selfesteem and selfeconfidence rx". ;-)

### fitness@home — 2015-01-29T03:17:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154170>

Kristi, I think you should track more measurements. I've been tracking mine since the early 90s. Back then I used a pen and paper...these days a spreadsheet. These are my measuring points:

R upper arm
L upper arm
Under the arms
Bust
Ribcage just under breasts
Natural waist
Fullest part of abdomen
Hips
R upper thigh
L upper thigh
R knee pudge  (right above knee cap)
L knee pudge
R calf
L calf

I add all the measurements together for a total # of inches. Periodically (semi annually) I will remeasure and compare with the previous results.

You could be losing more inches than you think...

P.s. I once lost 16 inches and had zero weight loss. My body shrank and that was good enough for me

### SeaHorse — 2015-01-29T14:14:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154190>

> [Kristi Lambert said:](/goto/post?id=154123)
>
> Thanks, Nicole, I will try that today, the wool is too cold for me too, I am actually reielved when I pull them off before stepping out of the tub

Hi Kristi….I've tried lots of foot wear for CT because I can't CT in the winter with my feet out. I wear FELTED wool socks or booties….the felting process makes a lot of difference to how wool holds your heat. I've used handmade and store-bought loose thick wool and then I just agitate them in the washer until they shrink and felt up…..they stiffen a bit and loose their stretch, but become much warmer to wear….
I've use close fitting neoprene booties over my socks sometimes, or put on another layer of wool as in felt slippers or bigger socks. I know neoprene blocks the good energy exchange with the bottom of the pond, but I usually am sitting on the bottom, up to my neck, so my legs get to have contact with the pond bottom. This contact is important in outdoor CT, but inside it wouldn't make that much difference. This year is the first year I've had no signs at all of Reynauds….every year it has gotten better, but my adaptation is in the slow lane…..You are doing great and I enjoy reading and listening to your posts….all the best
