### Kristi Lambert — 2015-01-29T22:07:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154221>

CT totally kicks A\*\*!!! My blood sugar was 78 last night, which is a major milestone for me - woot woot!

Woo, started with a 46 degree water temp this morning since I haven't had a chance to clear out my freezer for ice block space yet (so my tub warms up about 6 degrees as I CT), which was a bit harsh when I got in, but I was able to last 45 minutes before my feet said GET OUT, and I finally got my exposed skin temp to 51 degrees and skin under my shirt to 56 degrees, while still being able to enjoy the back yard and nature...lovin' it!

Thanks again to all of you lovely peeps for all of your help and encouragement on this wild adventure

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-02-01T02:11:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154407>

Thanks so much for all the suggestions about my cold feet, y'all! These did the trick:

[https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B000V9QRYI/ref=oh\_aui\_detailpage\_o04\_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1](https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B000V9QRYI/ref=oh_aui_detailpage_o04_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1)

I stayed in for over an hour today and only got out because the wind was getting too bitter. Some water gets in when I first get in but the booties are thick and the water inside stays alot warmer. The water temp started at 49 degrees and ended at 54 degrees. Leg skin temp was 54 and skin was 56 degrees under my shirt. I will also test the booties with wool socks inside them to see if I can get a tighter seal.

Coooooool!

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-02-03T00:50:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154562>

I should have done this when I started my journal, but here is what I have for context off the top of my head:

14 hour breach birth (not feet first but rather with twisted head)
chiropractor said xray indicated my C2 has been subluxated likely since birth
C6 is also bad
three smokers in family
ear infections, strep, tonsillectomy by 5 years old
found out at 14 that I was colorblind - cannot pass color dots test
several falls from horses as a teen
obese at 6 years old
started menses at 10 years old
long history of yo-yo dieting and disordered eating
diabetes around 2001
gyn treated massive buildup on uterine lining with prometrium 2001
gyn treated ovarian cysts with birth control pills for a month in 2008
periods were always embarassingly heavy
last period I have had was first month into keto - none since
over a decade of shift work in computer field
was working graveyard shift while caretaking for brother with cancer during day
took my own bloodwork in 2010 - BUN/Creatinine ratio was 8 and had high eosiniphils
took my own bloodwork in 2012 - BUN/Creatinine ratio was 10 and high TG
with Vitamin D of 11 and low phosphorus
have done many rounds of HCG, so feel vindicated by Dr Kruse's findings on HCG
missing the outer third of eyebrows and had thinning hair (hair has stopped falling out now)
always had difficulty reading - would get ghosting especially when reading from paper
very slow reader
bounced back and forth between raw food, Dr Fuhrman, Sally Fallon, Dr McDougall for a
few years
got two root canals on left side molars in 2007
have one amalgam filling
got certified with Dr Robert Morse International School of Detox in 2012
started doing his herbal supplements in 2011, stopped in late 2012

I will add more here as I think of it...

Updated 2/2/2015:

In 2011 Dr Morse did an iridology for me. I have radii solaris which shows toxins travelling past the gut to the rest of the organs (super leaky gut - I do have diverticulosis). He was very concerned by the cholesterol cloud on the outer half of my irises, the concern being that I may have a stroke. I still have that cloud and hope that as my vessels become more hydrophillic that I will see the cloud dissipate. Also have scurf rings relating to my skin issues.

Antennasearch shows the following:

### Sue-UK — 2015-02-03T13:02:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154597>

Interesting iridology pics Kristi  I got DH to take some iridology pics for me last July using a digital camera, when the time is right I'll get some fresh ones taken and compare, will probably leave it til July this year. Radii Solaris are interesting, I remember doing a course years ago and we had to check each others irises, I was the only one who had radii Solaris (brain area)  . When I went for the second course 6 months later there was no sign of them, but I'd done dry skin brushing, herbs etc in the meantime. But in the longer term I realised that my radii Solaris (or probably more accurately the body condition the sign reflected) weren't caused by lack of dry skin brushing, herbs etc .... I got caught up in trying to get rid of the sign, and then keeping the sign at bay, instead of asking _why_ ....... it took a few more years to realise the limitations of standard naturopathics and for the constant _yes but why_...... to begin ...

I've come to think of Iridology as a snapshot of a particular moment in time, that I can compare to another snapshot in time later (a bit like labs) but in the context of other observable data (FBG, weight/inch loss, better energy etc)..and the overall trend .... IIRC some iridology signs take longer to change or go, even if the related condition in the body has improved.

With changes in NN EMF over the last 20 years, (and its effects on signalling), maybe iridology is less useful as a working tool. I'm going to compare 2 sets of photos taken a year apart, no standard naturopathics, no supplements, just 3 legged stool and epi paleo

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-02-04T19:12:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-154730>

Howdy, lovelies, here is my weekly update:

Thanks, fitness@home[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?members/fitness-home.5474/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?members/fitness-home.5474/)
for the measurements expansion idea!

This week I started with no carbs and lower protein. The lower protein made me too hungry, so I brought that back up, and I added mushrooms back.

Breakfast:

15 oz can mackerel (60g protein) with turmeric, pepper, salt, mct oil
fatty decaf coffee with 3TB coconut oil, nutmeg, pumpkin pie spice, ceylon cinnamon

Dinner:

2c mushrooms with butter, sauteed in bacon grease
4 slices bacon
5 oz can salmon (20g protein) with turmeric, pepper, salt, mct oil
fatty decaf coffee with 3TB coconut oil, nutmeg, pumpkin pie spice, ceylon cinnamon

CT has been good, water temps 50-55 degrees this week. Did 60 minutes on all days but one.

Blood sugar has been really good, one morning was 78! The rest in the 80s.

Thanks to all of you for all of your encouragement and support

Namaste' !

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-02-11T14:43:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-155355>

Howdy, lovely peeps! Here is my update for this week:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Did CT every day. Had one day where I had 59 degree F water but the rest of the days I started at between 47 to 59 degrees F and stayed under 54 degres F for the 60 minutes.

Blood sugars in the 80s this week. Feeling better and sleeping better. Only got up to pee one night. Still get up around 4 only to switch to chair from futon but am going back to sleep well.

Food:

Breakfast:

15 oz can mackerel (60g protein) with turmeric, pepper, salt, mct oil
fatty decaf coffee with 3TB coconut oil, nutmeg, pumpkin pie spice, ceylon cinnamon

Dinner:

2c mushrooms with butter, sauteed in bacon grease
4 slices bacon
5 oz can salmon (20g protein) with turmeric, pepper, salt, mct oil
fatty decaf coffee with 3TB coconut oil, nutmeg, pumpkin pie spice, ceylon cinnamon

On two days I also added a fat bomb to lunch:

4TB coconut oil
1 tsp cocoa powder unsweetened
stevia glycerite to taste

On two days for lunch, instead of the mushrooms, bacon, and salmon, I had about 8 ounces of pulled pork.

After reading some of the posts, I decided to try using my Verilux Happylite as soon as I wake. I have been doing it for two days so far. I get up and toss my can of mackerel into a bowl with my turmeric/black pepper/coconut oil and make my fatty coffee, then sit by the light and eat it right away. I just want to see if this will help me on mornings where I have obligations prior to sunrise. I don't expect that I will use it much at all during long light cycles. Once my morning chores are done I still have some golden hour sun so I go out and sungaze and ground.

I also decided to try these glasses at night, while wearing my orange uvex glasses during the day when sitting at the computer:

[http://www.amazon.com/HDE-Protection-Safety-Glasses-Lasers/dp/B009T6RN0G/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1423691102&sr=8-1&keywords=HDE Laser Eye Protection Safety Glasses for Green and Blue Lasers with Case (Red)](http://www.amazon.com/HDE-Protection-Safety-Glasses-Lasers/dp/B009T6RN0G/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1423691102&sr=8-1&keywords=HDE+Laser+Eye+Protection+Safety+Glasses+for+Green+and+Blue+Lasers+with+Case+%28Red%29)

It seems to making me sleep longer. I switched to these because of the posts I read saying that green light also reduces melatonin. These new glasses have more space at the bottom, so I will see how it goes. I have been sleeping in the glasses instead of the mask for fear that light exposure when switching back and forth if I have to get up might disrupt the sleep cycle. Will play around with things and see...

LOL, looking at my laptop using darkroom mode of f.lux and finding it quite pleasant. It is helping me with my reading ability as well

Cheers

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-02-18T19:25:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-155969>

Howdy, lovely peeps!

Here is my update for this week:

It was a great CT week. Kept the water temps between 47 and 54 degrees each session and although I saw no weight loss or measurement changes, I am seeing shrinkage, particularly in thighs and belly. I also tested out my tolerance to commercial food. Had an asiago peppercorn dressing and Hardees mushroom swiss burgers without the bun. Looks like my body was not happy with those things. I am glad I did it just to do n=1 on that.

I have started eating a sauteed onion instead of mushrooms and plan to hit the cysteine rich foods this week.

Oh, and interesting, like someone posted earlier, I am seeing veins on my body that I have never seen before - cool!

I hope everyone is having a great week! Namaste

### Jude — 2015-02-19T01:55:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-156002>

Looking good Kristi
Your CT is impressive and so is your lack of hunger! I know JK says CT kicks hunger out the door and that he eats like a great white and it's heaps? Interesting.

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-02-19T02:24:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-156015>

Wow, thanks, Jude  I am elated by the lack of hunger, JK rocks totally! Finally the answer. I have lost and regained 100s of pounds over my lifetime (and OMG did I gain it back fast), and now I finally understand why - others have judged it as a character issue and now I see hope that this time all I need to do is follow what JK has laid out and fix my circadian mismatches and my environment and I will finally become free of that endless merry-go-round - and finally be able to enjoy food without guilt and shame

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-03-08T22:04:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-157895>

Howdy, lovely peeps!

Well, after much research of the forums, blogs, and external sites, I decided to add the HCG biohack in order to do an n=1 on the Holy Trinity  You are all so brilliant and helpful, I am so very grateful to learn from you! I decided I would do the HCG 2.0 protocol (Misty brought it up) Leptin Rx style, using the HA2CG drops, as described by @[cantweight](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1283/) Christi in her post. As @[nicld](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6689/) Nicole, @[Shijin13](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/969/) Gretchen, @[nonchalant](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/191/) Sally, @[ste colombe](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1374/) and others (please forgive me and let me know if I missed mentioning you - @[fitness@home](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5474/) has done HCG 2.0 successfully) are doing, I am maxing my seafood, doing CT, and keeping Leptin Rx compliance. This is working very well for me so far.

Here are my stats, keeping in mind that there were four extra days in my "week" this week since I was two days into the week when I decided to do this, then spent two days loading. I did not weigh myself prior to loading, nor after loading. I did not want to fall into the trap of micromanaging the weight. In fact, thanks so much @[nicld](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6689/) Nicole for sharing your video with Robin! I watched all her videos this week and it was a sober reminder. It's wild too, I remember her old channel she had when she was collecting all her research data from clients out in Utah for HCG (I think it was Utah). I bought Weight Loss Apocolypse as well.

So, I was very pleased to see an overall release of 2.4 pounds this week. I enjoyed my loading so I imagine I gained at least 6 pounds, and so the HA2CG drops seem to be working well. Hunger has not been bad at all, hardly at all now. Interestingly, I also started menstruating the other day. I did the same thing when I started keto in September, then had no periods after that until now. Seems to be my body's main form of detox, as the same thing happened when I would do juice feasts.

My CT temps have been starting at 46 and ending at 50. I only missed two days of the past 11 days. My typical day looks like this:

Here is what the total calories from the protein sources looks like:

Based on my BMR today my total calories from protein sources should be 658 and I also have 2 to 3 TB of coconut oil in the afternoon. I am eating my lean fish for my BAB so I can get the highest protein grams, then the fattier fish for dinner. I may switch the coconut oil to morning. I also found a link showing that the Chicken of the Sea salmon and white albacore have great DHA levels, so I am sticking with that brand as long as I can find it

I looked through all my old spreadsheets from my HCG rounds since 2006 and remembered that I have been successful using many variations of the HCG protocol, including the rogue stuff like cycling, miniloads, etc, so I am trying this:

My thinking is that with the HA2CG they recommend a minimum round for two weeks, and I figure I can do a continued round with a miniload each two weeks on Sundays, and to prevent fatigue or slowdown, skipping drops on Saturdays. I will see how it goes and adjust based on what my body tells me.

I am sooooooooooooooooooooooooooooo happy with Dr Kruse and the Leptin Reset, I have never felt better, thank you, Dr Kruse!!! I just want to satisfy my curiosity about what effects adding the HCG element to this will have

Namaste, beautiful peeps!

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-03-23T00:52:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-159238>

Hi there, lovely peeps! Here is my weekly update

I am loving the HCG 2.0 protocol and am still feeling great on the HA2CG drops (very little hunger). I am skipping the drops on Saturdays.

I did an experiment with doing a miniload and I gained 7 lbs, but lost 8.4 lbs, for a net loss of 1.4 lbs. I have no regrets, as I really want to find out if certain things work, so I choose not to be attached to the scale results. I enjoyed the miniload and am thinking that I just need to wait longer between reloads and perhaps modify what foods I allow - I may just do one if I see a big slowdown for a particular week. Another experiment I want to try again is to just do planned interruptions like I did in 2010 where I would do 5 days of Leptin Reset as my planned interruption. I did very well with that approach before. I am trying to decide how often to do that. Back in 2007 I was doing HCG through a clinic and they had me do 8 weeks of hcg then 3 weeks of P3 then back into P2 again. I did that over a 14 month period and lost 101 lbs. They had me on 800 calories and had me skip shots twice per week. So, I may try skipping drops twice a week and doing 8 weeks before my breaks. This is so fun! And the weightlossapocolypse videos and book help to keep me from slipping into micromanaging and getting insane. Staying mindful of how this can be a slippery slope, and reminding myself to stay detached from the numbers, to just observe and record and enjoy the experiment. The Leptin Reset combined with the CT and the HA2CG/HCG 2.0 is something very cool!!!

The CT is going very well, still luckily able to keep the skin temps in the 50-55 degree F range and doing an hour.

Namaste

Looking great Kristi

I am you fellow biohacker now...lol I try out the homaopatic HCG - and I am amazed!
I do not weigh... but in one week I have lost 1 cm on my upper arms, 4 cm on my belly and 1 cm on my butt  (and my loading days were like 6000 kcal/day..lol)
and I really do not have much to lose! I am/was at ideal weight...(~54 kg/166 cm) and it is fat gone.. no muscles...

I am amazed at how I have regular BM every morning.. no constipation at all... with that little food

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-31T15:50:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-159934>

re: hair loss, it happens to me when my hormones fluctuate greatly (like after I had my daughter I lost a lot of hair over several months), but also it seems to happen regularly every so often. My hairdresser is at a special needs place (for women who have cancer-related hair thinning and loss, etc, besides specializing in coloring/fixing bad color jobs) and she told me that most people do shed and regrow on a schedule, which varies person to person. For me it seems to be seasonal - every 3 months or so I'll lose handfuls in the shower but you can't see it except aroudn my forehead hairline (which is pretty irregular ever since my daughter was born).

Anyway, for what it's worth, it doesn't have to necessarily mean anything other than it's' just your hair's time to shed.

I love your dedication, by the way, it's really inspiring!

could you get fresh fatty fish?
Or get canned sardines in water? I think these would have way more DHA?
It sure is a no go on the original protocol but I eat very fatty seafood every day and I still loose fat like crazy  and I do not have much to loose.....
Can you pick nettles where you live too? I have added those in almost every day and it feels so good... a great booster on this diet me thinks
I do it also to not loose my hair again (I have read some loose hair on HCG..) that just have grown out so nicely since I lost so much last fall.....  Nettles should help

### Kristi Lambert — 2015-04-14T22:11:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-161199>

The chiller seems to be fairing well on the porch with 88 degree weather. The thermostat says the reservoir is 50 degrees (I have it set with a target of 39 degrees so it's working hard), my thermometer says the tub is still 46-47 degrees next to the output into the tub from the return hose. I am so happy! The tub stayed near 41 degrees late in the morning, so I should be able to CT all summer without messing with ics if I do early morning CT like I have been, and hopefully will only need to add ice from the icemaker if I do it later. I will have to see what the 90+ degree days are like. I have a tower fan blowing the heat output from the chiller out, and a little usb fan sends air from the opposite side to keep the ventillation going. I cover the pvc cold tub with some pipe insulation and made a top for it out of more pipe insulation LOL!

LOL, using a dropcam to monitor things

It is a small tub, I am guessing I fill it with maybe 25 gallons since my body takes up enough space to get it up to my armpits with that small amount. It is a 1/10 HP chiller, so I am really pleased with it, it doesn't vibrate too much and itsn't too loud.

Happy CTing, lovelies

### fitness@home — 2015-04-14T22:22:27+0300

It gets darn hot here too and I would need too much ice to cool things down. I love the our chiller and can't wait to get the stock tank insulated with spray foam so I have a bigger/deeper tub to CT in. We plan to make a cover for it also. If and when it ever gets finished I will post of pciture of it.

In the meantime, I use the chiller in my small/shallow upstairs bath tub... takes very little time to chill it down.

Okay then ....lets forget all the mushy stuff and get down to business!

This is your one and only life - don't piss it away \[please\] ....I understand that you have a lot on your plate - we all do.

There are lots of stories here that would make your hair curl. Stories that break your heart ...but that is life.

I am glad you came back........ but food is no longer your friend. You need to love yourself and care for yourself .... okay??????

from now on - medicate with music, get yourself some drums, sing at the top of your lungs, dance around naked

Just pick yourself up and dust yourself off and move forward.

Our optimal journey isn't always straight forward - there will be bumps, and likely huge heart breaking bumps ...but that should and will make us stronger.

I want to read every single day, five things that you did for yourself .....

no excuses....

[https://attunedvibrations.com/solfeggio/](https://attunedvibrations.com/solfeggio/)

I love the essential oils too. I cautiously only use DoTerra or Young Living or Peppermint Jim brands so far because they are touted to be clear of chemicals in their processing and are listed as therapeutic grade. I have been afraid to try other brands yet. I mix the carrot seed oil with jojoba oil. I use the peppermint oil and rosemary oil a lot. I love this web site as it has a huge database of issues and solutions:
[http://www.oil-testimonials.com/](http://www.oil-testimonials.com/)

### Kristi Lambert — 2016-09-20T13:05:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kristis-journal.12278/post-201650>

Five things:
1) Bought a small mosquito netting enclosure in which I can sit in my front yard or back yard safely (Zika virus concerns) - LEOSO-Portable-Folding-Opening-Mosquito - it has netting on the floor of it so I can properly ground through it while sunning - had to do this because our back porch has a mostly southern exposure so I have to ground outside.

Namaste

### Jack Kruse — 2014-12-05T07:05:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-11-comments-welcome-here.12294/post-149269>

Remember, Volkow and Achermann said that non native EMF (subthermal EMF) up regulates glucose metabolism. This means non native EMF increase NADH at cytochrome one naturally. NADPH comes from NAD+. For lay people, and even many scientists, the scientific discussions of NAD+ are so complicated that they seem to be virtually unfathomable. Why you ask? It is the quantum ledge of a cell. It is where thermodynamics meets the quantum realm just outside of our ability to perceive it. Light and dark are electromagnetic signals.
Circadian light signals are the general topic which are mostly ignored in many of the current publications, such as the high degree of circadian regulation of NAD+. Systemic NAD biosynthesis mediated by intra- and extracellular NAMPT. It functions as a driver that keeps up the pace of metabolism in multiple tissues/organs, and the NAD-dependent deacetylase SIRT 1 serves as a universal mediator that executes metabolic effects in a tissue-dependent manner in response to changes in systemic NAD biosynthesis. In sickness and aging NAD+ drops sharply negatively impacting on DNA repair and mitochondrial health and benefits conveyed by sirtuins. This action uncouples the thermodynamic realm from the quantum one in mitochondria. If you have no NAD+ you can entangle and tunnel........no matter what. Remember superoxide is a free radical that is our entangler..........

Cytochrome 1 uses NAD+ and NADH to create a small pulse of superoxide normally. Superoxide is a ROS. NADPH (EMF 4) provides the reducing equivalents for biosynthetic reactions and the [oxidation-reduction](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxidation-reduction) involved in protecting against the toxicity of ROS. When you fuel your diet with glucose, you deplete cytochrome 1 of NAD+ normally. When mitochondrial NAD+ levels are low, this information about a “low energy state” is sent to the cytoplasm where mitochondrial DNA is and to the nucleus where the somatic genes are housed. Calcium is the cation that signals this event. **Nicotinic acid adenine dinucleotide phosphate** (NAADP) is one of the most potent stimulators of intracellular Ca2+ release known to date. It is the new player in bio hacking nitrogen on the periodic table. Ca2+ signaling triggers a larger than expected elevation of reactive oxygen species in mitochondria, leading to the translocation of NF-κB and STAT-3 into the nucleus. Ca2+ is a big deal.........as you are all finding out. NAADP-regulated system appears to have a significant role in intracellular Ca2+ signaling. The NAADP receptor and its associated Ca2+ pool appear to be yoked and linked to important in several physiological processes including fertilization, T cell activation, and pancreatic secretion. This is how the door of leptin resistance leads to infertility, autoimmunity and diabetes.................

One last nugget...........did you know that NAD+ and NADH strongly absorb [ultraviolet](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ultraviolet) light? Did you know NAD+ is conserved across all three kingdoms of life? The peak absorption of NAD+ is at a [wavelength](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wavelength) of 259 [nanometers](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nanometer) (nm), but NADH also absorbs at higher wavelengths, with a second peak in UV absorption at 339 nm. If you get a sense of where I am headed............you might want to remember this.

### Da-mo — 2014-12-05T09:49:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-11-comments-welcome-here.12294/post-149274>

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Some background info on UV that might be applicable (bolded) even if the source has something to sell. . .

> 1\. Ultraviolet C (UVC) - 100 - 290 nm - UVC wavelengths are the shortest ultraviolet rays, extending from 100nm to 290 nm, and are the most carcinogenic. **While the sun generates ultraviolet C, the atmospheric ozone layer screens out virtually all UVC from reaching us. However, Ultraviolet C may become increasingly problematic for those living at high altitudes. If the depletion of the ozone layer through pollution continues, the consequences will be life-threatening on a large-scale.** UVC is severely photo damaging to the skin, resulting in skin burn with exposure. Artificial sources, such as some mercury arc-welding units, and germicidal lamps emit ultraviolet C. These wavelengths can very efficiently kill germs, giving rise to their common name, 'germicidal waves'.
> 2\. Ultraviolet B (UVB) - 290 - 320 nm - The current SPF rating system addresses only this specific wavelength. UVB is the intermediate wavelength of Ultraviolet rays, and causes the initial appearance of redness, commonly called 'sunburn.' UVB creates painful irritation, but is believed by many to be less damaging than UVA (320nm-400nm), which causes the pigmentation changes associated with tanning. UVB primarily damages the outer most layer of the skin, the epidermis. The result is skin redness and thickening of the the stratum corneum, (our body's attempt to reduce UVB impact on the epidermis). Excessive exposure to UVB is the foremost promoter of premature aging of the skin. This type of damage is cumulative, potentially resulting in basal cell and squamous cell cancers.
> 3\. Ultraviolet A (UVA) - 320 - 400 nm - The longer UVA wavelengths were once thought of as essentially harmless, contributing only to a 'healthy tan.' Scientific evidence now indicates that this is not true. UVA induces cutaneous photo damage, usually seen as dryness, uneven pigmentation, inflammation, skin darkening (tanning), and fine wrinkles along with skin cancer. Since even a low dose of UVA can penetrate to the underlying dermis, resulting photodamage will cause wrinkles and sagging skin. Furthermore, UVA adversely affects the deep dermis far more than the superficial 'sunburn' caused by UVB rays, resulting in a loss of the elastic quality of its supportive collagen, causing premature aging. **Unlike the shorter UVB (290-320 nm) wavelengths, UVA easily penetrates window glass.
> Interestingly, the amount of UVA reaching the earth, unlike UVB, retains essentially the same energy level every day of the year, morning, noon, and afternoon.** Deeply penetrating UVA radiation presents **the same** damaging effect to the skin in **mid-December at 9 am as it does in mid-July at 4 pm**. Therefore, those of us with sensitivities to light, whether the cause is genetic, disease related (such as Lupus and Rosacea), drug related (as with certain antibiotics and diuretics) or related to photodynamic therapy (PDT), need year-round, everyday, morning-to-night protection from all forms of light.
> **It is estimated that 10 to 12 times more UVA than UVB reaches the earth's surface at sea level.** UVA protection is not numerically addressed although you may see UVA and broad-spectrum protection on a package. Remember that broad spectrum is not total spectrum protection. The SPF rating system does not predict the ability of sunscreens to block UVA wavelengths.
> The most important aspect of UVA is the cumulative tissue damage that results from these deeply penetrating UV rays. Studies to date support the relationship of such UV exposure to the development of basal and squamous cell cancers, as well as pre-cancerous lesions. **Recently, it has been reported that depletion of Vitamin A in the skin by UVA exposure** may contribute to both photo aging and cancers of the skin.

h[ttp://www.mededge-inc.com/fallene.htm](http://www.mededge-inc.com/fallene.htm)

\-----

My response:

I very briefly skimmed this paper 'The transmembrane glycoprotein CD38 is a catalytically active transporter responsible for generation and influx of the second messenger cyclic ADP-ribose across membranes' -- [http://www.fasebj.org/content/12/14/1507.long](http://www.fasebj.org/content/12/14/1507.long)

I have no clue what 90% of that paper is saying ... but going to spew some thoughts anyway.

CD38 seems to be able to make Cyclic ADP-ribose (cADPR) from extracellular NAD+ in an exothermic reaction that requires no activation energy. In this reaction, the N-glycosidic linkage between nicotinamide and ribose in the NAD+ molecule is cleaved. This releases "cADPR-responsive calcium stores"

I somehow thought of this crazy sequence (completely pulled out of my ass):

\- N-glycosidic bond cleavage of NAD generates a nicotinamide radical

\- Entanglement of the now-cleaved nicotinamide with released calcium ion results (there was mention in the Dan Stickler podcast of Nitrogen radicals entangling to positive carrier molecules, but did not specify what those molecules were)

\- There must be some form of related calcium intake mechanism, triggered by some other feedback mechanism. I'm wondering if calcium action at its active site (eg: neuronal voltage-gated calcium channel) causes electron spin changes in the entangled nicotinamide somehow to get NAADP, which is a potent Ca2+ uptake stimulator (reducing extracellular calcium) --[http://en.wikipedia.org/.../Nicotinic\_acid\_adenine...](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicotinic_acid_adenine_dinucleotide_phosphate)

(I think it possible to get NAADP from Nicotinamide and NADP --[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1829193/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1829193/))

\- This mechanism would malfunction to cause excess Ca2+ release (and excess immune activation) if NAADP action is inhibited somehow. CD38 seems to be pH sensitive, so the proton concentration of the surrounding water will affect it's function.

I need to look into mold to see how these mechanics play out, I have a hunch it has to do with extracellular water ...

### Jack Kruse — 2014-12-06T21:22:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-11-comments-welcome-here.12294/post-149372>

Extended wakefulness of modern society affect the redox state of locus ceruleus cells in the brain. This is a nucleus neurosurgeons pay lots of attn too.

Bad sleep cause massive oxidation and a loss of reduction = low NAD+ levels in the mitochondria. Low NAD+ leads to the inability to make the cytochrome proteins in mitochondria. This destroys tunneling and entanglement in REM. So consider the following: The Locus ceruleus nerves are among the most metabolically active neurons that fire at increased rates across sustained wakefulness. Blue light really french fries these cells causing them to lose DHA in their cell membranes. If this is sustained chronically, mitochondria swell, release less IR light to water micelles, and this lack of heat release allows mitochondrial swelling and a loss of the adaptive mitochondrial metabolic responses. Redox injury results screwing sleep, learning, and longevity. The nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide-dependent deacetylase sirtuin type 3 (SirT3 is a mice SIRT and this stuff is studied in rodents more than humans who use SIRT 1 and 6) seems to coordinate how electrons and protons are handled by mitochondria along with the redox homeostasis. Normal circadian signaling of daily light oxidation during wakefulness upregulates SirT3 and antioxidants in these critical neurons. Darkness is how nature protects metabolic homeostasis. Where the mice crap interests me is that in data where mice are lacking SirT3 by experimental design, these mice lose the adaptive antioxidant response and get big time oxidative injury in the locus ceruleus across brief light times and wakefulness.

I think this has massive implications for humans in cities. I just think the STACS are different (SIRT 1 and 6) but I would imagine the game plan is the same. Mice are nocturnal and we are not so this is why I hate mice food or sleep studies as you all know. It is also why obesity researchers are clueless in my opinion, because they base most of what they believe on mice who use different 'Sirts' to regulate circadian cycles. This is a big clue that night time and daytime are clock regulated differently fundamentally. The more light becomes the key environmental driver the worse ECT does. First, cytochrome 1 is taken out and we can't make superoxide pulse to signal at all. This is what T2D is , but as time goes on it demolishes all the cytochromes because mtDNA is adjacent to cytochrome 1 and this mechanism destroys those 13 genes that make the cytochromes proteins and it bleeds into the quantum processes that recycle life during REM sleep. When it is broken neolithic diseases manifest. This is when we see other more complex diseases like OSA, PD, ALS, AD etc...........We need researchers looking at SIRT 1 and 6 in humans to tease this stuff out.

We also should remember that mitochondria are subject to evolutionary selection pressures due to altered circadian signaling within the brain and body clocks mentioned above. This simple fact is why most researchers' **_have been surprised_** that mitochondrial defects do not accumulate at high rates in tissues instead defective mitochondria are removed by the mitochondrial recycling programs that respond only to size and shape changed mediated by IR light and the the ability of water to constrict and condense water as it is heated.

Quoted (bolded parts are my emphasis):

Neutrinos only act gravitationally. There is no such thing as "the weak force." The EU folks talk about imaginary things in physics, well, they need to stop referring to "the weak force." If you know anything about this "force" it is not measured in newtons. In fact, it is dimensionless. It is just a number. I show it is actually a ratio. But it certainly is not a force.

**Dark matter is matter that does not interact with the electric and magnetic forces. The neutrino fits the bill. It is a perfect example of dark matter.**
As for the other imaginary "particles" of dark matter, we agree, they do not exist. Only the neutrinos exist. However, the neutrinos measured in labs are moving at high velocity and interacting with neutrons. **It is quite likely that there is a great reservoir of neutrinos with low velocity, which sit in pools near regions of dense matter**. Regardless of what they are called, the astrophysical calculations based upon dark matter may have substance.

Also, **the Casimir effect demonstrates that angular momentum, and hence mass, can be generated from the Aether.** That is, something is being converted into real visible matter. That something has mass and did not previously interact with the electric and magnetic forces. It is reasonable to conjecture that dark matter converts to visible matter in the Casimir effect. I propose that a similar process occurs in fusion reactions.

I know this flies in the face of many in the EU groups, but there is good evidence to support the existence of dark matter and I can quantify the conversion of dark matter to visible matter.
​

Essentially the entire microtubule becomes a straight DC wire that generates an electric monopole, right where there are tons of protons, and where the simultaneous attraction and repelling forces of the Casimir effect can "split protons" (more likely for beta decay to occur).

### Christos — 2014-12-07T02:23:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-11-comments-welcome-here.12294/post-149384>

Was looking into the PPP and its tie in to NAD+ production, the only pathway I can see is from the glycerol 3-phosphate shuttle which oxidizes NADH to NAD+. On the topic of SIRTS, i found this abstract:

Abstract:
The most common enzyme defect in humans is glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency, which affects more than 400 million people. G6PD shunts glucose into the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP) to generate nucleotides and reducing potential in the form of NADPH. In this issue, Wang _et al_ ([2014](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.15252/embj.201488713/full#embj201488713-bib-0007)) show that G6PD activity is post-translationally regulated by SIRT2, a cytoplasmic NAD+-dependent deacetylase, thereby linking NAD+ levels to DNA repair and oxidative defences, and identifying potential new approaches to treating this common genetic disease.

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.15252/embj.201488713/abstract](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.15252/embj.201488713/abstract)

As such NAD+ is needed to activate SIRT2 thus activating the PPP. Besides complex 1 oxidation of NADH to NAD+ and the G3P shuttle how else is NAD+ produced?

Hmm, now I need to think about G3P again. Two of Dr Kruse's posts come to mind ....

\----

'**PPP and Glycogen**' thread -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/ppp-and-glycogen.9798/#post-111421](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/ppp-and-glycogen.9798/#post-111421)

The key is understanding the G3Pdh shuttle in out mitochondria is a major redox switch

If you look in EE12 I posted a pic of the redox potentials from NADH to O2. Study it. It is critical. You guys all think life is based upon pathways in books that do not use the redox potential in that pic.

[http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-12-battery-charged/](http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-12-battery-charged/)

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

It appears that glycerol-3-Pdh is critcal protectant against electron and proton overload from any source in mitochondria. It protects us by inducing insulin resistance.

When you have high blood sugars married with high exogenous insulin or fructose consumption from a bad diet to induce physiologic insulin resistance y**ou need to have mitochondrial G3Pdh for effective reverse electron flow through complex I** to generate mandatory superoxide/H2O2 for mitochondrial signaling.

The interesting point is that mitochondrial G3Pdh inputs electrons to the CoEnQ couples in cytochromes for electron tunneling which has a redox potential of +20mV

So with respect to mitochondrial G3Pdh, it is taking a cytoplasmic NADH, which could theoretically be shuttled to the mitochondrial matrix, and hence to complex I, at -280mV, and inputting it to the ETC at +20mV.

The energy lost by skipping from -280mV to +20mV would normally pump **four protons** to generate heat.

After a glucose load any oxidation of the abnormally elevated FFAs of insulin resistant people still provides a continuous +20mV input using ETF dehydrogenase's FADH2 acting on the CoQ couple, which is ultimately derived from the FADH2 of the first step of beta oxidation, ie it's FADH2 transporting electrons all the way, there is no energy wastage when using fats to limit insulin's action.

In the absence of these inappropriate FFAs, the correct way to reduce the CoQ couple is using mtG3Pdh which shuts down insulin's action at the cost of generating heat because it uses NADH, stepped down to FADH2, as a direct input at the CoQ level.

We want a reduced the CoQ couple when there is metabolic oversupply as **a reduced CoQ couple allows reverse electron flow through complex I and insulin resistance. Insulin resistance, via reverse electron flow through complex I, is what is wanted, heat is a by-product.**

What if that elevated FFA level was maintained long term, say if you are eating a ketogenic template? As adipocytes become progressively more resistant to the the anti-lipolytic effect of insulin (another aspect not material now), plasma free fatty acids rise even under levels of insulin which should be suppressing them.

Unless these free fatty acids are converted to CoA derivatives they will DIRECTLY uncouple respiration themselves. This should reduce delta psi while increase metabolic rate

A reduced delta psi will not support reverse electron flow through complex I!!!!!! Here is where a lowered redox hurts. The essential insulin induced pulse of superoxide that is converted to H2O2, will not occur. Mitochondrial signaling is then BROKEN. There will be fasting insulin resistance. [This paper](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23730255) spelled it out.[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23730255](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23730255)

​

(\*\*\* Sidenote: How is this all linked to the action of superoxide dismutaste? \*\*\*)

Equation for Complex One activity: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NADH\_dehydrogenase\_(ubiquinone)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NADH_dehydrogenase_\(ubiquinone\))

NADH + H+ + CoQ + 4H+in → NAD+ + CoQH2 + 4H+out​

Reversing it (as is the case in insulin resistance) produces extra protons and consumes both NAD+ and Ubiquinol (CoQH2), implying that electrons are consumed in the reaction.

The reverse reaction seems to take place only when the membrane potential of the inner mitochondrial membrane is high (lots of electrons), and it feels to me as a way to specifically get rid of excess high powered electrons. Maybe that's why NAD+ absorbs strongly in the UV range ...

\----

'**Low Carb/Insulin Resistance**' thread -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/low-carb-insulin-resistance.8934/page-6#post-102730](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/low-carb-insulin-resistance.8934/page-6#post-102730)

This glycerol 3 phosphate molecule has a special shuttle. This is a metabolic pathway controlled by transition metal maxwell demons.

It allows electrons to skip cytochrome 1.
Using it allows cytoplasmic NADH to enter the electron transport chain.

They happen to have two special glycerol 3 phosphate dehydrogenases which make up the shuttle.

-   Free in the cytosol there is cytosolic G3P dehydrogenase, which actually uses NADH to add a pair of hydrogens to a glycolysis intermediate (dihydroxyacetone phosphate) to form G3P directly.
-   The other G3P dehydrogenase really does dehydrogenate G3P, back to dihydroxyacetone phosphate.

**This second G3P dehydrogenase is embedded in the outer surface of the inner mitochondrial membrane. KEY POINT!!!**

Think what I told you special in the quantum electron blog 2 years ago about FADH2? And it contains an FAD/FADH2 moiety which takes these two hydrogens and uses them to reduce the CoQ couple, feeding electrons in to the electron transport chain.

FADH2 is our special fat pathway quantum tunneler......it allows glucose to act like saturated fat when the shuttle is active in cold environments or during starvation.

When the shuttle is working we pump electrons from cytosolic NADH directly in to the mitochondrial respiratory chain through the FADH2 tunneler. When you do this............it is electron only required and no pumping of protons. Anybody see WHY yet?​

Nope, I don't see why yet . But I assume that you don't pump protons because you don't need to pump protons.

Random quote from wiki article on FADH2 ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flavin\_adenine\_dinucleotide](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide)):

The primary biochemical role of FADH2 in [eukaryotes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eukaryotes) is to carry high-energy electrons used for [oxidative phosphorylation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxidative_phosphorylation). Its hydrogens remain in the mitochondrial matrix, whilst FAD is tightly bound to a dehydrogenase enzyme
​

Does this mean that proton concentration in the mitochondrial matrix does not change as a result?  Fe-S clusters play into this as well, being the main electron carries for this FADH2 tunnel .... Again, I don't understand the science here enough, and more reading is required.

(Sidenote: FADH2 is yet another adenine containing compound. See my quick note on adenine below)

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-07T11:47:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-11-comments-welcome-here.12294/post-149410>

Most info I see cites adenine as the responsible agent for the UV absorption peak of both NAD+ and NADH.

However, none of the sources seem to say how absorption of UV light affects the shape of NAD+, and the resulting affects when bound to oxidoreductases.

Oxidoreductases themselves are big structures of multiple parallel beta sheets ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beta\_sheet](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beta_sheet)), whereby sheets are linked by hydrogen bonds, and each sheet has alpha helices at the ends ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha\_helix](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha_helix)).

My childish brain wants to think of these beta sheets as tightly packed ladders of hydrogen atoms just asking to be bounced around by all sorts of forces.

Sidenote: I remember Jack mentioning Total Adenine nucleotides (TAN) before, and how they should rise through the day and be highest right before sleep, which I assume will recycle these during autophagic processes. Caffeine's proposed mechanism of action is by inhibiting adenosine receptors and keeps total adenine nucleotide levels down​

NAD+ has a very unique shape:

I would think it is very polar thanks to all the phosphate bound oxygen atoms (and a hanging electron on 2 oxygen atoms), but the 3D structure looks strangely symmetrical.

Shape will be important when trying to answer the question of how exactly NAD+ activates other pathways like the sirtuin based pathways and the G3P shuttle. Why is this shape, which is relatively flat, and with nitrogens hanging at the edges and oxygen sitting closer to the center, and important configuration for cellular signalling?

### Da-mo — 2014-12-08T11:06:41+0200

Found the following. Dont know if it will help you since I dont understand what you are discussing but it seems applicable somehow.

> In oxidized NAD+ and NADP+, the non-bonding pair of electrons of the nicotinamide ring nitrogen are free to participate in the resonance of the ring. In reduced NADH and NADPH, that pair of electrons binds a proton, depriving the ring of two of the six electrons needed for resonance. The length of electron density in the resonant nicotinamide ring is of the order of the circumference of the nicotinamide ring. When nicotinamide is reduced, electron density is sequestered into two independent pi bonds, drastically reducing the length of the resonator and consequently drastically increasing its resonant frequency.

I laughed when you linked this video because it is coming down the pike in a few blogs................But to understand resonant connection you have to understand where that DC current comes from...........Mitochondria can be made into monopoles in cells during certain environmental signals.

Moessner and his colleagues use an astonishingly simple model to explain magnetic monopoles as a collective phenomenon in spin ice. The starting point of the model are the magnetic moments at the corners of the tetrahedra of which spin ice is composed. Moessner compares each magnetic moment to a dumbbell, with a north pole at one end and a south pole at the other. Two north and two south poles thus meet in the middle of each tetrahedron. The opposing magnetic charges of the poles in this configuration cancel.

 [Zoom Image](http://www.mpg.de/770982/zoom.jpg)
When one bar magnet in achain (a) flips, two monopoles (b and c) are created. In the spin ice model, such chains are ... [\[more\]](http://www.mpg.de/810957/Magnetic_Monopoles_as_Collective_Phenomenon#)
© Tchernyshov, O. Nature 451, 22-23 (2007)
If one of the dumbbells is now turned by 180 degrees, an imbalance arises in the middle of two adjacent tetrahedra. Three poles with the same polarity meet one with the opposite polarity: in one tetrahedron, three north poles face a single south pole, and in the adjacent tetrahedron, three south poles a north pole. So overall, there are surplus north poles in one tetrahedron, and surplus south poles in the other. This disturbs the magnetic neutrality at a local level, and thus requires the use of a tiny energy quantum.

The poles can now move apart; this requires only a little additional energy to overcome the attraction between the magnetic monopoles. As the distance between the poles increases, the energy required drops progressively. When an adjacent dumbbell flips, one of the poles migrates one tetrahedron further. Since the procedure can be repeated indefinitely, the magnetic monopoles are effectively freely mobile. In conventional magnetic substances, such as iron, this is not the case. Although these substances contain magnetic moments, the reversal of each moment requires the same amount of energy, since it is surrounded by opposing moments. For this reason, in ferromagnets, the attraction between two magnetic defects does not become weaker as they move apart; rather, the energy required increases without bound, almost as if they were at opposite ends of a spring being stretched apart. In spin ice, by contrast, chains of moments are tangled like spaghetti on a plate that can be pushed around at will. So monopoles are linked, as it were, not by a spring, but by a loose thread.

Da-mo's comment made me realise that I was thinking of Hydrogen as simply "a proton", when I should be thinking of it as a rogue element.

In the case of conversion of NAD+ to NADH (say during reverse electron flow through complex I to generate insulin resistance and superoxide), the chemical equation is:

RH2 + NAD+ → NADH + H+ + R;​

_(note that 'R' is just any molecular carrier for the 2 Hydrogen atoms which can easily donate them to NAD+)_

The intermediate reaction to this consists of splitting the Hydrogens off the RH2, forming a H- (hydride) and a H+ ion. Both these ions are likely entangled.

The H- goes to NAD+ in a reduction reaction that produces NADH, while the H+ ion seems to go freely into the water matrix. I would think that the free H+ is now entangled to the NADH molecule as a whole, and of course, this H+ ion is free to move within the body's proton cabling network (Tensegrity 5) to affect processes.

What kind of information and energy that H+ ion carries I do not know. I do have guesses of course ...

Not shown in the equation above is that the forward reaction requires the addition of 2 electrons. Assuming that the job-to-be-done of NAD+ is to sequester excess high-powered (aka: not easily controlled) electrons and protons.

Invoking Onsager makes me assign the role to NAD+ as an energy redistribution agent. NAD+ would take high-powered electrons from one part of the system, calm them down, and liberate a H+ ion, which would naturally flow to areas of lesser H+ concentration, where they can be used for energy via the beta decay processes talked about in Tensegrity 10 and 11.

The process of using that H+ ion for energy would effect the entangled NADH. I want to say that it finds a way to get back to NAD+, but I don't know the mechanism.

....

I have been told that dissolving tumors is easy, it is preventing them from coming back that is the challenge....

The main component of Guo Lin Qigong is breathing a specific way while walking. It is called xi xi hu breathing (xi = inhale, hu = exhale).

Starting at about 12 seconds, people are in lines doing Guo Lin Qigong. Starting at 20 seconds you can hear the instructor saying xi ("she") xi ("she") hu ("who"). In English this is inhale, inhale, exhale. Two inhales as you swing your arms one way, exhale as you swing them to the other. There are variations on how the arms swing from side to side, but the breathing is always two inhales then one exhale. It's not really important how exactly you do the hand and arm movements (for example, how high your arms go, how far you swing your arms, your hand positions -- are they up or down, etc), but you do want them to be coordinated with the breathing. Breathing is coordinated with moving left or right. Breathing is the most important part of the practice.

Yes I think some of those terms can be problematic to discuss because people are often treating them from different layers of meaning. These layers are not necessarily conflicting, just possibly at different scales that could be nevertheless congruent - i.e. instinctive/intuitive/rational/logical etc. So as my signature suggests, choose a layer you're confortable with.

Take the word dimension - many could take that to mean alternative universes or we could take that to mean changes of scale from macroscopic to quantum. In this way it would lead us to the idea of things being fractal in nature. As above, so below.

Or the word ascension - that implies the aspects of changes in space, time, state, knowledge and has differences in quality whether you treat it as an act, a state or a destination. I could treat ascension as just a change in the perspective of the observer or the ability or act of observing from different perspectives (dimensions/scales).

Words can be such clumsy things .

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-11T01:16:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-11-comments-welcome-here.12294/post-149771>

Going to derail this thread a little with a summary of this article I just saw: [https://medium.com/the-physics-arxi...ecord-smashed-by-sulphur-hydride-c853795079bb](https://medium.com/the-physics-arxiv-blog/high-temperature-superconductivity-record-smashed-by-sulphur-hydride-c853795079bb)

**High Temperature Superconductivity Record Smashed By Sulphur Hydride**

Today, Mikhail Eremets and a couple of pals from the Max Planck Institute for Chemistry in Mainz, Germany, have measured sulphur hydride superconducting at a temperature of 190 Kelvin (-83 degrees Celsius).

**There is a caveat, of course. The material has to be squeezed at pressures greater than 150 gigapascals** — that’s about half the pressure at the centre of the Earth.

Sulphur hydride is not one of these new ceramic high-temperature superconductors. On the contrary, it appears to be a conventional superconductor described by a theory that has been well understood for over 50 years.

Back in 1968, the British physicist Neil Ashcroft predicted that hydrogen could superconduct at high temperatures and pressures. His idea was that because hydrogen is so light, it should form a lattice that is able to vibrate at very high frequencies and therefore superconduct at high temperatures.

Since then, other physicists have shown that hydrogen ought to be up to do this even at room temperature (albeit at pressures greater than those at the centre of the Earth).
​

\-----

Firstly, this is Hydrogen Sulfide superconducting at a higher than expected temperature. We've already heard Jack mention H2S as an important quantum gas in the body, but I'm not going to dwell on that topic in this post because I don't know enough.

Instead, the fact that "high pressure" is needed for higher temperature superconductivity to occur caught my eye. What does "high pressure" mean?

To me, it just means that particles are very close together. If so, then we need to ask what makes particles move closer together. Cold does it, but there must be purely magnetically-induced means of getting that to happen as well.

I stumbled across the Casimir effect while reading Tensegrity #10 -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...elcome-comments-here.12245/page-3#post-148816](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/page-3#post-148816)

The region in between the sheets looks like it could be a "high pressure zone" .....

### Da-mo — 2014-12-11T01:42:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-11-comments-welcome-here.12294/post-149774>

Initial googling found these . . .

> In fact, **at separations of 10 nm**—about 100 times the typical size of an atom—the Casimir effect produces the equivalent of about 1 [atmosphere of pressure](http://forum.jackkruse.com/wiki/Atmosphere_\(unit\)) (the precise value depending on surface geometry and other factors). [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Casimir\_effect](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Casimir_effect)

> Outer and inner membranes were considered to be in close proximity (contact sites) at regions where the **distance between the centers of the inner and outer membranes was ≤10 nm**. [http://www.pnas.org/content/94/14/7314.full.pdf](http://www.pnas.org/content/94/14/7314.full.pdf)

### Da-mo — 2014-12-11T03:12:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-11-comments-welcome-here.12294/post-149783>

and then this . . .

> The cysteine level inside mitochondria is approximately three times that in the cytosol. **Translocation of CSE to mitochondria metabolized cysteine, produced H2S inside mitochondria, and increased ATP production.** Inhibition of CSE activity reversed A23187-stimulated mitochondrial ATP production. H2S improved mitochondrial ATP production in SMCs with hypoxia, which alone decreased ATP production. **These results suggest that translocation of CSE to mitochondria on specific stress stimulations is a unique mechanism to promote H2S production inside mitochondria, which subsequently sustains mitochondrial ATP production under hypoxic conditions.** [http://www.pnas.org/content/109/8/2943.full.pdf html](http://www.pnas.org/content/109/8/2943.full.pdf+html)

So the ingredients might be there for superconductivity.

The above also sheds light on the use of H2S for inducing low oxygen requirements as discussed in this thread [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...about-infrared-and-ct.9675/page-6#post-146991](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/a-little-story-about-infrared-and-ct.9675/page-6#post-146991)

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=146991)
>
> H2S is an uncoupler........it allows life to live without O2 sometime for longer than one would imagine. The Skiier from Norway being case in point. H2S rises in cold.

### PaulG — 2014-12-11T03:55:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-11-comments-welcome-here.12294/post-149794>

Good sleuthing Da-Mo and Yew

[http://www.sciencealert.com/physicists-achieve-superconductivity-at-room-temperature](http://www.sciencealert.com/physicists-achieve-superconductivity-at-room-temperature)

> These Cooper pairs of electrons are able to ‘tunnel’ between the alternating layers "like ghosts can pass through walls, figuratively speaking - a typical quantum effect,”[they report](http://phys.org/news/2014-12-superconductivity-cooling.html), but it was thought this could only occur at super-cooled temperatures.
>
> But then the physicists from Max Planck decided to **see what would happen if they irradiated the YBCO ceramic material with infrared laser pulses**. They found that for a fraction of a second, the ceramic becomes superconducting at room temperature. And when we say “a fraction of a second”, we mean a fraction. “It was only a few millionths of a millisecond,” [says Adam Clark Estes at Gizmodo](http://www.gizmodo.in/science/Scientists-Created-a-Room-Temperature-Superconductor-With-Lasers/articleshow/45379649.cms). "That's a very, very brief lifespan for our amazing new room temperature superconductor. However, the successful experiment is proof that such a thing is possible."
>
> The team suspects this is because the pulses from the laser cause individual atoms in the crystal lattice structure of the ceramic to shift momentarily, which increases the superconductivity of the material.

copper materializes from the giant solution in your cells via electrolysis..........when the current varies you get a problem. Becker and I have talked about copper effects of nnEMF forces.......all tied to Ca2+ and electrical changes in cell membranes. Bone allows electrostatic bonding of 2 copper ions......lysyl oxidase does the same for collagen. And In the brain.........copper comes out of solution in the basal ganglia and liver in Wilson's disease...........showing the brain gut connection electrically.

# Thread: Hibernation

### JMO — 2014-12-07T00:12:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernation.12316/post-149375>

Just got home from visiting my sister from the hospital. She is 57 and is probably one of the oldest living with PWS (Prader-Willi Syndrome). We always thgt she had Downs Syndrome but found out later it was PWS. This disease has to be a story abt energy. They seek food 24x7 and literally eat themselves to death. Obseity at a very young age. She prob has the intelligence of a 8 year old but she could never learn time. There are probably a lot of things that could be learned from her syndrome. Lately she has been unable to arouse because her body temp goes down to 94 -hypothermia and she goes into hibernation. She went out for 24 hours and when her body temp eventually goes up she wakes up and says HI like nothing happened. Not sure what sets these episodes up but I think it's dehydration. It's really hard to make her drink water. I checked her BUN/creatinine and it's 26. She has the docs totally stumped. I look at her disease with a new light now and see she is almost out of energy. A sad disease and very interesting.

### sjoshua — 2014-12-08T19:26:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernation.12316/post-149531>

Wow, it seems her body is overriding 'conscious' choices in order to achieve the restoration it desires/requires... some internally driven semiconductor chilling.

Curious. does she only 'hibernate' when she's an inpatient or does it also happen when at home?

I'd agree you are quite on point with it being an energy story, though that is true of every disease, I'd say this is an more extreme example.

> [JMO said:](/goto/post?id=149375)
>
> She prob has the intelligence of a 8 year old but she could never learn time.

I can't blame her here, I've been trying my whole life and don't fully understand time...

### Jack Kruse — 2014-12-08T19:38:33+0200

When the body is out of energy is changes to pseudohypoxia and it begins to entangle the 3 gases that allow hiberation to occur. CO, H2S, and NO. this uncouples mitochondria. All these are reactions because of a lack of superoxide pulse at cytochrome 1 due to low NAD+. This is talked about in Sinclair paper from cell in 2013 that I referenced in EE12-13 and has been showing back up in Tensegrity 5-11. My biohack in Tensegrity 5 was all about hypoxia........pseudohypoxia is a condition that develops when you cant pulse superoxide to make new mitochondria. Next step is cancer generation.................Her mitochondria are the cause of the phase redox shift. You are capable of teaching her docs a lot now.......just put it all together. The answers are all there if you know where to look.

> I can't blame her here, I've been trying my whole life and don't fully understand time...

She is at her group home when she just goes unconscious and is impossible to wake. She has done this while eating so know she has no control as food is the most important priority for a person with PWS. The last few times they have called an ambulance and we are noticing when her temp goes down she goes to 'sleep'. This was the longest she's been unconscious for abt 24 hours. They put a heart pacer in last time it happened but didn't help the hypothermia problem although it does seem like it helped her be more energetic. [http://ghr.nlm.nih.gov/condition/prader-willi-syndrome](http://ghr.nlm.nih.gov/condition/prader-willi-syndrome)

Yes...it does seem like its an extreme example of a energy deficient person. They are born floppy and it makes me think they are missing their energy network...so is that why they have a insatiable appetite...blonde hair, fair skinned. There were 3 that lived close in our neighborhood almost the same age-so I always wonder abt environmental issues.

The answers are all there if you know where to look.

I was curbsided this AM about a patient that supposedly has [Shapiro Syndrome](http://rarediseases.info.nih.gov/gard/4815/disease/resources/1). I don't think he actually has the full genetic version which only affects 20-50 people worldwide, making it incredibly rare. But they think that this particular guy developed a posttraumatic version of Shapiro's after head trauma years ago. Apparently he undergoes cyclic episodes of severe hypothermia in which he becomes unresponsive for days. They did EEG's on him during these spells, which were essentially normal awake EEG's, leading some to think that he was "faking a seizure." But he was clearly not faking core temperatures of 94 degrees, which freaked out his doctors. Essentially he goes into hibernation and scares the crap out of everybody, and nobody has a clue of what to do with him when this is going on. But after a few days, this resolves and he returns to normal function and normal core temperature until the next attack. This has apparently been occurring for years, although his family now thinks it is becoming worse. (More frequent, more prolonged episodes, I think.) They believe that all of this was induced by a traumatic brain injury...
[http://www.orpha.net/consor/cgi-bin/OC\_Exp.php?lng=EN&Expert=29822](http://www.orpha.net/consor/cgi-bin/OC_Exp.php?lng=EN&Expert=29822)

### NeilBB — 2014-12-14T20:04:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernation.12316/post-150094>

> [JMO said:](/goto/post?id=149375)
>
> Just got home from visiting my sister from the hospital. She is 57 and is probably one of the oldest living with PWS (Prader-Willi Syndrome). We always thgt she had Downs Syndrome but found out later it was PWS. This disease has to be a story abt energy. They seek food 24x7 and literally eat themselves to death. Obseity at a very young age. She prob has the intelligence of a 8 year old but she could never learn time. There are probably a lot of things that could be learned from her syndrome. Lately she has been unable to arouse because her body temp goes down to 94 -hypothermia and she goes into hibernation. She went out for 24 hours and when her body temp eventually goes up she wakes up and says HI like nothing happened. Not sure what sets these episodes up but I think it's dehydration. It's really hard to make her drink water. I checked her BUN/creatinine and it's 26. She has the docs totally stumped. I look at her disease with a new light now and see she is almost out of energy. A sad disease and very interesting.

I also have a couple of seizure patients with Prader-Willi (and also Angelman) Syndrome. But I have not seen the hibernation issue specifically in them.
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11180221](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11180221)

### JMO — 2016-03-09T03:52:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernation.12316/post-190493>

Here's a pic of Shar (Prader-Willi Syndrome). She had cataract surgery this summer and has just completely gone downhill. She's having so many mental issues - waking up at night calling family making up stuff like the staff is in her room or that the police are going to take her away. Bad behavior and throwing fits, biting herself and the 21 meds a day she is on...is not helping...imagine that. She's in a group home and they have taken her to the hospital like 6 times since Jan. Got her some glasses to wear at night but she has her circadian rhythm so messed up. Although it was not the best place, there are a couple crisis centers that specialize with Prader-Willi in other areas of US. The docs here and the group home facility don't understand the syndrome. She has gained back prob 125 lbs in a few years. She is smart enough to know she gets more food at the hospital hence the repeat of bad behavior plus mental issues now. Everything is about food. We had a family meeting over the weekend and I see things so much differently than everyone. She is like the canary in the coal mine.

Any tissues with mitochondria will be most effected......the reason for food is that she needs the electrons to keep her voltage high but if the electrons are not excited by sunlight, and they are not in her case, then she has the drive and craving for more and more electrons only because none are excited to give her the UV she needs............

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-12-10T01:07:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dht.12317/post-149632>

do you mean when one is not converting testosterone to estrogen, DHT is the biomarker? i have a girl with hugely increased testosterone post partum...pg normal at menses (when she took the test, so no indication of ovulation time levels)

### persistence — 2014-12-12T04:16:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dht.12317/post-149867>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=149396)
>
> conversion to estrogen........more protons leads to DHT

I thought testosterone converts to both DHT and estrogens. When you stop conversion to DHT via a drug like Propecia, more of that testosterone may convert to estrogen.

I FINALLY found a way to stop taking Propecia and hope that the mess it creates will slowly unwind. What I discovered was that topical urea (used to treat acne and other skin conditions by forcing more rapid skin turnover) can be used on the scalp. And in my case it seems to directly attack whatever gets stagnated by too much DHT in follicles. The topical urea appears to completely remove the itchiness and hair loss I previously experienced whenever I stopped Propecia. I'm using topical urea 35% in a foam formulation.

# Thread: Are the waves getting too large????

### caroline — 2014-12-07T04:46:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-the-waves-getting-too-large.12318/post-149397>

Dr. K. has said to me that if the waves are getting too large I must ask questions....

I do know that a bunch of us are getting swamped by all the blogs ...and even some threads.

It is amazing to see so many taking all of this on board and processing all the science and asking great questions! I love to see folks digging in ...and all the light bulb moments.

No matter how much I want to ...I will never be at this level......let's be real here!

soooooo - in the spirit of learning - and loving every second of this........and wanting to do so much better -
Do you have questions that would help your understanding ....

Dr. K. has always said there are no stupid questions ......if we want to know - and can't get a handle on it - we need to ask ....or risk being perpetually in the dark. I hate being in the dark!

Dr. kruse is shining his flashlight for all of us ...... so - what do you want to understand better????

We are all here for the same reasons - our journey to our own optimal life .... increase our knowledge, listen to the whisperings of mother nature and dance at the edge of our life.

what simple thing is driving you crazy ...and you want to understand better?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-12-07T05:08:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-the-waves-getting-too-large.12318/post-149400>

Today in New Orleans I was interviewed for a movie.........none of the questions they asked me moved the dime...........I think their project will help mankind. When they speak to Kate I have a sense my words and her story will resonate on many levels..........but here is the rub:
I'm but an apprentice, in a craft where no one ever becomes a master. The difference between me an others.......is that I realize my limitations.
the question is how all do you view your role?

### caroline — 2014-12-07T05:45:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-the-waves-getting-too-large.12318/post-149402>

What a great question ....... how do we view our role.......

It seems I could talk forever about quantum entanglement and quantum magic and living/enjoying life in the moment and I feel it in every fibre of my being ...and it certainly doesn't go un noticed. I get such amazing and positive. feedback from friends and strangers alike - every single day. I feel extraordinarily lucky for so many reasons.

But - can I talk about QED ... nope - not really. For sure - I do get the overall message and I can be an example of how all of this that you are illuminating for us impacts my life. I keep being told how healthy I look and how happy I am and I can't wipe the smile off my face!

The last three + years I have learned countless life lessons from you and others here...... an incredible win/win for me - inspite of my lack of science understanding and abilities to put up graphs like Yew and Josh!!!

Do I want to understand all this better .... of course - I do love all the brain pain

### Lahelada — 2014-12-07T11:38:31+0200

IThanks for making waves Caroline.

I am the one though to say to my friend that her insistence on " diet only fixes all" is like the girl who hides a run in her stockings by moving her skirt up or down but that in the end a run is a run,it will only get bigger and visible I am the girl with the three mjnute message about sunlight and DHA interspersed with enough science to get even recalcitrant docs to look

### Josh — 2014-12-07T19:07:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-the-waves-getting-too-large.12318/post-149428>

> The Three Prisoners:
> After the death of Rabbi Uri of Istalisk, who was called 'The Seraph', one of the Hassidim came to Rabbi Birnham and wanted to become his disciple. Rabbi Birnham asked, "What was your teacher's way of instructing you to serve?"
> "His way," said the Hassid, "was to plant humanity in our hearts. That was why everyone who came to him, whether he was a nobleman or a scholar, had first to fill two large buckets at the well in the marketplace, or to do some other hard and menial labor in the street."
> Rabbi Birnham said, "I shall tell you a story....
> "Three men, two of them wise and one foolish, were once put in a dungeon black as night, and every day food and eating utensils were lowered down to them. The darkness and the misery of the imprisonment had deprived the fool of his last bit of sense, so that he no longer knew how to use the utensils; he could not see. One of his companions showed him, but the next day he had forgotten again. And so his wise companion had to teach him continually. But the third prisoner sat in silence and did not bother about the fool.
> "Once the second prisoner asked him why he never offered his help. 'Look,' said the other, 'you take infinite trouble and yet you never reach the goal because every day destroys your work. But I, sitting here, am not just sitting. I am trying to bore a hole in the wall so that the light and sun can enter, and all three of us can see everything.' "
