> I thought you weren't happy with your Doc???? or did I misread that?

Well, I'm not UN-happy with her.

It was just that the first couple of times I brought up the sex drive issue she danced around it and I didn't push.

The third time I pushed and then she explained why she'd not done anything before - because my answers to questions about sleep, weight, periods, etc and whatever else at THAT time had given her no legit reason to order tests and bill insurance. So she went on to explain she works with this local compounding pharmacy down there in PA (I'm in NY and drive 45 minutes to get to her) to provide BHRT for her patients in this situation who push her and really feel they need some help, and she gave me the lady's phone number at the pharmacy and said to let her know I'd spoken to J.K. and had been referred to her.

But back then I didn't know anything about BHRT, so I went home and looked it up online and the first things I saw were things that said don't take estrogen if you have a family history of strokes, and all kinds of warnings about cancer and this and that, so I panicked. I waited a week or two then called the pharmacy lady, then hemmed and hawed with her on the phone because I was still uncertain of what I was even trying to do, not to mention whaat effects it could have on me. The way my doctor had said she can't help me, but contact this other lady and she can, didn't make sense to me at the time and felt like it was some kind of shady drug deal or something, LOL.

I didn't understand, plain and simple, so I chickened out and didn't call her back. But that's MY fault, not my doctor's. At first I didn't push, then later I didn't follow through. I can't blame her for not being a mind reader though I don't think. I feel like giving her a try, getting a start with her help (my "training wheels" sort of) and then I can hopefully tweak things with the help of what I learn here, with or without her continued help (with or without taking off those training wheels!).

### Penny — 2015-01-02T08:07:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151358>

Hi Hope2Learn and Happy New Year

Here is a link for the blue blockers we wear:
[http://www.amazon.com/Uvex-S0360X-U...1420177289&sr=8-2&keywords=UVEX+blue+blockers](http://www.amazon.com/Uvex-S0360X-Ultra-spec-SCT-Orange-Anti-Fog/dp/B003OBZ64M/ref=sr_1_2?ie=UTF8&qid=1420177289&sr=8-2&keywords=UVEX+blue+blockers)

Paying attention to the light is paramount IMHO - try to eat and be active during sunlight and when the sun goes down, block all blue light - this will help your adrenals make hormones - very powerful -

Dr. K has a bunch of product recomendations and one of them was a blue light with ionizer for the morning when it's cloudy - see his catalog at the top of the home page -

I think vitamin k1 is the one that helps you clot and k2 is the vitamin that keeps the calcium in the bone along with vitamin d - I have also read that fish oil helps with clotting - not sure if you can take fish oil, since you can't eat fish - but I think maybe an epi pen should be your number one priority.... for reasons that should be obvious

Loved your algae "cut out the middleman" post... it will help with iodine, not sure if it helps DHA... maybe flax/walnuts for the DHA? You are peeing so much because DHA is so low -

I really liked Tensegrity 7 - worth a read - in fact the whole tensegrity series and EE (energy and epigenetics) was quite an eye opener -

It sounds like you are living in a sea of EMF - most people get the Trifield meter:
[http://www.amazon.com/Trifield-100X...TF8&qid=1420178038&sr=8-1&keywords=emf+meters](http://www.amazon.com/Trifield-100XE-EMF-Meter/dp/B00050WQ1G/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1420178038&sr=8-1&keywords=emf+meters)

Also, be aware that turning off something might not stop it from emitting wireless - unplugging is great as is putting everything in airplane mode - keep your head away from the wall when you sleep if you suspect dirty power -

Your reaction to the probiotics leads me to believe your gut is a mess so bone broths and fermented foods would be good for you as is garlic, oregano, rosemary, ginger and thyme - turmeric is fantastic as it raises glutathione - this is critical for health and detoxification -

You also might want to get an infrared light and stand in front of it if it's too cold outside - I thought this was cool:

Maybe show your daughter the movie "Mobilize" ? It's tough to get people on board with turning off their toys - one way is to take measurements right in front of them...

The white bread is pretty awful for you - it constipates you and increases your glutamate load - try elanaspantry for a pretty decent bread recipe -

He got them as safety glasses but says he just likes them, his eyes feel better when he wears them. Interesting! I have question.. when I scroll down on Amazon to the other items people buy when buying the blue blocker glasses, there are a lot of orange lightbulbs. Do you know why this might be that people are buying orange bulbs to use with the glasses? Is that something that people HERE tend to do?

Again, I know that on my glaucoma forum (led by one of the top glaucoma specialists in the world, based on US News and World Report... I am so lucky to be finding these resources!!) that lots of people wear the orange and I think they even use the orange bulb there because of the benefit on the optic nerve if I recall correctly, but I will have to go back over there and ask Dr. Anderson about this subject once I get all my ducks in a row and know exactly what all I want to ask of whom and why!

> I think vitamin k1 is the one that helps you clot and k2 is the vitamin that keeps the calcium in the bone along with vitamin d - I have also read that fish oil helps with clotting - not sure if you can take fish oil, since you can't eat fish - but I think maybe an epi pen should be your number one priority.... for reasons that should be obvious

Yes, definitely I will talk to my doctor about that, and about being referred someplace for very specific seafood allergy testing so that I can find out what I can have and what I cannot.

I think the post on page one of my Journal where it was suggested that perhaps my fillings are upsetting my body's mercury tolerance was VERY interesting indeed. But if I get these fillings taken out I will also have to have most of my teeth removed, front few aside, because every single one of my molars has fillings, all of my wisdom teeth are long gone, and some of my molars are no longer there and just have spaces left. (I do already know there is one biological dentist in my general area who actually graduated from a school also in my area, I happened across a banner ad for him on a wellness site a month or so ago, but my insurance would not cover that and I do not have much cash out of pocket to work with at any given time, so as most people do, I have to plan my spending carefully.)

I was supposed to get a root canal done on one of them but everything in my gut screamed at me right up til the second I got into the dentist chair (I do not have a dentist phobia so this was very unusual for me) so once I was there I asked him to just pull it out and throw the offending tooth in the garbage after showing it to me so I could properly take in what damage I'd done to my body. He did all he could to dissuade me but eventually pulled it, let me see it, and threw it away. I'm far happier with it gone.

Each time I"ve ever gotten a filling I've felt wonderful after drilling out tbe bad, and horrible as and after they push the filling into the hole. It's such a horrible feeling of pressure that you have no choice but to just get used to and absorb. But if you let yourself really take it in, as they're pushing the material down into the hole, and let yourself actually FEEL the pressure... well I think most people can't let themselves or they'd go crazy and insist on having the fillings out. I know my mouth is a mess but its' clean and un-infected at least... \*sigh\*

(splitting my reply into two posts because the board software says it's too long)...

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-02T15:00:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151368>

> Loved your algae "cut out the middleman" post... it will help with iodine, not sure if it helps DHA... maybe flax/walnuts for the DHA? You are peeing so much because DHA is so low -

The DHA is made in the algae, seafood doesn't actually make it in hteir own bodies, they only have it to give to us because of their diet on the algae. Now supplements are available that have pure algaeic DHA instead of DHA made second-hand from fish who ate the algae, and they tend to be more expensive and are touted as being more "pure" but I have NO clue about the legitimacy of those claims. But I do know taht the fish are the middleman, the DHA starts in the algae and since humans like to eat fish but not algae so much, we tend to get out DHA from the fish (plus you get those wondeful other benefits like protein, etc, from fish). For me, that's not as ideal though...

> I really liked Tensegrity 7 - worth a read - in fact the whole tensegrity series and EE (energy and epigenetics) was quite an eye opener -

I've been making my way through them and will continue until Iv'e taken in everything I can.

> It sounds like you are living in a sea of EMF - most people get the Trifield meter:
> [http://www.amazon.com/Trifield-100XE-EMF-Meter/dp/B00050WQ1G/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1420178038&sr=8-1&keywords=emf meters](http://www.amazon.com/Trifield-100XE-EMF-Meter/dp/B00050WQ1G/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1420178038&sr=8-1&keywords=emf+meters)

[http://www.amazon.com/Trifield-100X...TF8&qid=1420178038&sr=8-1&keywords=emf+meters](http://www.amazon.com/Trifield-100XE-EMF-Meter/dp/B00050WQ1G/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1420178038&sr=8-1&keywords=emf+meters)

Excellent, thanks, that's the one I've been eyeing for like a year now. It is now in my cart at amazon along with the blue blockers. I will continue researching today to see if I need to order anything else and will go from there (much less expensive even combined than one appointment with a bio dentist I'm sure... and besides, I \*know\* I need these things, but I may not need that dentist.. anyhow that'd be a "last resort" thing, switching out my fillings.)

> Also, be aware that turning off something might not stop it from emitting wireless - unplugging is great as is putting everything in airplane mode - keep your head away from the wall when you sleep if you suspect dirty power -
>
> Your reaction to the probiotics leads me to believe your gut is a mess so bone broths and fermented foods would be good for you as is garlic, oregano, rosemary, ginger and thyme - turmeric is fantastic as it raises glutathione - this is critical for health and detoxification -

Oh, interesting! I thought my reaction to the probiotics meant that my balance was fine without them and that by taking them I was throwing things off, hence my reaction. But the fact that our well water was never tested from January 2002 through 2008 when my husband got so sick, and THEN we found 14 forms of total coliform bacteria, including e-coli, in our water... well it makes me wonder if we dont' all have gut issues. But I also know that if I focus on "fixing my gut" then I will never actually get to the stuff that I need to do, because people who focus on that without having outward symptoms have a tough time knowing when it's fixed, among other reasons. I almost think I will see what I can do without this focus and then work on this as a fine-tuning project later, perhaps. Not sure yet... but for sure I can look into the fermented foods and bone broths. I used to make my own chicken stock al lteh time, which is just bone broth with vegetables and spices of course. Unless there's something else about bone broth that I should know and don't!

> You also might want to get an infrared light and stand in front of it if it's too cold outside - I thought this was cool:

hm that IS kinda neat! I've been advised for some 20 years now to get a light box as sold for people with seasonal affective disorder (hilarious name, eh? Pretty sure everyone has it but most have no SYMPTOMS of it. It would be more accurate, I think, to say I suffer from outwardly noticeable SYMPTOMS of seasonal affective disorder, really... ) but I have not done so.

> Maybe show your daughter the movie "Mobilize" ? It's tough to get people on board with turning off their toys - one way is to take measurements right in front of them...

I will check it out first and then see what I think, thank you for the suggestion.

> The white bread is pretty awful for you - it constipates you and increases your glutamate load - try elanaspantry for a pretty decent bread recipe -

I will check on that, too. You know, my cousin's wife owns her own homemade bread company where she only bakes special orders, she specializes in gluten and dairy free (uses coconut oil) but can also make just about anything else you can think of. Her company is Bare Bread. Most of her customers she gets via word of mouth or local people in her area pick up her business card so I'm not sure that she has much of an online presence, but here's her facebook - [https://www.facebook.com/BareBread](https://www.facebook.com/BareBread) - anyway, I will have to keep this in mind and put it at the top of my list for "first tweaks".

Thank you again for your time and helful post!

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-02T15:36:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151369>

I'm just thinking about the daylight thing... my alarm goes off at 5:30am so I can get my daughter up and ready to get on the school bus at 6:30am... sunrise today is 7:32am and sunset is 4:43pm today.... how do you all deal with this? I can't sleep until sunrise, my child would be an hour late for school if nothing else... and she doesn't even get home until 3:15pm, so how am I supposed to just go to bed or whatever at 4:43pm? And just think of how many hours of down-time that would be for me, too, if I live with the natural light cycle.. I'd be in bed from 445pm through 730am which is 13 hours and 45 minutes! yikes!!

This is a great conundrum for me. Input would be very welcome.

(IT's probably worth noting that I live in an area that is on the top 10 or even top 5 places in the USA with the least amount of direct sunlight. We have 85% cloudy days here, so I very rarely get direct sunlight. I say almost daily how much I hate it here, despite living here since I was 3. I was born on the shore though and spent all my time at the beach, in the sun, in the ocean, barefoot in the sand, until we came here. I recall moving here and spending most of my time finding little patches of sunlight on teh floor to curl up and sleep in, like a dog. I really can't stand it here but no, my husband will not move at this time, we can't. Our entire life savings and all of our money, period, is tied up in this home... now, we've come close to divorce a million times in the past few years and if that ever happens I"m out of here and will go and do where and what is needed for my well being, but right this moment I cannot relocate so will have to work with what suggestions I receive.)

EDITED TO ADD: Another question! What is the deal with concrete re: grounding? Our main section of our house is made on a concrete slab and is overlaid with tiles in the kitchen and bathroom (carpet elsewhere). Would the tiles possibly be suitable for grounding if there is concrete under it, or do each/both cause interference or how does that work? Like I said before, if it's 32 d egrees F outside I'll go out and ground in bare feet but when it's -2 with a windchill, not so much, LOL!

### fitness@home — 2015-01-02T15:43:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151370>

Hope, you can wear the blueblockers before the sun rises and then again after sunset until you go to bed.

My body is so tuned to the sunset these days that I start yawning within 20 minutes of putting the blueblockers on in the evening. My internal clock also knows when it is 8:30 p.m. because on work nights that's when I hit the sack (get up at 4 a.m.). I don't have the luxury of sleeping until daylight and turn on the "red" lamps in our family room for light in the morning.

(I will throw this in here randomly, because I have NO ifea what if anything it might mean - my body naturally becomes ready for sleep at 9:30pm almost without fail, and I find myself waking up spontaneously around 4:30am no matter whether I have distractions or not. I sometimes wonder what is the deal with the 4:30am wakeup though? hm. And lemme just say I'm getting sick of these hot flashes when trying to sleep, too. Now they're just about every night. bleh.)

### fitness@home — 2015-01-02T15:52:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151372>

You can purchase yellow, orange, or red light bulbs to replace the bulbs in your lamps for use at night. Pretty cheap. Check Lowes or Home Depot for them. I also have a salt lamp in there.

Even though I have red bulbs in the family room my husband has the big screen TV on EVERY evening, so I still have to wear blueblockers. I also do a little surfing on my tablet in the evenings.

BTW, my husband is completely used to having low lights (or none) in the evening now. He used to complain at first but I think he likes it now.

### nicld — 2015-01-02T18:09:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151387>

On the weekends and when I am off I let myself sleep until the sun wakes me. During the week I do have to wake up about 5:20am but normally start waking before then anyways or my cat comes and lays on me since he knows what time I need to get up. As soon as my alarm goes off he make sure that i get up to feed him.

Ibuprofen ruins the gut. I was on 2400 mg a day a number of years ago due to over training inflammation. I know that messed with my gut. I did have to get treated for SIBO in 2013 and been working on the gut ever since.

I know that Jack has spoke about seafood allergies but I cannot remember where that was and he has said that it can be fixed. Hopefully someone else who has a better memory can find it.

I have the light that Penny said [http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B0...iveASIN=B000W8Y7FY&linkCode=as2&tag=jackru-20](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B000W8Y7FY/ref=as_li_qf_sp_asin_il_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=9325&creativeASIN=B000W8Y7FY&linkCode=as2&tag=jackru-20) and I love it. During the week I use it when I get ready but on the weekends I try to get 2 hours of light when I can't get outside. Not safe to sit outside for 2 hours at -10 windchill and I know that you know.

Also went through the hot stages at night when I first started but it has got better. Hang in there.

I did hear that tile on concrete would be fine as long as it is not heated with electricity under it. I think the hot water heated concrete should be fine and when we get to getting around to a new place that will be something I will be putting in a basement.

Thank you for the nice compliment about my writings.  I often marvel at how long they've become when all I have done is say what's on my mind without even going into things deeply, LOL. I worry that my rambling will put people off, but I can't really change it in the grand scheme of things. So I just be myself and hope for the best.

I am taking baby steps for sure.. I donk't want to change or add or subtract too much at once because then I won't be able to tell what change is having what effect.. today I discussed the EMF thing with my husband.. he was immediately on board with doing anything it takes to get wi fi out of here, since he's not into technology anyway, LOL.. the blue light he listened to me talk about but had no real input for now other than offering to get me some of the orange glasses like his, but I don't want some that are "like" blue blockers, I want to be sure they ARE blue blockers.. but it's nice that he's so supportive. I didn't mention anything about hormones to him though, because I don't want his supportiveness to be self-serving, or at least not yet. I'll play that card if/when he becomes difficult about some of the changes we're all about to experience around here, largely as far as the refrigerator is concerned. But he won't be a problem, I dokn't want to go into his story but suffice it to say he started all this stuff way before I ever did anyway ,he'll be glad to be forced to get back into it again I'm sure. The same way that I'm recognizing some of what he was doing in what I'm reading is the same way I bet he'll realize what I'm up to when he begins to see the food and supplements I'm going to be buying.

It's all good.

I feel kind of positive today for the first time in a long time. Then again I took a multivitamin today and two fish oil supplements (I was nervous at first but I just asked myself, what are you alergic to? Shellfish. What is in these pills? Not shellfish. Is there an allergy warning on the label? No. Okay then, just take them. And I did. And no reaction after 2 hours now, so I think I should be good to go with these!  ), grounded while working in the kitchen all morning, left all the inside lights I possibly could OFF all day today while I was home, ran errands to get what sunlight I could, and bought steak and fresh lettuce, carrots, celery, cucumber and cherry tomatoes (ok I cheated!) to make for dinner tonight... and s, whether ideal or not, my body just got a taste of a LOT of things it's been missing for a very long time. I hope it likes it, because there's gonna be a lot more where that came from! Baby steps.

Don't rush through life - or anything else - just to get to the end, because all the important stuff is the stuff that happens along the way.

Now, getting it right.... that is something that I am learning about. When I know better, I do better, like with the breastfeeding situation... breastfed my middle child only six months and he was nowhere near as healthy as his older brother, so I breastfed my youngest nearly four years.... I will always do my best, but I must understand the WHYS ... personal quirk of mine... I do not do well blindly following orders, I want to verify for myself that things make sense in my life so that I can get the most out of life... part of why I'm loving this place, and especially your blogs and podcasts.. thank you for taking the time to extend your helping of others beyond your "day job".

No doubt about it. right now my signal is crap and my noise is through the roof. But I'm going to learn how to change all that, and then I am going to implement what I have learned.

I intend to keep this lymph node situation from being lymphoma at the end of this "watch and wait", and I further intend to stop this glaucoma in it's tracks. And you are going to be instrumental in that. It is what it is... and what it is, is a new beginning. My telomeres thank you, I'm sure. Or they will.

### Penny — 2015-01-03T01:37:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151416>

I think you could save yourself a boatload of BS by getting this test or something like it:

[http://www.lef.org/~/media/lef/files/pdf/Food\_Safe\_Allergy\_Sample\_Report.pdf](http://www.lef.org/~/media/lef/files/pdf/Food_Safe_Allergy_Sample_Report.pdf)

You can also guarantee your gut is a mess because of your ibuprofren intake - add this stuff to your order on prime - it's a pretty good all around probiotic:
[http://www.amazon.com/Garden-Life-Primal-Defense-Capsules/dp/B000GWG8FS/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1420241026&sr=8-1&keywords=primal defense ultra](http://www.amazon.com/Garden-Life-Primal-Defense-Capsules/dp/B000GWG8FS/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1420241026&sr=8-1&keywords=primal+defense+ultra)

I wouldn't count on earthing in the house until you measure as it could backfire on you if your electricity is dirty...

How's that epi pen purchase coming... ?

I love your algae-fest line of reasoning - I have some Irish Moss here that I nearly gagged on once - there's a recipe for tea using Irish moss in the nourishing traditions book - but alas, I find myself dragging my feet...

I thought the Japanese had some kind of enzyme that can break down algae but most people can't? Not sure how that works....
[http://news.sciencemag.org/biology/2010/04/japanese-guts-are-made-sushi](http://news.sciencemag.org/biology/2010/04/japanese-guts-are-made-sushi)
Maybe the bacteria to break down the algae rides in on the algae and you can just adapt by eating more algae? That would be cool... I wonder what algae has the most DHA and if it's bioavailable?

I've been eating kippered herring from Trader Joe's and it has been divine... I needed a cheap easy source of DHA that I could stand - sardines are tough for me to ingest and oysters I'll do under duress....

I'm a huge fan of zinc for healing the gut as without it you don't make enough stomach acid - lozengers work the best it seems unless you want to pop for some zinc with carnosine - you can also do zinc with betaine HCL - that helps with absorption -

This stuff is my go-to stuff to heal the gut:
[http://www.amazon.com/NOW-Foods-Ulc...=UTF8&qid=1420241771&sr=8-1&keywords=ulcetrol](http://www.amazon.com/NOW-Foods-Ulcetrol-60-Tablets/dp/B0013OXM0M/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1420241771&sr=8-1&keywords=ulcetrol)

Great call on the "skip the root canal" BTW...

I read at night with a lamp with a red incandescent bulb I got at Lowes for $3.00... that and the ice packs I put on my back at night knock me out...

CFL lights are hideous - they have mercury and they pulse the electricity - go with LED or incandescent -

You might try Neuromins DHA for the fish oil:
[http://www.amazon.com/Natures-Way-N...sr\_1\_1\_cm\_cr\_acr\_txt?ie=UTF8&showViewpoints=1](http://www.amazon.com/Natures-Way-Neuromins-200mg-Softgels/product-reviews/B0009ETA4O/ref=sr_1_1_cm_cr_acr_txt?ie=UTF8&showViewpoints=1)

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-03T03:09:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151422>

[http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0014483510000837](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0014483510000837)

"Retinal ganglion cell axons within the globe are functionally specialised being richly provided with many mitochondria.

Mitochondria produce the high energy that is required for nerve conduction in the unmylenated part of the ganglion cell axons and for the maintenance of optimum neuronal function.

We proposed that in the initiation of **glaucoma (POAG) a**n alteration in the quality of blood flow dynamics in the optic nerve head results in sustained or intermittent ischemia of a defined nature. **This results in normal mitochondrial function being negatively affected and as a consequence retinal ganglion cell function is compromised**. Ganglion cells in this state are now susceptible to secondary insults which they would normally tolerate. One secondary insult to ganglion cell mitochondria in such a state might be light entering the eye. Other **insults to the ganglion cells might come from substances such as glutamate, prostaglandins and nitric oxide released from astrocytes and microglia in the optic nerve head region. Such cascades of events initiated by ischemia to the optic nerve head region ultimately cause ganglion cells to die at different rates.**"

I need to figure out what to do about this. ASAP.

\--------
(thinking out loud)... if the heart is not really a pump but actually a magnetic attractant... with all those energized mitochondria... and if the heart flow when crappy is due to lack of magnetism in the mitochondria...

the eyes have a boatload of (normally, when functioning optimally) energized mitochondria... perhaps the way that the fluid flows through the trabecular meshwork in the eyes, which glaucoma specialists readily admit they do not really under stand fully, is also a matter of magnetics rather than a pumping action as normally thought.. perhaps rather than having an issue pumping the fluid through the meshwork, glaucoma eyes have an issue magnetically attracting it through.... I do not understand enough (yet!) to know how silly this sounds...

(edit - YAY it's not just me! "_**According to an alternative view, primary mitochondrial abnormalities may disturb IOP regulatory mechanisms, thereby leading to the elevation of IOP and initiation of the neurodegenerative injury.**_")

optic nerves carry electrical signals from the rods and cones to the brain. In all of my musings wondering why we can have cochlear implants for hearing loss but not optic nerve implants for glaucoma and other vision loss, the best answers I've foudn is the trouble with making the connection on the brain end... but I mean, we shouldn't need to make a new "internet cable" to send the electrical impulses down, we HAVE teh optic nerve.. w just have to not let those damn retinal ganglion cells die... how does mitochondria play into this... how does it all come together.. light, magnetism, water, so many things all are RIGHT HERE in the eye issue.. I do not want to go blind. Glaucoma has gone nowhere in over 40 years. Sure we have some new drops but that's all pharmaceuticals. We have no new info about what is happening or why now, than we did in the earliest studies and textbooks out there. Because some people have high IOP. But some with glaucoma have normal tension. For every possible reason there's an exception with glaucoma.

The thing is, many people out there are older, desperate, monied, but have nobody working for us to find a solution..some go out of the country and get stem cells injected behind their eyes, they apply tens units to the eye area, you name it, acupuncture, etc (why does that work? how? I understand it's actually a useful thing to do for a lot of reasons for a lot of people..) .. people will pay to implement things they think will elad to a solution, or heck, to just work for real towards a reason for this to happen to begin with.. WHY is my IOP high? and WHY do my optic nerves not handle that stress? ...

(thinking out loud like I said)

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-03T03:32:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151423>

[http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short) - note to self - good stuff here.

> **Mitochondrial Dysfunction Leading to Neurodegenerative Injury in Glaucoma**
> Neurons, because of their high energy requirement, are heavily dependent on mitochondria for survival. Mitochondria not only constitute an energy-generating system, but are also critically involved in calcium signaling and apoptosis. Mitochondrial function can be affected by mutations in mitochondrial and nuclear DNA, chemical insults to components of the electron transport chain, and a lack of substrates such as oxygen. The latter is relevant to tissue hypoxia that is believed to be present in the glaucomatous retina and optic nerve head either primarily or secondary to elevated IOP. Any malfunction of the mitochondrial electron transport chain results in an excessive generation of free radicals. When this overwhelms the intrinsic antioxidant capacity, amplified generation of free radicals results in the state of oxidative stress, which is evident in glaucomatous tissues. Are impaired mitochondrial energy metabolism and oxidative stress in glaucoma a consequence of or a cause of neurodegenerative injury? Evidently, oxidative stress leads to oxidative damage to cellular macromolecules such as DNA, proteins, and lipids, along with energy depletion and a local dysregulation of calcium homeostasis, resulting in neuronal degeneration.[17](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-17) Free radical injury constitutes a caspase-independent component of the mitochondrial cell death pathway in RGCs.[18](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-18) Further supporting the occurrence of oxidative damage during glaucomatous neurodegeneration is that many retinal proteins exhibit oxidative modifications in experimental glaucoma, which may lead to important structural and functional alterations.[19](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-19) An example of oxidation end products in human glaucoma is provided by a recent study documenting the amplified generation of advanced glycation end products in association with protein oxidation in the optic nerve head and retina of human donor eyes with glaucoma.[20](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-20)
>
> A related discussion was on the view that RGCs in a low energetic state would be more susceptible to any other adverse effect. For example, in vitro studies have proposed that by affecting the mitochondria, continuous light exposure may be detrimental to already compromised RGCs in glaucoma.[21](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-21)
>
> Mitochondria are morphologically dynamic organelles exhibiting a precise balance of ongoing fission and fusion during development and aging, which can be modified by disease. Mitochondrial fission, characterized by the conversion of tubular fused mitochondria into isolated small organelles, translocation of dynamin-related protein 1, and reduction of cellular ATP, has been shown to be triggered in RGCs with exposure to elevated hydrostatic pressure, in vitro. In addition, IOP elevation in vivo has been linked to mitochondrial damage in the optic nerve head by the promotion of mitochondrial fission, cristae depletion, and alterations in the expression and distribution of optic atrophy type 1 in DBA/2J mice.[22](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-22) Whether the elevated IOP or secondary ischemia leads to mitochondrial alterations in animal models is unclear, as these two insults cannot be fully dissected in human glaucoma. **According to an alternative view, primary mitochondrial abnormalities may disturb IOP regulatory mechanisms, thereby leading to the elevation of IOP and initiation of the neurodegenerative injury.**
>
> Another exciting focus of discussion was the potential role of reactive oxygen species (ROS) as signaling molecules. It has been proposed that after an initial insult to RGC axons at the optic nerve head, besides neurotrophin insufficiency, increased superoxide generation may also signal apoptosis of the RGC soma. Evidently, there is an increase in mitochondrial superoxide production within RGCs after axonal injury that is further amplified by oxidative stress.[23](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-23) It is also evident that a shift to a reduced intracellular redox state induced by the use of sulfhydryl-reducing agents markedly prolongs RGC survival in in vitro and in vivo models of axonal injury. RGC mitochondria regulate superoxide production differently from other neuronal cells, most likely as a result of differential expression and function of the mitochondrial electron transport chain components.[24](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-24) How ROS act in signaling RGC apoptosis is unknown. An improved understanding of the RGC response to axonal injury in in vivo models of chronic pressure-induced glaucoma can provide specific information on ROS-mediated cell death signaling in glaucoma.
>
> Thus, the structure and function of mitochondria are critical determinants of neuronal health, whereas mitochondrial dysfunction leads to RGC death through caspase-dependent and caspase-independent pathways, initiated by the loss of mitochondrial membrane potential, release of cell death mediators, and oxidative stress.[18](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-18) These mitochondria-dependent events are precisely controlled by the members of the Bcl2 family of proteins. In glaucoma, the proapoptotic Bcl2 family members Bad, Bax, and Bid have all been implicated in RGC death, with Bax being the principal regulator of the mitochondrial cell death pathway in RGCs.[25](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-25) Several upstream modulators of the Bcl2 proteins also exhibit important links to RGC death during glaucomatous neurodegeneration. Death receptor binding can initiate the apoptotic caspase cascade in RGCs, in which caspase-8 is a proximal effector caspase.[5](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-5) Besides this extrinsic (receptor-mediated) pathway, after proteolytic cleave by caspase-8, the truncated Bid can also participate in the activation of the intrinsic (mitochondrial) cell death pathway. Other upstream events regulating the Bcl2 family members have also been increasingly recognized as control points within the RGC. For example, **the phosphorylation state of Bad is critical in determining whether it is able to translocate to the mitochondrion, where it can promote the release of cytochrome _c_.[26](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-26) In its phosphorylated state in the presence of neurotropic signals, Bad is sequestered in the cytoplasm by binding to the protein 14-3-3.[27](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-27) However, in ocular hypertensive eyes Bad is dephosphorylated and 14-3-3 is phosphorylated, thereby resulting in the dissociation of Bad from 14-3-3 and mitochondrial translocation for proapoptotic function. Thus, the phosphorylation state of 14-3-3 also plays an important role in the cytoplasmic sequestration of phosphorylated Bad, thereby adding another level to the regulatory control. Bad can be dephosphorylated by calcineurin, 14-3-3 can be phosphorylated by JNK, and pharmacologic inhibition of calcineurin provides neuroprotection to RGCs in experimental glaucoma as does the inhibition of JNK.** _\[ That seems like it's important but I'm not sure what it means just yet. \]_ Animal models provide a critical experimental tool to further explore cellular mechanisms and specific treatment targets. However, establishing differences between acute and chronic models remains a prerequisite. Particularly concerning the age-dependent component of mitochondrial dysfunction/oxidative stress, it would also be more informative to characterize differences between young and old animals.
>
> The observations just described illustrate the complexity of cellular events that include not only cell death signals, but also intrinsic survival signals triggered in RGCs. **Cross-talk between death-promoting signals (such as death-promoting kinase activity, mitochondrial dysfunction, and caspase cascades) and survival-promoting signals (such as neurotrophic signals, antioxidant mechanisms, and expression of proteins that inhibit apoptosis) determines whether an RGC will die or survive the death stimulus.** Considerable evidence also supports the involvement of heat shock proteins (HSPs), including HSP27, in intrinsic protection mechanisms of retinal cells in glaucoma. HSP27 is upregulated in experimental models of glaucoma, as well as in human glaucoma.[28](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-28) However, **it has become apparent that the phosphorylation state of HSP27 is a critical determinant of its ability to act in a protective capacity as detected in glial cells.**[29](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-29) [30](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-30) It is unclear whether optic nerve head and retinal astrocytes respond differently to glaucomatous stress, or whether glia supply RGCs with HSPs as a neurosupportive effort in glaucomatous eyes. Additional questions are whether the upregulation of HSPs in glaucomatous RGCs may serve as an antigenic stimulus activating the innate and/or adaptive immune response, or whether they may signal the microglial cleaning of stressed cells after binding TLRs.

(continued...)

(Just leaving this here so I can find it again, though I'm reading it now I'm sure I will refer back to it while formulating my glaucoma biohack. Most important one in the long run though I'm working on sex drive as the first priority. But this one will be a lifelong necessity so of course will take a lot of planning and research.. may as well keep some good sources tucked away here for future (and current) reference.)

> **As the conclusion of the discussions thus far was that the functional status of mitochondria may threaten neuronal survival, targeting mitochondrial events with a specific chemical inhibitor or genetic manipulation appears to be a logical approach to neuroprotection. The remaining part of the discussions in this session was therefore focused on potential treatment strategies targeting mitochondria-mediated events.** There are many similarities described between the neurodegenerative processes in glaucoma and other neurodegenerative diseases.[31](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-31) An increasing number of studies over the past couple of decades support that oxidative injury is a common feature of many age-related neurodegenerative diseases. Therefore, data emerging from other neurodegenerative disease models may also benefit glaucoma research. An interesting discussion was on observations related to interactions of oxidants with mitochondrial enzymes that form the α-ketoglutarate-dehydrogenase complex. Oxidative stress-induced reduction in the activity of this enzyme complex has been linked to numerous age-related neurodegenerative diseases, including Alzheimer’s disease. By exhibiting critical links to the pathophysiology, such examples of oxidative injury offer effective targets for anti-oxidant treatments.[32](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-32) A criticism was the lack of convincing clinical evidence to support RGC loss in patients with Alzheimer’s disease. Despite such controversies, mitochondrial energy deficits and oxidative stress may be central to the neurodegenerative injury in different diseases, but may be expressed differently. Oxidative stress therefore appears to be a common treatment target to provide neuroprotection in different neurodegenerative injuries. Another criticism was also related to the lack of epidemiologic correlations.[33](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-33) For example, epidemiologic studies have not detected an association of glaucoma with oxidative stress-causing conditions like light exposure and smoking. Epidemiologic studies have even found diabetes protective against neurodegenerative injury in glaucoma—a likely result of the preconditioning adaptive response, as discussed in Session I. Obviously, susceptibility to neurodegenerative injury is determined by many individual factors and their complex interplay, which is yet to be clarified.
>
> Another relevant discussion topic was the work exploring a treatment paradigm for Leber’s hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON), which is known to be associated with mitochondrial DNA mutations. For example, in a recent study, cells with the G11778A mutation in the complex I subunit, which is responsible for most cases of LHON, were infected with a viral vector containing the human mitochondrial superoxide dismutase gene. Augmenting the mitochondrial antioxidant defenses by viral-mediated gene transfer has resulted in increased cell survival.[34](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-34) **Whether similar treatments directed to mitochondria for enhancing antioxidant defense can be applied in glaucoma remains to be determined. What would be the most appropriate agent(s), who should be treated, and what would be the side effects of various mitochondria-targeted treatment approaches? _\[biohack opportunity alert! ME ME ME!\]_**
>
> Additional discussions on mitochondria-related neuroprotective strategies were focused on the control of mitochondrial function by targeting the Bcl2 family. A caveat in the therapeutic targeting of mitochondria-mediated events is the reversal of the early steps of the cell death cascade. Most important, **once the mitochondrial lipid bilayer is compromised after the mitochondrial translocation of Bax, cell death is inevitable, because already triggered events, disruption of oxidative phosphorylation, loss in ATP production, increased generation of ROS, and release of cytochrome _c_ start the commitment step that represents the point of no return for apoptotic cell death**. Consequently, any strategy targeting mitochondria to block cell death will be effective only if the therapeutic intervention occurs before Bax-mediated events.[35](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-35) At this point of discussion, targeting the upstream phosphorylation cascades associated with the 14-3-3 scaffolding of proapoptotic Bcl-2 proteins in the cytoplasm appears to be a promising strategy for RGC protection. However, there are other upstream molecular events that have been related to apoptotic cell death independent of mitochondrial changes, which are not yet well understood. These events include a downregulation of normal gene expression in RGCs,[36](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-36) and a dramatic shrinkage of RGC somas and dendritic trees before the execution of cell death by apoptosis. Such cellular events, also important in determining the success of neuroprotective treatments, should be further explored.
>
> A very important factor in the neuroprotective treatment of glaucomatous neurodegeneration is that cellular events leading to RGC soma death are independent of the molecular processes leading to axonal self-destruction—most evident in findings that although glaucomatous death of RGC soma can be blocked by genetic mutation of the Bax gene, axonal degeneration proceeds.[25](http://www.iovs.org/content/50/3/1001.short#ref-25) This evidence emphasizes that neuroprotection may be achieved by parallel strategies targeting RGC somas and axons, which critically need a comprehensive understanding of specific cellular processes in different subcellular compartments using the most representative experimental models. In addition, for the purpose of functional gain, treatment strategies activating RGCs for axonal regeneration and normal function are equally important as strategies inhibiting neurodegenerative signals. Based on these two separate demands, neuroprotective treatment strategies genuinely require a challenging design blocking RGC soma death and axonal degeneration while promoting regenerative events to secure visual function./

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-03T04:22:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151428>

hm this also looks good but I don't have a subscription, but will make a note of it here in case I feel like searching a little harder later -
[http://journals.lww.com/glaucomajou...Mitochondrial\_Dysfunction\_and\_Glaucoma.1.aspx](http://journals.lww.com/glaucomajournal/Abstract/2009/02000/Mitochondrial_Dysfunction_and_Glaucoma.1.aspx)

"**Abstract**

Glaucoma is increasingly recognized as a neurodegenerative disorder, characterized by the accelerated loss of **retinal ganglion cells (RGCs)** and their axons. Open angle glaucoma prevalence and incidence increase exponentially with increasing age, yet the pathophysiology underlying increasing age as a risk factor for glaucoma is not well understood. Accumulating evidence points to age-related mitochondrial dysfunction playing a key role in the etiology of other neurodegenerative disorders including amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, Alzheimer and Parkinson disease. **The 2 major functions of mitochondria are the generation of ATP through oxidative phosphorylation and the regulation of cell death by apoptosis. This review details evidence to support our hypothesis that age-associated mitochondrial dysfunction renders RGCs susceptible to glaucomatous injury by reducing the energy available for repair processes and predisposing RGCs to apoptosis.** Eliciting the role of mitochondria in glaucoma pathogenesis may uncover novel therapeutic targets for protecting the optic nerve and preventing vision loss in glaucoma. "

\--------------

and another - [http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/...sCustomisedMessage=&userIsAuthenticated=false](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1442-9071.2008.01740.x/abstract;jsessionid=901B7DDFE57FABA408B19AEB27715FFE.f02t04?deniedAccessCustomisedMessage=&userIsAuthenticated=false) -

"**Abstract**
Primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG) is a pressure-sensitive optic neuropathy which results in the death of retinal ganglion cells and causes associated loss of vision. Presently, the only accepted treatment strategy is to lower the intraocular pressure; however, for some patients this is insufficient to prevent progressive disease. **Although the pathogenesis of POAG remains unclear, there is considerable evidence that energy failure at the optic nerve head may be involved.** Neuroprotection, a strategy which directly enhances the survival of neurons, is desirable, but remains clinically elusive. One particular form of neuroprotection involves the notion of enhancing the energy supply of neurons. These ‘bioenergetic’ methods of neuroprotection have proven successful in animal models of other neurodegenerative diseases and conditions, including Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and traumatic brain injury, but have been relatively unexplored in glaucoma models. **This review focuses on some of the potential approaches for bioenergetic neuroprotection in the retina, including increasing the energy buffering capacity of damaged cells, decreasing the permeability of the mitochondrial membrane pore and free radical scavenging."**

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-03T05:08:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151432>

[http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1471489212001774](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1471489212001774)

"Mitochondrial dysfunction increases reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and when this overwhelms the cellular antioxidant defences, oxidative stress ensues. Oxidative stress is recognized as a common pathologic pathway in many neurodegenerative diseases. **Recent reports have also demonstrated oxidative stress in ocular tissues derived from experimental glaucoma models and clinical samples. There is also accumulating evidence pointing to mitochondrial dysfunction being present in some glaucoma patients. Thus oxidative stress from mitochondrial dysfunction may also play a causal role in glaucoma. The mechanisms by which oxidative stress may induce retinal ganglion cell loss in glaucoma are not fully understood but could include direct neurotoxic effects from ROS** or indirect damage from oxidative stress-induced dysfunction of glial cells. This review will consider the evidence for the presence of oxidative stress in glaucoma; the mechanisms by which oxidative stress may contribute to disease pathogenesis; and also consider therapeutic approaches that target oxidative stress as a means of protecting against optic nerve degeneration."

So how do I limit ROS?

[http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-13-quantum-water-chemistry-2/](http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-13-quantum-water-chemistry-2/)

"when water is made hot, its strongly nucleophilic oxygen core becomes strongly reactive. This reactive oxygen species is what drives many of life’s critical reactions in cells and in pathways for signaling. This is where ROS comes from. "

So if ROS comes from water being made hot, it would seem reasonable that by water being made cold we could limit it, and have a positive effect on glaucoma... ?

Cold water face dunks sound more and more promising!

crazy question, but which teeth were infected and needed to be pulled? Dr. Jerry Tennant has correlated root canals in certain teeth to eye disease via a accupuncture merideans... he has them in his book Healing Eye Disease - I'm curious if there is a correlation - that's me, ever curious about the weird...  It's like the chi is a freeway for the bacteria in the teeth to get to an organ... trippy book too...

You should really dump the sugar -

In fact, you might have some sort of mycoplasma lingering under your fillings - so just for shits and giggles, try brushing your teeth with hydrogen peroxide and baking soda several times a day and see if there is any change in your eyes - I have someone doing this and her mouth has stopped hurting - she has a few autoimmune diseases and there are people out there who think "stealth pathogens" are real...kill them with oxygen I say...

Zinc is also great for the eyes - a caveat - I am no doctor and could be completely full of it...

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-03T15:11:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151456>

> [Penny said:](/goto/post?id=151439)
>
> > Probably also worth noting that I've had antibiotics upwards of 30+ times in my life that I can count probably. I get sinus infections yearly, have had antibiotics when I was bitten by a dog way back when, **when I had a bad tooth and got dry socket, when I was bitten by my cat recentl**y, you name it. I always get a yeast infection the second I go on most antibiotics so now they only use zithromax or else omnicef on me. My monistat gets called in in at least batches of 3 because one doesn't clear it anymore. I do eat lots of yogurt (greek) when on antibiotics but still. Happens every time now.
>
>
> Uh, is it possible you might have gotten babesia from your cat??? Has the cat been tested for anything?

She has not been tested for anything, no.

I got bit accidentally on June 25 or so when trying to help her but she was scared and disoriented and bit me by accident thinking my hand was the handle of the jump rope she had just been stalking and attacking (umm what do you want me to say, it's a cat, haha!)... it seemed fine, I cleaned it, etc... two days later it was warm and puffy, I looked it up and turns out cat bites are the worst animal bites you can get, bacteria-wise. Saw my doctor, he gave me broad spectrum antibiotics which I took all of, it got better immediately and no problems.

Then mid-August I went with my daughter to the eye doctor for her back to school eye exam. The eye doctor is my sister in law so when my daughter was done, she asked me how long since my last exam? Any headaches? I said it's been six years and yes a lot of headaches. She sat me down, did a test, fired up the equipment that had been shut down for the weekend already and made people come back in and do additional testing on teh spot because she knew it was glaucoma she was seeing. I told her about the cat bite, she said she can see it's not toxoplasmosis related eye trouble, nor is it sarcoidosis, neither is there a tumor pressing on the optic nerve, etc. It's optic nerve damage fro mhigh intra ocular pressure. I said, just making sure because of the timing of the cat bite. She said nope, if you'd come in at any point in the past six years we'd have probably seen this developing.

Then August 28 I found the swollen lymph node in my neck, my doctor among other things thought I could have toxoplasmosis when considering the things I may have gotten from the cat that may not have been killed from the antibiotic, and that could possibly have a 8+ week incubation period, but the hemetologist/oncologist said no, the incubation period doesn't match up and that's just a science fact you can't really mess with, it is what it is, doesn't match up, doesn't work, isn't the cause of the swollen lymph node. He did CT scans of my head, neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis looking for lymphoma because this was one of hte last things it could be if it's not sarcoidosis, and he tested for viruses that can be activated and/or re-activated which could affect the lymph system. My Cytomegaovirus IgG and IgM were both high but the CMV DNA test was negative, so he said it's not CMV. My EBV tests were both equivocal, not positive, and I have never had chicken pox or any other EBV related virus so he doesn't think that's it.

The surgeon wanted to remove the node and have it gone over but the oncologist said that if it is lymphoma it's the kind that I won't have to do anything about for awhile maybe never, so we'll indefinitely watch and wait, no appointment needed for fofllow up because flow cytometry on my blood was negative for all common lymphoma and leukemia markers and light chains. So it could be lymphoma but if so it will be non-hodgkins because of how slow it's moving, and ... well anyway no point in going into all of this, I'll copy thigns over from my cancer blog if need be.. just so glad that for NOW it's not cancer... or if it is, I don't have to know it yet, because lymphoma is the only kind where you CAN watch and wait ,the cells become irregular so slowly that chemo won't even recognize them as different yet and work on them anyhow if that's what this is...

BUT anyway so yeah. hey've been REALLY Thorough at trying to figure out what's up with my eyes and lymph node especially is it could pertain to the cat bite. The bottom line is that my aunt and brother are both glaucoma suspects and I have it.

Now, also interesting and which I haven't brought up yet is that there may well be a connective tissue disorder in the family .. but I don't want to go into chromosomes and whatnot just yet. But suffice it to say that genetic testing has shown a chromosomal abnormality in my family which also plays into the possibility of developing glaucoma, so I believe this is a legitimate diagnosis, and I trust my glaucoma specialist. Not just because he trained at Wills, which is rated number two in the world for glaucoma, but because my gut says to. For now I'm alright with that.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-03T15:24:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151457>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=151442)
>
> If you don't know something you have to ask! JK doesn't seem to think replacing fillings is a good idea - but I don't want to speak for him ..... this has come up a number of times. Dr. K. used to be a dentist......

I hope he makes his way back in here and really takes a read of what I have going on. Either he will become famous for fixing my eyes, or I will. He might want to get in on this now while there's still time.  Because I intend to turn the glaucoma situation on it's head and make all the doctors out there who are focusing on high IOP reconsider their way of thinking.

If you look up glaucoma, which nobody EVER does because they think "old people just get it, it's just aging eyes" (NO IT"S NOT), then you will find that the same information is regurgitated time and time again all over the internet but its' the same old stuff foudn in 1970s textbooks - no research has been done since then that has led to any changes, advances, developments, or new understandings. Pharmacological products have been introduced aimed at glaucoma patients, but no actual new knowledge has occurred. We know less about why glaucoma happens (as the **second leading cause of blindness** in the world as it still is!) than just about anything else.

**If you ask people their top three fears most will say cancer, heart attack, going blind.** Well we know there are TONS of societies working to help with cancer research, we know the heart associations of the world are out there working hard to educate about eating, exercising, signs of heart attack, what to do if you feel them, medicines for people who have had them... now what are we doing about glaucoma/blindness? Painting a red line on a white stick and saying I can help you apply for a dog?? WTF?

Well, I realize that statistically I'm in the single digits as far as the number of people who get glaucoma at 41. I realize being a young female of reasonable attractiveness should be able to get me some attention, a platform, upon which to climb and holler to the mountains, "PLEASE SUPPORT GLAUCOMA RESEARCH. **I WILL BE BLIND IN 20 YEARS BEST CASE SCENARIO. THE CLOCK IS TICKING.** WHAT THE FARK ARE YOU GONNA DO ABOUT IT?"... okay well there are any number of things that I could holler and that would be least effective but point is, look, science doesn't know why people have optic nerves that sometimes fail at high pressure, sometimes do fine at high pressure, sometimes fail at normal pressure, usually do fine at normal pressure. If some eyes are glaucomatous with normal pressure, then IOP isn't the whole story. What IS?

Research is starting to focus on mitrochondria and energy failures and I want to figure this shit out like now. I found Jack and I think I can do this.

I want BHRT first but then the eyes have to be prioritized. Yes I'd like to lose this peri-menopause+eating at the holidays weight but I really really am sick of going to bed at night and praying to keep the cancer away and keep my eyes good.

_**How many people get told within 14 days time that they are going blind and have to start seeing a cancer doctor? And what are the odds that, further, they are NOT connected diagnoses? Freakin miniscule but thats' what I got dealt, my friends, so that is what I am playing with. And I intend to win.**_

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-03T23:49:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151525>

I generally rely on greek yogurt and eggwhites for protein early in the day, but I understand eggs are bad for inflammation (a chronic problem of mine) so I'm wondering if dairy is as well? Seems I read that it is. I would prefer steak for breakfast anyway but it's expensive but probably is where my breakfast routine is headed, along with leafy greens. My husband used to add in brown rice with this sort of morning meal. I believe this is bad because grains aren't growing in the winter where I live. And also because I already have an EMF problem right now, so with that knowledge I want to actively fight the carbohydrate cravings and just eliminate them as much as possible ahead of time.. Is that a legitimate thought process?

### Penny — 2015-01-04T03:21:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151552>

Well, here is some more advice - hey free advice is free advice - if you look at this website, it seems to me that it would be useful to get the blood thinned/flowing/less clotted and gingko biloba and DHA are good at that - I'm not advocating this formula - but I like looking at formulas and researching what they do and God by now I have everything in every formula anyway...

[http://www.sciencebasedhealth.com/A...es/Research\_Summaries\_Optic\_Nerve\_Formula.pdf](http://www.sciencebasedhealth.com/Assets/PDF/ResearchSummaries/Research_Summaries_Optic_Nerve_Formula.pdf)

and here is more stuff about the lack of DHA and glaucoma:
**Primary open-angle glaucoma patients have reduced levels of blood docosahexaenoic and eicosapentaenoic acids.**
[Ren H](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Ren%20H%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=16410047)1, [Magulike N](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Magulike%20N%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=16410047), [Ghebremeskel K](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Ghebremeskel%20K%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=16410047), [Crawford M](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Crawford%20M%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=16410047).
**[Author information](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16410047#)**
**Abstract**
The aetiology of primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG), which is the commonest cause of non-remediable blindness and visual impairment, is not well understood. Nevertheless, increased intraocular pressure, and vascular factors such as ocular blood flow deficits are thought to be risk factors. There is evidence of decreased optic nerve blood velocity and increased red blood cell aggregability in POAG. These factors are influenced by fatty acids. We have investigated if glaucoma patients have abnormal blood fatty acid composition. Patients with POAG (n=10) and their healthy siblings (n=8) were enrolled. Compared with their healthy siblings, the glaucoma patients had reduced eicosapentaenoic (EPA, P<0.01), and docosahexaenoic (DHA, P<0.05) fatty acids and total omega3 long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (LCPUFA) (P<0.05) in red cell choline phosphoglycerides (CPG); decreased EPA (P<0.05) in ethanolamine phosphoglycerides (EPG); lower EPA (P<0.05) and total omega3 LCPUFA (P<0.05) in serine phosphoglycerides (SPG). Similarly, they had reduced EPA, DHA and total omega3 LCPUFA in plasma CPG (P<0.005) and triglycerides (P<0.05). These findings may be significant, since EPA and DHA could modulate impaired systemic microcirculation and ocular blood flow and optic neuropathy, which are the main physiological changes associated with glaucoma.

So, it seems to me that antioxidants like curcumin, resveretrol, grape seed extract, pycnagonal, vit c / e would be beneficial as would stuff to get the blood unclotted - naturally, the reason it's clotting is you are dehydrated from all the EMF in your house - look at this 3 minute video and you can see the problem:

and here is some Russian research on calcium efflux from EMF linking to the increase in glaucoma:
[http://scholar.google.com/scholar?q...a=X&ei=9ZOoVMOiFsPUoATz4YKwDA&ved=0CCcQgQMwAA](http://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=calcium+efflux+emf+glaucoma&hl=en&as_sdt=0&as_vis=1&oi=scholart&sa=X&ei=9ZOoVMOiFsPUoATz4YKwDA&ved=0CCcQgQMwAA)

and here is a link to [www.lessemf.com](http://www.lessemf.com) where you can buy a grounding cable for your laptop and a pad for it - you also might want to get the silver gloves with a grounding cord IMHO... you say you are on the laptop a lot - is there a desk top computer with stone age mouse available for you to use? Maybe when everyone goes off to school/work, you can unplug some of these devices?

Here is what I use for DHA in addtion to eating seafood - you should get an allergy test IMHO to see if it is all seafood - I use it almost solely for its blood thinning properties - I know it's probably oxidised but strokes really suck...:
[http://www.amazon.com/Nordic-Natura...\_3?ie=UTF8&qid=1420334373&sr=8-3&keywords=DHA](http://www.amazon.com/Nordic-Naturals-Prenatal-Unflavored-Softgel/dp/B0062CW1BG/ref=sr_1_3?ie=UTF8&qid=1420334373&sr=8-3&keywords=DHA)

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-04T03:51:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151557>

Penny, your post led me to find this - [http://www.iovs.org/content/49/11/4912.short](http://www.iovs.org/content/49/11/4912.short) -
**Mitochondrial Defects and Dysfunction in Calcium Regulation in Glaucomatous Trabecular Meshwork Cells**

"Disruption in intracellular calcium ion (Ca2+) homeostasis has major effects on health. Persistent Ca2+ overload induces mitochondrial permeability transition pore (MPTP) opening, which prompts mitochondrial release of calcium (mCICR) and reactive oxygen species (ROS) into the cytosol which, in turn, compromises mitochondrial function. This study was conducted to examine intracellular Ca2+ levels and mitochondrial vulnerability to Ca2+ stress in trabecular meshwork (TM) of individuals with primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG)."

"**conclusions. POAG TM cells have defective mitochondrial function, which causes them to be abnormally vulnerable to Ca2+ stress.** The dysfunction in calcium regulation by these cells may contribute to the failure of this tissue to control IOP. Pharmacologic inhibitors of IP3R, MPTP opening, and cyclophilin D could have clinical implications for primary open-angle glaucoma."

(more at link) - thanks for the links and suggestions! I'm going to replace this wireless mouse with another wired one next time I go out. I always used them before until a few months ago when I got the wireless one so it won't hurt anything to go back to wired. I will look into grounding cables for the laptop, thank you for that suggestion as well. I will see what kind of DHA I bought and share the link here to see what people think of it.. hopefully it's not useless, but I'm' still learning so I didn't know for sure what I was even looking for.

### Penny — 2015-01-04T03:58:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151559>

It's not useless if it unclots your blood So I just found another link with 2 interesting links at the link - one on vitamin c and one on green tea being a great antioxidant for glaucoma -

For calcium efflux, I think the "recipe" is 1 part MK4 to 10 parts vitamin d - magnesium helps and so does b12 - epsom salts would be fabulous for you - the only vitamin k out there with enough in it is the one from Life Extension - but Dr. K would be the expert on calcium efflux - actually, [www.lef.org](http://www.lef.org) is a fantastic resource with cutting edge stuff in it -

[http://www.lef.org/Magazine/2010/6/In-The-News/Page-01?p=1](http://www.lef.org/Magazine/2010/6/In-The-News/Page-01?p=1)

**Green Tea Could Help Combat Diseases of the Eye**
Green tea catechins are able to penetrate eye tissue, which may help protect against glaucoma and other eye diseases, according to Hong Kong researchers.

Chi Pui Pang of the Chinese University of Hong Kong and colleagues orally administered a green tea extract to rats and measured catechin and 8-epi-isoprostane (a marker of oxidative stress) levels in the animals’ eye tissues at varying time points up to 20 hours later. The team found varying distribution of catechins throughout the animals’ cornea, lens, retina, choroid-sclera, vitreous humor, and aqueous humor. A significant reduction in 8-epi-isoprostane rapidly followed green tea administration and was well maintained in all tissue and fluid examined with the exception of the choroid-sclera, demonstrating the antioxidant activity of catechins absorbed by these areas.\*

“Our results indicate that green tea consumption could benefit the eye against oxidative stress,” the authors conclude.

—Dayna Dye

Reference
\* J Agric Food Chem. 2010 Feb 10;58(3):1523-34.
**Editor’s note:** Delivery of orally consumed nutrients to the eye can be less than optimal. This study reassures those who consume green tea and green tea supplements that tea catechins do penetrate eye tissue to exert their antioxidant benefits.

And my EMF meter has shipped, it will be here day after tomorrow... Jack's book and my blue blockers should also be here that day.

Already made sure my son's xbox is hard wired and not using wi fi, and disconnected my old PDA that used to be one foot from my pillow, and packed it away - no more wi fi there either.

But who knows how the wiring really is until the meter is here.. fingers crossed that i can get this part of things sorted fairly easily!

Yep my rheumatologist sait it was Reyanud's because of how they turn blue and red and white or whatever. I did two sessions of face dunking today after my shower, and then had steak for breakfast and put beef stew in the crockpot for later.

Just got off the phone with the compounding pharmacy - they said they do the consult in person or over the phone, whichever I want, and I asked if they do testing and if so, saliva or blood? And she said most of the time he knows what you will need based on the symptoms you have but sometimes if things seem way off or something he'll do some testing.

I said "well I am the kind of person who wants a good solid baseline to work off of, I don't want to pour one hormone into my system if say my adrenals aren't in good shape, so maybe I should check all of that and also get my thyroid done first, for example".. she said "oh do you have thyroid issues too?" And I said "no but my labs over the past seven years consistently put me in the very lowest possible range of normal for the most part and I'd like to optimize things if I'm gonna do it".

She said that she will talk to him and see what stuff he wants to do and meanwhile I got my appointment for the consult set up for the 21st, in person (I just feel better that way). I said I'm in NY so I have issues ordering my own tests and she said that I should think about what I want to have done and talk to him abuot it and compare it to what he thinks he would like to have done, and together we can put together a list of tests to do and he can quote me a price.

He's gonna call back today to do that though so I need to figure out what I want to do.

She said something about ZRT when I said testing.. is that a lab? (Ah yes, thought so! [https://store.zrtlab.com/](https://store.zrtlab.com/) ) I need to look that up and get a list together immediately!

Ahhh so excited!

### Penny — 2015-01-06T07:14:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-151794>

Hi Hope2Learn - I have another book for you to buy... and I have high hopes it will cure your glaucoma... It's by Dr. Jerry Tennant MD extraordinaire and it's called "Healing is Voltage: Healing Eye Diseases" - truly awesome - there is an unholy amount of info in this book - easy to read and he tells you exactly what to do... and not just about the eye - lots of info - I learned a lot by reading it - BTW, it is quite likely that you are full of toxic gut pathogens generating tons of ammonia and alchohol for the sulfur pathway to process that it is being overwhelmed... quite common in ADD - in fact Dr. Feingold would give 300 mg of molybdenum 3 times a day and I think it was 500mg of pantothenic acid - molybdenum also helps clear coffee/chocolate (xanthines - thanks Josh for that incredible link you sent me) as well as alchohol and sulfites - Here is a bit about the enzymes that use molybdenum:
Molybdenum _cofactor_ is a _cofactor_ required for the activity of enzymes such as sulfite _oxidase_, _xanthine_ oxidoreductase, and aldehyde _oxidase_. It is a coordination complex formed between _molybdopterin_ (which, despite the name, does not contain molybdenum) and an oxide of molybdenum.

Truly, this info would have been marbelous in my twenties... but at least I knew about B12...

Anyway, you might want to take lots of B12 to fight glaucoma because it is protective against glutamate storms - He also goes into the Nitric Oxide system and how it affects the eye - he thinks it's a lymphatic system screwup and that causes pressure in the eye to build - then he talks about how vitamin A is great for the eye, but unfortuneately vitamin A is fat soluble and so if you have no gall bladder, you don't have enough bile and so may become deficient in vitamin A - then he talks about how nitric oxide is made from amino acids - I think l-arginine - but if you are low in zinc, B1 and iodine (this is how EMF screws up your stomach - it uses up your iodine) you can't make stomach acid to break down proteins into amino acids - anyway the beginning of the book talks about all the different circuits involved in computers that relate to the body and also he talks about Becker's work - he correlates at least 6 different ancient systems using "chi" along with accupuncture meridiansn and which organs are affected - anyway, he makes Dr. K a lot easier to understand - I thought it was great - anyway, you were looking for original thinking and I think this guy is it...

There is also a Dr. Kondrot who does a question and answer thing on Sundays - he also uses microcurrent to heal the eyes like Jerry Tennant -

So, in the book he talks about stagnant lymph in the eye - along with the cranial sacrum pump - I thought for shits and giggles you could try taking red root because it helps tone the lymphatic system - so I googled red root lymph and glaucoma and came across this amazing pdf on nutrition for the eyes:
[https://www.herbaltherapeutics.net/...\_for\_eye,\_ear,\_nose\_and\_throat\_conditions.pdf](https://www.herbaltherapeutics.net/_media/herbal_therapies_for_eye,_ear,_nose_and_throat_conditions.pdf)

I would strongly urge you to get the book as it has info not just on the eye but this whole energy healing modality stuff is explained - this book answered so many questions I had about a lot of topics - really great - let me know what you think of it -

take care

Thank you for the information! Tennants website only talks about macular degeneration and givevs no information on optic nerve related issues but perhaps his book will be different. I have heard of Dr. Kkndrot and know somenody who has employed his techniques as a patient of his and despite spending a long time (year plus if I recall correctly) using the electric stimulous recommended has not had any improvement of his glaucoma, and in fact it continues to progress, as it always does. He has also had stem cells injected behind his eyes, and any number of other treatments that many think of as quackery, including acupuncture, to no avail (though he still recommends acupuncture for other reasons).

Glaucoma is not likely to be solved by woowoo-turned-science I'm afraid. Which is why I am here. I have always preferred to reverse engineer problems into solutions, and this will be no different. Jack says "biohacking", I say reverse engineering of humans. Same thing IMO.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-06T18:47:10+0200
