> Diagnosed with glaucoma, referred to an oncologist for suspicion of cancer, find out I have Hashi's, narrowly avoid a trip to the ER due to exposure to some seafood that I \*STILL\* don't have allergy test results in regards to, then find out my eyes are continuing to worsen to an extent that actually has my glaucoma specialist concerned. Which I didn't think this guy could do, he's so laid back.
>
> W.
> T.
> F.
>
> I know that I'm new here. I know that I"m not wll known, and lately all that I've shown is my moody PMS side, and now this. But SERIOUSLY. Seriously. OMG.
>
> The last thing I expected was to find out that this wasn't under control. F\*&&!!!!!!!!
>
> WHY. I mean, I know I should be happy, right? It's not like i'm going deaf, this way I can still hear the music. And I always said if I had to lose one sense just don't take my hearing, because I just need to hear the music and it'll be okay.
>
> But nobody is home and I just got crappy news and my eyes are STILL NOT cooperating, seriously? AND I CAN"T TAKE THESE NEW DROPS NOW?
>
> I'm just ... out of words. Not even Jack can save my eye sight, I don't think. Not Jack, not me, not my eye doctor.
>
> This sucks. There is SO MUCH I haven't seen yet... d@m&^!!!

I am so sorry you are going through all of this, Hope... I was just sitting here trying to think of things from my perspective as a newbie...I know very little..but I was wondering if using some aggressive CT fournier effect therapy might help with the brain and ultimately with the glaucoma...here is a link Dr Kruse put out there somewhere a while back and I wondered if maybe it would help deliver more of the goods to you eye tissues...anyway, here is the link to a news story: [http://abc13.com/health/doctor-uses-cooling-treatment-to-save-his-own-life/426432/](http://abc13.com/health/doctor-uses-cooling-treatment-to-save-his-own-life/426432/)

Now obviously I have no idea in which directions you want to apply the fournier effect, up, down, or both, or not at all, or if it will even help, but I am just throwing it out there to chew on...for example as you have seen in the CT posts on the forum, several people CT their bodies with an IR lamp aimed at the parts not in the water...etc...

Also, just did this search which might help:

### cinnamon — 2015-01-22T00:28:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153567>

> [Hope2Learn said:](/goto/post?id=153527)
>
> ... Fournier would be doable except for lack of a hottub to do it in. ...

Do you have a large plastic box/bucket/tote, or an old plastic dishpan that is big enough to stand in? I put hot water with some epsom salts in mine and then stand in it in front of the sink while I splash cold water on my face and hands. My tap water is really cold, but I imagine yours might be too? Try to drink some very cold good water just before and while you are doing this. If the water at your feet starts to cool off, pour a bit more hot water in.

Don't give up!

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-22T00:56:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153568>

oh you're all so sweet, it's so humbling and lovely to come home from a long day of appointments to find such kind and thoughtful messages of encouragement and support. Thank you all for that. I want to - and will - address each post individually in a little while, but for now my eyes are bothering me and I'm just plain worn out. I figure I have the energy to type one post for now, or at least one GOOD post, before I turn into a zombie for the evening.

And I wanted that post to be about my pharmaceutical consult today.

I was first referred to this man, L.B., a couple of years ago, but I never actually made an appointment with him until a few weeks ago, with the first appointment being today. He and his wife have a smalltown compounding pharmacy and do BHRT together with my gyn, and surely other doctors in the area. I knew that much when I made the appointment for the consult.

Well, today I spent about 90+ minutes having someone tell me almost word for word a lot fo what Jack has been teaching, and what you all have been saying. In fact, I asked L.B. within the first three minutes if he knew who Jack Kruse is. He said "who is John Kruse?" and I said "Jack" and then said a little bit, and then we moved on. Ninety minutes later I was writing down Jack's website for B. to take a look at in his spare time, because I think he's worked out 85% of what Jack teaches already on his own, and could be really tremendously helpful to people around here (even more so) if he exposed himself to some of these ideas he hasn't come up with himself yet.

Anyway, he told me almost straight away that:

\- my gut is probably bad, and mentioned my HPA axis (forget what he said now)

\- I probably... no definitely.. have adrenal fatigue and have to get that sorted first thing,

\- I need iodine, ESPECIALLY if I have Hashi's

\- I crave sodium because I NEED sodium, and I shouldn't restrict it at all. My rings don't fit my fingers in the morning and I have sock marks on my ankles at night because I need more salt to act as a dieuretic and for other needs. But I should quit all carbs/grains completely and get my salt via sea salt or himalayan or kosher, whole salt mixed into warm water twice daily.

\- I need to go on the special diet that He came up with about 7 years ago that totally eliminates grains and dairy and limit fruit to summer only, and mostly berries (and don't eat bananas at all, they're just worthless carb/sugar sticks in yellow peels) and I need to not touch these (and a few other) things at all again ever, and in about 6 months I should be feeling better if I tend to all these things with his three step protocol: His diet, His relaxation techniques, and His supplements that he recommends for Adrenal Fatigue.

\- Stop eating egg-whites and start eating the whole egg! Cholesterol is good, the USDA pushes grains and is corrupt as much as big pharma due to politics and money and there has never ever been one person autopsied who has ever had saturated fat in their arteries, it's always transfat.

okay, so this made me feel three parts good and one part skeptical.. HIS diet sounded just like Jack's for the most part. I wasn't sure what to make of this. And his salesmanship of his protocol that takes a six month commitment and consists of these particular supplements he recommends to everyone made me feel a little skeptical. And the fact that they said they don't need test results to know what someone needs, they can figure that out based on symptoms.. and 90% of people who come in have symptoms of adrenal fatigue... and are sold his protocol... BUT all that he said sounds like what Jack says mostly... so... I think maybe I'm just pessimistic/paranoid and should trust this guy. But then again... I dunno. He said he goes to conferences and asked if I ever heard of some Walsh guy who he said is big into adrenal fatigue, and said that they sat down and talked a few years ago because Walsh wanted to know what L.B. was doing to help his patients so effectively with adrenal fatigue.. so that sounded like a good reference if only I had a clue who this fellow was... I will look him up...he also gave me paperwork that includes a link to westo

For my sex drive he said he's recommending JK prescribe me progesterone lozenges that he makes there and splits in half before putting them in the bottle - I put one half-lozenge between my tongue and cheek the first two days of the last half of my cycle or something like that. I don't know, I don't have a prescription yet or anything, and I won't be at that part of my cycle for a couple of weeks.

My brain just shut right off so I'll post this and hope that you all forgive me for skipping the personal replies for now. I called the glaucoma doctor earlier but he's in surgery today so I have to hang in there until tomorrow to find out if I can take a different drop. Interestingly, B. said that the older carbonic .. something or other drops largely worked like a diuretic, and he said that he thinks me increasing my salt intake might very well help my glaucoma because of that.

Also said he has ladies put the iodine right on their necks over their thyroid sometimes....

yeah braindead sorry.    sorry again for ranting lately...

oh um... he also said forget about DHA, it's not important. \*gulp\* He said forget fish oil supplements altogether, he compounds capsules of flaxseed oil for omega 3's and recommends that instead... .??

Just realized I didn't put that above comment into context...
I called my glaucoma specialist two days ago and asked if I could come in and exchange these new drops for some other kind. Then I explained that the reason I don'nt want to take these is, the paperwork has an allergen warning that says if you have an allergy where you have an anaphylactic reaction, this medicine may make those reactions more likely and in those people, it may make epinephrine not work. I explained that since I'm allergic to the only alternative treatment, benadryl, this would leave me with no options if I were to have another allergic reaction where my throat closes.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-26T14:24:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153893>

The new glaucoma drops are a no-go as well. They decreased my heart rate such that it put me to sleep/made me pass out depending on whether I was putting them in before bed or in the morning. Bradycardia and syncope are deal breakers for me, since dad died of a sudden heart attack at 46 and my 50 year old brother has already had two, and I am 42 now.

That means the prostaglandins are out, the beta blockers are out, and the carbonic anhydrase inhibitors are apparently out, or at least this one is. I will call my GS later today but chances are quite high that he's going to try to get me to let him burn holes in my eyeballs with a laser. Can I get a "F\*\*\* NO!" ? Sorry doc, no can do. My vision is 20/20 and 20/25, and that procedure will shit-can my vision. Will it be worse than 20/500? No. That's why they justify doing it. "You could become legally blind without it" (which would be 20/500 or worse). They don't take into account that my vision is excellent at this time, and that they'd be causing damage to eyes that may never even be damaged otherwise, or at least not for a long time.

crap-a-doodle-doo.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-26T16:25:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153899>

random thoughts for today..
\- I want to build an ingloo or snow fort, so if we get this nor'easter I will, even if my daughter doesn't want to play in the snow with me. I haven't played in the snow for at least 15 years if not 25.
\- my son clearly has bad adrenal fatigue but he is so skin and bones that he's lived his whole life in ketosis, so I have no idea where to begin to help him.
\- television, social media, and most websites make me feel bad about myself, while this place makes me hopeful and challenged.
\- my N=1 is not motivated by sternness, hostility, rudeness, cruelty, or even flat apathy, and CERTAINLY not by stereotypical sports-like "motivational speeches" consisting of "SUCK IT UP! WORK HARDER! GO GO GO LITTLE SISSY!" or equivalents... it is only motivated by compassion, and it is difficult to be compassionate from inside rather than getting it from others. I need to learn this skill.

I am always willing and able to give this gift to others in need but I find it nearly impossible to find someone willing to do so for me. Society is not moving forward IMO. I can either resent this fact and stagnate, or accept it and try to find a solution. I spent several days stagnating and stomping my foot and whining. Now it's time to get back on the horse and try again to find a solution.

But I still believe that many things "take a village" and I believe that we ARE All connected, so I find it ridiculous to turn around and tell myself that I must do things on my own. That is not the 'right way'. Even if it is the neccessary way, for now.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-26T21:28:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153916>

Okay, it all comes back to DHA. And if I can't get it from MOST supplements, and I can't eat seafood, then I have to find the exception to the MOST supplements rule. Because MOST is not all.

But I did finally grasp that CT is not for me at this time and that's OKAY because I"m living in a chronically uncoupled state anyhow. Not ideal, but hey at least it has one perk - while the cold intolerance exists, the CTing is unnecessary anyhow. The reason behind it sucks but it is what it is, until it isn't because I make it not be. Which is why I'm here.

(And yes Vitamin A and Vitamin D, and leafy greens are all good for me... and EMF and blue light are bad for me... already got the multivitamin and vitamin D supplements, the blue blockers, the EMF meter, unplugged the microwave, keep most lights off as much as possible all day and night... drinking bottled spring water or RO water as much as possible, switched out my pretzels at night for organic trail mix consisting of a few types of nuts (not not peanuts, they're not even nuts) and dried cherries and raisins... my sleep is always regular so that's good...

I already know that dairy makes me feel like ick and avoid it habitually most of the time except for some cheeses and for awhile I was having a greek yogurt every morning for the protein, but now I'm eating steak for breakfast by 9am instead of eating nothing until noon and then having carbs and dairy (yogurt and toast)....

....so, I'm working on it even if I Don't update each day to say "took my vitamins and supps again today, just got done iwht my breakfast steak for the 11th day in a row, and drinking water again instead of my soda"... even though those are all true and new and consistantly being applied. But my glaucoma has been first in my mind lately so that's been what I've been bitching about, instead of listing the good I do each day. But I am consistently working on things, to such an extent that I'm already sick of going for blood tests and DEXA scans and follow up appointments to go over results and consults with the compounding pharmacist, etc etc...)

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-26T21:43:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153917>

speaking of glaucoma, I didn't take the Simbrinza yesterday morning or night nor this morning, and called the doctor's office a little bit ago to let them know about my reaction, and to ask if he would like me to try something else. I told them that he just gave this to me and began to explain how it made me feel, and they interrupted me to take my name. I told them, and they said "okay we'll note it in your chart for you" and got off the phone.

Humm. Interesting strategy. Perhaps I have overstayed my welcome there like most other places I turn up.

I suppose I will run it by my doctor when I see him next so that he can explain to me how to interpret his receptionist's response. I'm sure he knows her better than I do.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-27T00:50:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153932>

Just a short update - my mood is much better when I stay away from carbs and dairy. Definitely.

but my eyes... they hurt so much right now. it terrifies me. i can't use this second drop and it's not time for the regular one until bedtime and they are just so not good. these pressure headaches/eyeaches always mean the pressure is up more.

i will have to do something short term whether i like it or not because of the pain. and who knows how long my optic nerves can withstand this higher pressure.

this has me feeling very, very sad right now. lack of control is so difficult for me.

All of the things Im learning here will help, surely ARE helping already, but the fact is that glaucoma causes blindness when it can't be controlled, and right now it's not controlled and I'm running out of drug classes to try, and time is of the essence. So I will have to find a non-JK intervention in coming days most likely, and that is so very frightening to me.

so I just wanted to say thank you all for stopping in and checking in on me and letting me know you're around. I'm very isolated out here in the snowy hills with a snowstorm coming in and no friends anywhere that I can see them more than a couple of times a year, it's just me and immediate family.. and sometimes it really helps to have outside perspective.

PS - before I got to post this my eye doctor called back. I was a tad weepy and apologized (totally unlike me in person, which is probably why my emotions spill out here so easily - have to vent somewhere) and bottom line is he is calling me in some azopt, another carbonic anhydrase inhibitor but without the drug that slows the heartbeat for some people. he said he didn't have anymore free samples of anything else at first and i said i don't care, whatever it takes, please find me something because they really hurt... so.. my husband is getting the new drops for me before he comes home. he is plowing now...

i'm so thankful people are willing to help. mister rogers would be pleased.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-27T01:04:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153933>

note to self:
[http://www.discoverymedicine.com/Ro...nction-of-mitochondrial-cytochrome-c-oxidase/](http://www.discoverymedicine.com/Robert-O-Poyton/2011/02/20/therapeutic-photobiomodulation-nitric-oxide-and-a-novel-function-of-mitochondrial-cytochrome-c-oxidase/)

reminds me of :

[http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-10-multiple-sclerosis/](http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-10-multiple-sclerosis/)

"What happens in a mitochondria when there is too few electrons and too many protons? Cytochrome c is a small heme protein found loosely associated with the inner membrane of the mitochondria. Cytochrome c is a highly water soluble protein, unlike other cytochromes. Cytochrome c carries one electron. It is capable of undergoing oxidation and reduction, _**but does not bind oxygen**_. It transfers electrons between Complexes III (Coenzyme Q – Cyt C reductase) and IV (Cyt C oxidase). Cytochrome c is also involved in initiation of apoptosis.
So when electrons are lost to the environment, apoptosis runs rampant. This is how myelin is destroyed. Cytochrome c is suspected to be the functional complex in low-level laser therapy. Ironically, MS patient emit diffuse light. This is why low level laser therapy is helpful to those with MS. In LLLT, red light and some near infra-red wavelengths penetrate tissue in order to increase cellular regeneration. Red light provides photons to help restore tissues by rescuing Cytochrome c. [Optogenetics](http://video.mit.edu/watch/optogenetics-controlling-the-brain-with-light-7659/) one day, may heal demyelinating plaques by controlling the brain with flashes of polarized light. I mentioned this science in relation to DHA and AQA 4 function earlier. How light polarizes water is incredibly important in the human brain. The information contained in the light signal determines how AQA 4 can act or how it can result in disease. MS is a disease of light. Moreover, these actions directs the quantum electrodynamics of water in tight spaces below 1.4 nm. This is where instantaneous energy and info is imparted to microtubules and where quantum biologic computing begins."

(note to self continued... )
... so read it when the eyes behave.

### JanSz — 2015-01-27T01:53:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153937>

> [Hope2Learn said:](/goto/post?id=153932)
>
> but my eyes... they hurt so much right now. it terrifies me. i can't use this second drop and it's not time for the regular one until bedtime and they are just so not good. these pressure headaches/eyeaches always mean the pressure is up more.
>
> i will have to do something short term whether i like it or not because of the pain. and who knows how long my optic nerves can withstand this higher pressure.

You footer says:
_1\. I was diagnosed with early Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma in August, 2014 at age 41._
\---------------------------------------------------------
I have narrow angles, but no increased pressure.
Had this procedure done on both eyes.

Ask your doc if you could be candidate for this procedure.
From description below it seems like you may be.

[http://www.glaucoma.org/treatment/laser-iridotomy-and-narrow-angles.php](http://www.glaucoma.org/treatment/laser-iridotomy-and-narrow-angles.php)

Narrow angles may be a precursor to angle-closure glaucoma, the kind of glaucoma that can have a sudden, painful onset or a slow unrelenting downhill course.

The best time to prevent the damage that angle-closure glaucoma can cause is to treat it with a laser iridotomy **before** the actual disease sets in. This is preventative medicine at its best.
\===========================

[](http://www.glaucoma.org/assets_c/2011/02/iridot_po_antero_lat-thumb-290xauto-161.jpg)

**Video: Laser Iridotomy**

[http://www.glaucoma.org/treatment/video-laser-iridotomy.php](http://www.glaucoma.org/treatment/video-laser-iridotomy.php)

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-27T02:08:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153940>

Yes, exactly JanSz! That is the laser procedure that will be first in line and the shunt options are after the laser ceases to work. You are very lucky to do so well with narrow angles! What an inspiration you are in so many ways! I have been a participant at the best glaucoma forum on the internet for some time now - [http://198.170.234.66/discus/messages/39/39.html](http://198.170.234.66/discus/messages/39/39.html) - and the kind "danderson" (Dr. Anderson of Bascomb Pamer in Florida, the #1 rated eye institute in the world according to varied sources) has taken many opportunities to help me and others to understand things like this. He is the one holding the flashlight for glaucoma sufferers, like Jack is here. It's so nice that you also understand about these things. (I am sabretoothmom at the forum above.. that has long been my usual name. I still feel funny not using it here.. oh well.)

The problem with iridotomy is that it reduces 20/20 vision to something less than that, and/or causes other types of visual decline, so the timing of it should be carefullytimed so that you haven't lost any peripheral vision yet due to optic nerve damage from glaucoma, but so that you won't be too upset at hurting the vision you DO have because you will know something worse is now right around the corner. With narrow angles, the second they discovered the angles that procedure was warranted because if you do suffer sudden angle closure you will be irreversibly blind pretty much instantly. So that was right for you.

For me, I have 20/20 in one eye and 20/25 in the other and have only just been diagnosed. Most people can do well on drops, with open angle glaucoma, for many years without having to move onto the iridotomoies and/or shunts later. I don't want to be rushed into an iridotomy when I've only just had a few months so far to try drops. I realize it's challenging with me because the prostaglandins don't work anymore, beta blockers are contraindicated, and the first carbonic anhydrase inhibitor wasn't good for me either, but I don't want to rush into the procedure either since my sight IS still "perfect' and will not be afterwards.

So that's where the "terrified" comes from, because I realize the glaucoma specialist's flow chart says when the patient fails on two or more drug classes you offer iridotomy if their pressures are at or above 30, and I know mine are going to be when I go back to re-check them in about a week. I don't want him to try to get me to go that route yet.. it's just not right for me. I can feel it in my gut.

Hope that makes sense.

I'm not sure I'd heard of that before, very interesting, thank you for that! My goal is to stay on drops forever, or wean off them, but to never ever have any procedure done to my eyes. I have read and understand far too much now from the patients and doctor on the prevent blindness website to ever let myself be subjected to most of the surgical options. It's so strange, we still dont' know what causes glaucoma, we only know that in some people with high IOP their optic nerves become damaged.. but in some people with high IOP it doesn't become danaged.. and in some people with normal IOP it still becomes damaged... yet the only thing medicine focuses on is lowering IOP. Same with surgery. There is no guarantee it will lower IOP and often they do not, and usually even if they initially do it is temporary... yet they always 100% of the time damage some of the vision you DO have.

Which is why I do not want surgery while my vision is still 20/20 and my peripheral vision is still unaffected by the glaucoma.

But I do want my eyes not to hurt... so hopefully we can get this sorted out...

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-27T13:07:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153983>

Couldn't get my Azopt after all last night. Insurance doesn't cover it, and it's $350. There is a generic available and the pharmacist tried to call the eye doctor to ask if he could substitute it but the eye doctor was gone for the night. \*sigh\*

\--------

regarding Canaloplasty, because I thought that was intereting to find a glaucoma procedure I hadn't heard of yet - here is what Dr. Anderson had to say about it in 2008 -

> Posted on Wednesday, April 23, 2008 - 08:57 am at [http://198.170.234.66/discus/messages/39/35131.html](http://198.170.234.66/discus/messages/39/35131.html) :
> I don't think the answer is in on canaloplasty. It is a more difficult operation for the surgeon, but once you learn to do it, some have had good success. They write about it and advocate it. Others have not found it to be better than techniques they have been using.
>
> Less invasive than a trabeculectomy? Maybe a little, but the basic steps for giving anesthesia, cutting into the conjunctive to reach the scleral surface, making a flap of slcera to reach the inner structures of the eye, etc., are the same. It is really only the final step that is different. There may be less risk of infection later on. However, it may not lower the pressure as much as a trabeculectomy and it depends on how much lower the pressure needs to be in a particular patient. Also, anything that is more difficult for the surgeon (at least until he learns to do the delicate steps well) means there is more chance of something not going perfectly well.
>
> I think you should choose your surgeon, and let the surgeon decide on what method has worked best for him.
> \------------
> It would be an advantage not to have a bleb, and that is part of the interest in this new procedure. I think the jury is still out on how well it works, and it may be that it lowers the pressure sufficiently in some people or some types of glaucoma, but not others.

So, still far more invasive than laser, and equally invasive to trabs, but doesn't leave a bleb. So I will file the info away for much, much later years after I have exhausted all drops and drop combos, and after laser has been done the max of 3x per eye and cannot be done again. Options are good, I like options.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-27T16:50:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-153997>

"This medicine may cause a loss of potassium from your body. To help prevent this, your doctor may want you to eat or drink foods that have a high potassium content (for example, orange or other citrus fruit juices) or take a potassium supplement. It is very important to follow these directions. **Also, it is important not to change your diet on your own.** This is more important if you are already on a special diet (as for diabetes) or if you are taking a potassium supplement. Extra potassium may not be necessary and, in some cases, too much potassium could be harmful.

For diabetic patients:

-   This medicine may raise blood and urine sugar levels. While you are using this medicine, be especially careful in testing for sugar in your blood or urine. If you have any questions about this, check with your doctor.

Your doctor may want you to increase the amount of fluids you drink while you are taking this medicine. This is to prevent kidney stones. However, **do not increase the amount of fluids you drink without first checking with your doctor."**

**..... COME. ON.**

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-28T05:02:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154053>

Thank you for the suggestions, both of you.  Face in the snow sounds very rejuvenating! Seaweed too... I will see what I can come up with...

update:

It's been another long day. I finally got a generic eye drop and will try it tonight.

Today we also had to bring our13 year old little dog, Daisy, to the vet. She is diabetic (3 years now) and mostly blind and for the past few weeks has been getting fatter and fatter (she's a little dog on a strict diet because of diabetes so we knew something was wrong). She was constipated so the vet gave us advice and said to call back if it doesn't work. It seemed to be working because she was going, at least some... but still she was so fat. Today I felt her belly and told my husband right away that we have to get her into the vet. It was so bloated and felt like a balloon. He took her in and they did an xray and/or ultrasound,then came in and told him that there would be no charge. Our poor girl has a body full of tumor that is full of blood vessels... she has only as long to live as her body will tolerate the pressure.

This has been a hard year. My brother died in May unexpectedly of liver failure, I was diagnosed with glaucoma, hashi's, sent to an oncologist for possible lymph node cancer... losing my dog now too...someday I'll tell you all the stuff that I haven't yet and then this seems like nothing... believe it or not. Personally, I'd rather not believe it..

see you tomorrow.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-28T14:40:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154099>

Hmmm had my annual pap recently and got the online test results back. Positive for HPV MRNA E6/E7.

"E6, E7 mRNA Test is only positive if there is overexpression of E6, E7."
".. the overexpression of E6, E7 mRNA in a cell is the molecular switch leading to cervical cancer"

[http://incelldx.com/wp-content/uploads/400101\_01-EN-HPV-OncoTect-Brochure.pdf](http://incelldx.com/wp-content/uploads/400101_01-EN-HPV-OncoTect-Brochure.pdf)

"E6E7 mRNA are precursors to E6E7 oncoproteins, which are functional biological molecules and more directly relevant to disease progression."

"Data have shown that over-expression of E6 and E7 oncoproteins is a critical and necessary step toward HPV-related disease progression and cancer.16 Among their oncogenic functions, these oncoproteins inactivate tumor suppressor proteins. This disrupts the normal cell cycle and cell death while promoting cell immortality and proliferation. Continuous over-expression of E6 and E7 proteins causes proliferation of cervical carcinoma cells, and high-grade cervical disease and invasive cervical cancers have demonstrated high levels of E6 and E7."

[http://www.mlo-online.com/articles/201304/hpv-testing-and-cancer-will-e6-e7-change-the-paradigm.php](http://www.mlo-online.com/articles/201304/hpv-testing-and-cancer-will-e6-e7-change-the-paradigm.php)

neato. by which I mean, craptastic. \*eyeroll\*
\-------------
edited to add: I did just send out my 23andme kit so I will have more info like this before long, so now's a good time to keep it all in perspective and learn to continue to do so.  My morning always improves after my steak breakfast. Now just gotta cut down on the coffee.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-29T15:58:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154200>

So far so good with the generic eye drop. They gave me a generic for Truspot instead of Azopt but that's fine. I'm pleased thus far and have a bit more hope for successful drop treatment for the time being. I will return to my glaucoma specialist on the 3rd and we will re-check pressures. Hopefully this is working.

I have spent all morning.. about 3 hours now anyhow... researching. A friend of mine who has had glaucoma far longer than I, and who has tried pretty much every drop, surgery, and alternative treatment is going to be going overseas to try something new. He has had no help to identify, let alone help, the source of an odd visual defect that he experiences. The most experienced and highly regarded institues for glaucoma, and doctors, cannot explain to him what he is seeing and they tend to insinuate that it is all in his head.

I am forming theories but I suspect that we're looking at non-migraine retinal spreading depression issues, which incidentally can contribute to high IOP.... I can't articulate what my brain is working to put together right now though... but for my own sake I will put some links here that I need to further consider...

what my friend appears to visually perceive at times (according to me, based on his descriptions BUT he has just told me this is NOT what he sees so perhaps I'm entirely on the wrong track here.. but something still pulls me to this research...) :

basic information of what i think is going on - I bet he experiences "The spreading of a self-propagating wave of cellular [depolarization](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Depolarization) in the [cerebral cortex](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cerebral_cortex)." :
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cortical\_spreading\_depression](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cortical_spreading_depression)

can only see the abstract on this one due to lack of subscription  :
[http://www.nrcresearchpress.com/doi/abs/10.1139/y87-171#.VMoeBSwlm8A](http://www.nrcresearchpress.com/doi/abs/10.1139/y87-171#.VMoeBSwlm8A)

The guy from the youtube video above has his own blog and studies migraine etc and from this article alone I find myself wondering if he's a member here:
[http://www.scilogs.com/gray-matters...without-being-in-contact-to-an-ion-reservoir/](http://www.scilogs.com/gray-matters/never-seen-a-neuron-without-being-in-contact-to-an-ion-reservoir/)

time to cook steak, will add more links later so i can find them again easily when i'm' ready to revisit this interesting phenomenon...

> Confusion = opportunity to see something new as it cancels out certainty/dogma.
> That is how I see it.

I have no dogma nor certainty to cancel out. I'm just f\*cking confused. I have no background to worry about correcting or modifying. I never took any kind of biology, physics, or chemistry classes. I don't know the parts of a cell. I don't know how photosynthesis works. I don't know anything about anything. Which is great for learning when the method isn't college level right off the bat.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-30T18:21:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154280>

So I just sat at the computer and woke it up since I hadn't been on it in awhile, and as I was moving the mouse to wake it up I was thinking "I should go and say something apologetic about the moodiness ... I am suffering from a severe lack of oxy" (from being touch-phobic of my husband because when he's within about 22 feet of me his magnetics and mine just clash terribly and it sucks\*\*).

But anyway, the moment the computer woke up I saw an email from Groupon advertising "adult toys from Adam and Eve". LOL is the universe trying to tell me something??

\*\*(This was a tangible switch of magnetics, too. I was at physical therapy and they had put the tens unit thing on me and told me to adjust it however I wanted and left the room and I had no idea what was what so I cranked it up, and then when I left I went outside and said to myself out loud in the car something to the effect of "well everything just somehow flopped upside down.. this sucks" because I could FEEL that I had somehow just become opposite of everything I was, and where happy and positive had been before, moody and negative was afterwards.. and really, so many things changed... with my health and otherwise... all of the things in my health history come AFTER when my back went out and I had PT etc... but, yeah, that thing with my husband was one of the most noticeable things... sounds crazy but it's just so totally not.)

note to self -

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23768921](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23768921)

**"The role of inflammation in the pathogenesis of glaucoma.**

Glaucoma is an ocular disorder characterized by the progressive loss of retinal ganglion cells (RGC) and their axons. There are various hypotheses concerning the cause of RGC death. Previously, glaucoma was defined by high intraocular pressure (IOP); during the past decade, however, glaucoma specialists have acknowledged that elevated IOP is the most important risk factor for glaucoma, but does not define the disease. Other factors such as genetics, blood flow, and **excitotoxicity** are suggested as **potential causal factors for progressive RGC death observed in glaucoma**. We review recent studies elucidating a possible role of **low-grade inflammation** as a causal factor in the pathogenesis of glaucoma."
\-----------

[http://jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-3-autoimmunity-cancer-autism/](http://jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-3-autoimmunity-cancer-autism/)

"when calcium rises inside your cell membrane it is pumped out immediately because a rise intracellular calcium mediates apoptosis and autophagy and causes massive problems in intracellular signaling." \[this is called Calcium Efflux, yes?\] "**Rising intracellular calcium goes by another name you might have heard; it is called excitotoxicity.** Excitotoxicity is tied to heart disease, neuro-degeneration, atherosclerosis, sleep apnea, alterations of circadian cycles and autoimmune conditions. **Calcium efflux occurs due to direct alteration of voltage gated channels when an altered electromagnetic field is present.** The native EMF field of our planet is in the extreme low frequency range and when it is in this range, EMF waves act like waves and not particles. When the field is comprised of higher frequency waves from any source, like the sun, a Wifi router or a microwave, it begins to act more like a particle. See electromagnetic fields can act as waves or particles according to the natures laws defined in quantum electrodynamic theory. This is how the photoelectric effect works by nature’s laws. This one little detail has massive consequences for all of us. When the field changes from its normal wave characteristics to a higher energy, particle-based field, there are biologic consequences. What have been some found in experiments already done? We see a huge spike in depression, thyroid deficiency and the development of many immune system diseases. These diseases have been written about in many places in my blog, but I have yet to link things all together for you. Today we will do just that. "

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-31T14:43:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154361>

Good morning... random thoughts for this morning...

1\. it's best to feed hte cats before bed because if they have no dry cat food (defined as being able to see ANY portion of the bottom of their bowl through the food, apparently) then they will continue waking me up all night long to find out if I'm awake yet. Cats are smart asses.

2\. just realized I spent all yesterday reading what Jack has to say and then regurgitating it to a friend of mine from the glaucoma board who is trying to find some more last d itch efforts he can engage in while traditional "medicine" (pharmaceuticals salespeople) continues doing nothing to find the real cure for glaucoma, which can't be done because WE DON"T EVEN KNOW THE CAUSE YET!!

3\. found a blog post yesterday here where jack says something about hypothyroid people waking up to feel like the world is ending. is that why I wake up feeling positively possessed by an evil spirit and go around for hours spewing hatred and discontent pretty much every morning for the past number of years? Could that also be attributable in some way to my undiagnosed thyroid issue?

4\. I fail around 4pm daily. I wonder if this is partly a cortisol story. I have woken at 330am for as long as I can remember.. I began to notice it enough to actually talk about it happening about 6 years ago but I think it's been going on a lot longer. Around 3:30pm I turn into a complete and utter zombie, and even caffeine doesn't make me coherent again. By 8pm brain truly shuts off and I can't even help my daughter with middle school math. I wonder if my cortisol thinks 3:30am is dawn and 3:30pm is dusk? BTW, the reason I sai "I fail around 4pm daily" is because when brain and energy switch off, that's when cravings for sweets and carbs switches on. I haven't found anything to help with this yet. At some point between 4pm and 10pm, when I go to bed, I invariably end up snacking on carbs and/or sweets every single night. And since my brain has already gone ot autopilot I find myself trying to argue with myself and just eventually giving in because I will find nothing else worth snacking on when foraging. I really REALLY want to get those allergy test results back ASAP. I need to know what else I can eat safely. At least this time they specifically listed things to test me for, including oysters, clams, scallops, shrimp, lobster, cod, salmon, and dairy. I forget what else, there was another fish or two.

5\. they got my 23andme sample overnight and say it will now be 4-6 weeks processing. I hope it's faster because that will give me a lot to enjoy researching for a long time once it's in.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-31T16:23:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154371>

\*sigh\* a friend just confused the crap out of me.

I was saying that i'm upset at 23andme because they don't tell you until after they receive your sample that women can't have their paternal line analyzed, they'll only get maternal line results.

This upsets me because all of the health issues I'm concerned about come from my father's side. So I said I would have to get my son to give his sample for me to get a picture of my own paternal genetics, because this is what video 5 of 5 on 23andme says for female members to do.

And my friend said, why would that even matter for you, you don't get your father's Y chromosome anyway, you only get his X - cuz you're a female - so why would genetic issues related to his Y even be pertinent to you.

And then I said, &%(^)(&$(!! YOU HAVE CONFUSED ME.

and so here I am. Anyone able to explain this to me? I told him it matters because I got half my DNA from dad and half from mom and just because I have no Y I have nothing for them to test to reveal to me what that DNA from my dad contains, but it doens't mean it's not there. I just can't test it in myself, that's all.

Is this at all right?

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-01T21:01:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154471>

I wonder what's up with this - [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21981853](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21981853) .. it would be interesting to understand the science behind what is going on inside a person when this treatment is being undergone. [http://savir-center.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/06/English-ACS-patient-brochure.pdf](http://savir-center.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/06/English-ACS-patient-brochure.pdf)

[http://savir-center.com/the-science-of-vision-restoration/](http://savir-center.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/06/English-ACS-patient-brochure.pdf)

"Our innovative vision restoration treatments can enhance visual functions even after many years because almost all patients have some degree of vision left, called “residual vision”. Vision is not a black-and-white matter (seeing versus not seeing), but one of shades of vision, or different levels of partial vision, which need to be identified and then strengthened.

Research in the Sabel laboratory over decades has shown that the brain can, in fact, adapt rather well to vision loss when stimulated properly through a process called “neuroplasticity”. "

"Damage of visual structures is usually not complete, some parts of structures survives it, such as the above mentioned areas of residual vision at the border of scotoma, but not only them. There are some more areas that have a potential for the vision restoration. These are:

-   “islands of residual vision” inside the blind field
-   Alternate there is one central vision pathway, that begins at the eye, follows the optic nerve and goes to the occipital lobe, the part of the brain that is responsible for vision. But this pathway is not the only one, there are other pathways, that connect the eye and different parts of the brain. These ones are called alternative pathways or “extra striate” pathways. visual pathways unaffected by the damage.

Noninvasive current therapy influenced the above mentioned areas as it can provoke vision-like percepts, also known as [phosphenes](http://savir-center.com/research/science.php#), which trigger activity in the brain (kind of like a heart pace maker) which then leads to excitability changes in visual cortex and other brain structures. This sensitivity change improves visual functions and can explain the therapeutic effects of the treatment."

hmmmm I wonder how legit this is...?

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-04T00:09:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154654>

My pressures were 22 and 22 today. That's not great but for me, my doctor said he'll take it. I agree.

The funny thing is, I kept using the old medicine in one eye and used the new medicine in the other eye for a test run and the way things worked out, he said that it would appear that neither one was having any effect at first and then said well.. maybe they're both equally effective, put it that way, and said I can choose which drop to use between now and my next appointment in May.

I don't want to go back and write a whole long post here like I just did in an email and don't want to copy and paste it here either but bottom line is, I didn't tell the doctor but I think that in reality neither drop is working anymore BUT I think my pressures are down, however minimally - but satisfactorily for the time being - because of what I'm doing with my diet, etc because of what I've learned here.

I'm going to keep being compliant with drops obviously, but I'm also going to keep on doing what I"ve been doing here and bumping it up one notch at a time and when I go back in May I'm interested to see what happens. Very. My doctor is relatively young and from a prestigious medical establishment prior to being here, and I would love to blow him away in May and then tell him what I did to make it happen and get him on board with Jack's methods. In my imagination, at least, that would lead to him going back and giving some exciting talks to his colleagues about these promising new developments.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-04T22:06:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154738>

Daisy-dog is still hanging in there. She's not wanting her diabetic Rx dog food so we're cooking her organic grass fed ground beef, which keeps her eating which keeps her happy. It is one of her greatest joys. I was thinking about last summer, how she would go up on the hill with me in our backyard (we have 5+ acres of pine forest that starts just beyond the hill area) and she would help me pick the ripe dewberries (they're like a blackberry but on low prickley brambles) that grow up there naturally... she would bite them off the vines and eat them and I would tell ehr that her eyes would get worse from it (she has cataracts from her diabetes). We picked so many cool-whip sized containers of dewberries.. probably at least 8 of them, which are largely in the freezer now. Next year I will be more mindful of eating them ripe off the vines, but I will miss Daisy following me up there and me having to lead her back down by clapping and talking to her (because she's so blind). My garden didn't do very well last year.. every time I put my vegetables in the ground they die up there, so this tie I will leave them in the huge tubs that I had them in this year at first, before putting them up there. I had a gut feeling that I should put them in giant containers, which is hte only reason we bought so much soil, but when we transplanted them up into the garden area (in name only because everything dies up there despite having the best sun) we did put the soil there too and mix it in so now it's really deep and lush up there.. maybe this year will be the year. But I think I'll plant both places just in case.

But either way I have a backup.. out little town has a free Community Organic Garden where you can go to pick free organic veggies on your way home each day if you want to. That's where we've gotten ou tomatoes and green beans for years now but so many other things are usually picked before they're even ripe, so I'll still do my own at home...

well I'm rambling.. killing time before getting my daughter off hte bus in a m inute, while my bone broth boils away... it smells SO good.. I always put a few cloves of garlic in there and periodically switch them out to keep the flavor strong... I love onions and garlic... I can't afford the grassfed beef (except groudn beef) but they had organic grassfed free-range chicken drumsticks for $1.99 a pound so I got a couple of pounds of those and have them boiling away with some himalayan pink sea salt for flavor and ACV to soften the bones more... already pulle the meat from the drumsticks and then put the bones back in after breaking the ones that wer soft enough...

my new allergy test results are in, but when i logged into the online portal it said "see scanned copy" which I don't have and my doctor doesn't have it yet either so... still waiting...

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-05T13:09:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154800>

yesterday I finally called back the compounding pharmacists and said no iodine until I see the endocrinologist in April, but please do get me the prescription for progesterone lozenges (he faxes his recommendations to my gyn who faxes back the Rx so he can fill it). I asked whether I had remembered right that he said only take htem on days 17 and 18 and he said no start them at that time and then continue for about 2 weeks until you get your period again. (I was thinking if I start on day 18 that's only 10 days ti my next period because I'm regular like that, but didn't bother saying it).

But I took so long to call him that I realized after I got off the phone that today is already day 19 or 20. So now I have another complete cycle to wait since the prescription isn't even there yet, let alone compounded and ready to pick up. No point in screwing my whole body up by being impatient and starting them the second I get them I guess. But what do I know.

Edit: then again I"m randomly spotting today outta nowhere.. probably because my daughter has her period and my body is probably trying to cycle in tune with hers. fun.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-05T23:21:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154855>

my girl's on the way to the vet to either be put down the rest of the way (she just had a seizure or something, it was horrible) or else to have her remains tended to somehow, but one way or another that's it. Miss Daisy Mae Diabeetus-dog B. is not coming back home. She was born July 24, 2001 in an Amish family's home. We got her through a friend of that family, who had electricity and therefor a telephone and so facilitated the adoption of these people's puppies. We adopted her September 21 or thereabouts of the same year, shortly after the attacks on the World Trade Center... we only had two children at the time (my daughter came 18 months later - shes never known life without Daisy..), and a cat (Pretty Miss Kitty) who we had adopted as a stray hanging around our house... so she's 13 1/2... and has been diabetic for 3 years now, getting two shots of insulin per day, along with an anti-pee pill at each shot time/meal-time (consisting of prescription dog food) and lately, 1/4 tsp of powdered laxative per feeding.... we've tried to take good care of her.. being sure not to re-arrange the house or anything now that she's blind... today we both got dressed to go out and get work/errands done but after he left I saw that she was really unwell and from that moment on I laid with her for 3 hours until he could get back home (I called him and said he had to come back but he snow plows and we're in NY...)... then he laid with her for another hour until she just had the seizure/convulsions... horrible.. so horrible....

glad it's over now.... wish he didn't have to go from here with her... but relieved that i don't have to... the groudn is cold and frozen now... i don't know what we'll do with her... i thought maybe cremation... i hate this.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-07T01:24:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154943>

You know, sometimes such basic things are made so complex and get so over-thought that you don't see the obviousness right in front of your face.

I'm one of those people who jumped into the CT technology and whatnot right in the middle, and in doing so ended up scrutinizing little tiny bits and pieces and ignoring the super simple cliff notes version:

when you hit your thumb with a hammer, and it gets swollen, what do you do right away?

you put ice on it.

why?

because the cold brings down the swelling.

swelling is inflammation.

cold stops inflammation.

duh.

I should've just thought about it on a basic, logical level instead of thinking about the science and technical terms and stuff.

that was just so easy it's not even funny. I can convince anyone of this with that logic. "you have chronic inflammation? you need to get cold. why? because cold brings down swelling, what, did you never drop a can of soup on your toe? okay then."

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-07T16:07:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154964>

So the answer to the "guess my seafood allergy test results" is ...

they say I am not allergic to anything they tested. No reaction whatsoever to any of them. Yet my serum IgE was elevated so we STILL don't know what I'm allergic to.

So I can presume it was a flawed test and continue eating no seafood at all of any kind, or I can assume it was correct (which is obviously more likely) and just start eating whatever I want.

which brings me to another point of contention.

Seafood? EWWWWWW I don't want to put stanky weird-textured crap into my mouth! oh well. one thing at a time.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-07T16:16:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154965>

[http://www.glaucoma.org/glaucoma/glaucoma-and-the-brain.php](http://www.glaucoma.org/glaucoma/glaucoma-and-the-brain.php)
**"Researchers now view glaucoma as a disease of the brain — a neurodegenerative disease** — rather than simply an eye disease. Recent research has shown that the complex connection between the eye and the brain is an important key to the disease.

Glaucoma shares a number of features with degenerative brain diseases such as Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, and Lou Gehrig’s disease. In all of these diseases, age and family history are significant risk factors, and specific areas of the brain are damaged over time. In glaucoma, the only difference is that the “specific area of the brain” affected is the eye and optic nerve!"

Neat, it's a brain disease. Now if I only knew of a doctor who works on brains..

"Indeed the eye’s retina and optic nerve are a part of the brain: during early development, a small part of the brain pouches out and becomes the retina and optic nerve. **Inside the eye, a group of neurons called retinal ganglion cells collect all of the visual information and pass it down their extensions, called axons, through the optic nerve and back to the rest of the brain**. The ganglion cell, which collects all the vision information from the other retinal cells, is the one type of cell that is initially damaged by glaucoma.

The optic nerve continues to be a major focus for researching the underlying causes of glaucoma. Whether due to mechanical trauma, decreased blood flow, or other causes, optic nerve axon injury causes changes in retinal ganglion cells, eventually causing cell death."

"
Because the retinal ganglion cell axon stretches from the retina through the optic nerve to the brain, its surrounding cells also become damaged by glaucoma. Within the retina, other cells, such as amacrine cells, degenerate and rewire their connections after retinal ganglion cells are lost.

**There are also changes in the brain within the lateral geniculate nucleus (the main brain target of optic nerve axons), and even the visual cortex, which is the part of the brain that helps us interpret visual information.**

Thus, in addition to treatments directed at lowering eye pressure, still the mainstay of glaucoma therapy, **there may be opportunities to develop treatments directed at the retina and the brain. Some promising treatments that promote nerve health, called neurotrophic factors, could help at multiple places in the visual pathway.**

**For example, neurotrophic factors such as ciliary neurotrophic factor (CNTF) may keep retinal ganglion cells from dying, a process called _neuroprotection_; they may increase axon regrowth down the optic nerve, called regeneration; and they may improve the support between the dying retinal ganglion cells and their surrounding cells in the retina and brain, called _neuroenhancement_.**

The understanding that one key to glaucoma is in the connections within the retina and to the brain has led to exciting advances in research that may well lead to new potential treatments."

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-07T16:26:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154966>

[http://www.glaucoma.org/research/catalyst-for-a-cure-glaucoma-and-the-brain.php](http://www.glaucoma.org/research/catalyst-for-a-cure-glaucoma-and-the-brain.php)

"And what that experiment did, from the Calkins laboratory, was to really ask, that process of the ganglion cells, as it actually connects in the brain, how does that process behave? **So it's simply asking the reverse.** It's labeling the cell in the retina and asking how it interacts, does it functionally interact, in the brain." (reminds me of MC2 = E, and so on...)

"The visual system isn't only the eye. **It involves the optic pathways and the brain, and these data really point us in a new direction. It actually increases the workload, in some sense, because we have to now consider the brain, and I don't think that has been adequately considered in this disease.**"

"The point of this study is that it shows that early in the disease, that not only are cells getting sick in the retina, but the ganglion cell, its process, **this cable that goes all the way back to the brain, begins to lose its connectivity back in the brain early in the disease. So we think about this pathway, we have to treat… we can’t treat the eye to treat glaucoma, we have to treat the entire pathway.** It’s a very important concept."

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-07T16:29:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154967>

"The CFC researchers have obtained a detailed understanding of this complex disease, and have revealed novel approaches for slowing disease progression. **Importantly, they characterized glaucoma as a progressive, neurodegenerative disease**, and provided significant evidence that targeting early events has the greatest therapeutic potential. **They showed that RGCs undergo functional decline and genetic deprogramming before they are permanently lost, and they defined a window of “vulnerability” for RGCs during which there is the potential for rescue.**"

[http://www.glaucoma.org/research/catalyst-for-a-cure.php](http://www.glaucoma.org/research/catalyst-for-a-cure.php)

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-07T16:40:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-154970>

some current research involves this idea:

"Currently, we know that glia become activated in glaucoma, but we do not know by what process. **More and more evidence from other parts of the brain suggest that a molecule called ATP is part of the signaling mechanism where damaged neurons tell the glia that they are in distress.** We have preliminary evidence that this is also true in the retina after damage to the optic nerve. This proposal is aimed at determining if dying neurons in the retina (ganglion cells) signal to the retinal glia by releasing ATP through specialized channels made up of a protein called **Pannexin1**. An increased understanding of how the neurons and glia communicate in glaucoma should lead to new developments on how to "tame" the glia into a non-pathologic role, thus increasing the potential for greater preservation of sight."

[http://www.glaucoma.org/research/2014-research-grants.php](http://www.glaucoma.org/research/2014-research-grants.php)

"**A recent finding in our laboratory led to the novel hypothesis that immune system events involving lymphocytes are necessary for RGC cell death and optic nerve axon loss in glaucoma**. This project will determine whether specific populations of lymphocytes are required for RGC death in glaucoma. Based on findings from this study, therapies might eventually be designed to modulate the immune system in order to prevent vision loss and blindness in glaucoma patients."

Interesting, we already know because of my lymph nodes in my neck that I have some lymph system/immune system issues going on, evidenced further by my anti-TPO antibodies being ramped up... as well as my serum IgE despite not being able to find any allergens in my diet or environment with testing...

"We found that RGCs are the sole retinal neuron that express **TRPV4, a pressure-sensitive channels that is permeable to calcium ions**. This is intriguing because **calcium is known to induce remodeling of cells and RGC degeneration in glaucoma**. We will use molecular, histological, calcium imaging and electrophysiological methods to characterize the sensitivity of these channels to membrane stretch by mimicking the effect of IOP on RGC perikarya and axons. Second, we will characterize the effect of stretch on cells isolated from mouse glaucoma models. Third, we will study the effect of small molecule antagonist drugs to prevent stretch-mediated RGC degeneration and death. The study is strongly supported by preliminary evidence obtained in vitro and in vivo."

Well, step 3 was the step that included the wait time because of other samples...

**Step 3: Sample received at the lab**

You will receive an email when your sample is received at the lab. Once a sample is received, it enters the queue for processing.

This step includes the time a sample spends in the queue at the lab, as well as the visual inspection of your sample, DNA extraction, and amplification of DNA from your sample.

\-----------------------------

I'm already on step 4 which is active analysis, and we're four days in. I was just wondering how long the actual analysis step takes, that's all.

**Step 4: DNA analysis**

Analysis refers to the process of [genotyping](https://customercare.23andme.com/entries/21262606-What-is-the-difference-between-genotyping-and-sequencing-) on our custom microarray chip.

This step also includes [quality review](https://customercare.23andme.com/entries/21864859-Quality-Review), where the data returned from a set of samples is reviewed to ensure that it meets our standards for call rate and accuracy.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-08T19:45:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-155052>

something weird i just noticed... i don't ever weigh myself, so i don't know how my digital scale compares to the one at the doctor's office but i was a little over 130 within the past several weeks at the doctor. I was 127.8 yesterday morning, I'm 124.8 this morning/right now. Wonder what's up with that? All I did was swap out any other breakfast items for a steak daily, and the other switches shouldn't by conventional wisdom make a difference, because BY CONVENTIONAL WISDOM/dogma, it was a calorie for calorie swap. I traded at least 2-3 cans of diet soda per day for 2-3 bottles of spring water, and I switched sweet 'n' low for stevia. Calorie for calorie, no change at all.. yet weight seems to be falling off when I've not really done much else but replace toast with margarine and greek yogurt for breakast daily with a fat steak, aside from those 2 things.

Pretty interesting IMO.

Oh, I also stopped taking 2 ibuprofen every morning for my big toe arthritis, and my whole digestive system reminds me of being a kid again! Then I took 2 yesterday because the pain gets pretty bad sometimes, and this morning my stomach is gorked up again. So, for a LONG time - years- my taking 2 ibuprofen daily has probably been screwing up my gut. So glad that just a couple of weeks off it already has me showing such an obvious improvement, even with removing the greek yogurt which I \*had\* added in just because of the digestive stuff!

(edit, and it's my TOM so I should be bloated but feel pretty normal in that regard.. crazy.)

like:
"CYP2C19 Intermediate Metabolizer. Your body breaks down some medicines at a slightly slower than normal rate (which is represented by gs150). Individuals with gs152 genotypes have even slower metabolism. \*anti-epileptics (such as diazepam, phenytoin, and phenobarbitone) \*anti-depressants (such as amitriptyline and clomipramine) \*anti-platelet drug clopidogrel (Plavix) \*anti-ulcer proton pump inhibitors like omeprazole (trade names Losec and Prilosec), esomeprazole (trade name Nexium), and lansoprazole (Prevacid) \*hormones (estrogen, progesterone)."

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-10T19:22:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-155252>

and

**s4244285** is a SNP in the [CYP2C19](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/CYP2C19) gene, potentially encoding the CYP2C19\*2 variant. This variant is the most common reason for poor metabolism of compounds like mephenytoin (an anti-convulsant), some antidepressants, the anti-platelet drug [Plavix](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Plavix), and some drugs used for [ulcer](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Ulcer) conditions of various types. \[[PMID 8195181](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8195181?dopt=Abstract)\]
The risk allele is **rs4244285**(A).

As a nonfunctioning [CYP2C19](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/CYP2C19), this variant would be expected to be a poor metabolizer of several commonly prescribed drugs, including anti-[ulcer](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Ulcer) drugs like [omeprazole](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Omeprazole) (trade names [Losec](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Losec) and [Prilosec](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Prilosec)), [esomeprazole](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Esomeprazole) (trade name [Nexium](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Nexium)), and [lansoprazole](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Lansoprazole) (trade name [Prevacid](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Prevacid)).

In Caucasians, SNPs in [CYP2C19](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/CYP2C19) are relatively rare (in contrast to SNPs in [CYP2D6](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/CYP2D6)), but SNPs in this gene are common in Asians. [Ulcer](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Ulcer) treatment with [omeprazole](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Omeprazole) to reduce Helicobacter pylori has been shown to vary depending on a patient's [CYP2C19](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/CYP2C19) genotype, varying from 28% in patients homozygous for [CYP2C19](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/CYP2C19) alleles encoding fully functional proteins to 100% in patients with variations leading to poor metabolism. The fact that poor metabolizers for many cytochrome p450s achieve higher therapeutic success for some drugs is speculated to be because for some of the drug being broken down (ie metabolized) slower, the effective concentrations are both higher and longer lasting. \[[PMID 9867757](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9867757?dopt=Abstract)\]

However, other drugs clearly work less well in carriers of reduced function CYP2C19 alleles. An example of such a drug is [clopidogrel](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Clopidogrel), sold under the brand name [Plavix](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Plavix). This has now (2010) been acknowledged by the FDA, who have added a boxed warning to Plavix, alerting patients and health care professionals that the drug can be less effective in people who have CYP2C19 variants and cannot convert the drug as effectively to its active form

(much more here - [http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Rs4244285](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Rs4244285) )

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-11T15:39:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-155359>

oh dear. I was right about so many things in my past, not the least of which I cannot process just about any drugs. I have many, many SNPs that state that I completely lack enzymes for drug processing. Remarkable, really. LIke, I have:

\- little to no CYP2D6 (edit - were gonna go with "none" because I have TWO SNPs that each encode for this and the second one states that "If two copies of this (or similar) changes are inherited, poor metabolism ('PM') of debrisoquine is observed. Many other drugs are typically first metabolized by CYP2D6 including dextromorphan, sparteine, metoprolol, nortriptyline and many other antidepressants and codeine."

\- completely Nonfunctional CYP3A5 - "drug metabolism effects; common Two copies of the nonexpressing CYP3A5\*3 allele. Impacts metabolism of tacrolimus, an immunosuppressive drug used in organ transplantation.

\- little to no functional CYP2C19

\- C3435T is a SNP located in the ABCB1 gene which is partly non-functional, leading to slower drug metabolism of certain drugs

\- One copy of CYP2A6\*5 non-functioning variant, which gives me half the processing ability for certain other drugs including nicotine

\- CYP2C19 is nonfuntional - "this variant would be expected to be a poor metabolizer of several commonly prescribed drugs, including anti-ulcer drugs like omeprazole (trade names Losec and Prilosec), esomeprazole (trade name Nexium), and lansoprazole (trade name Prevacid)"

\- several/many SNPs give the warning that antidepressants will be 7x less effective in me, and each one lists anotehr class of dugs I cant take, totalling all SSRIs and TCAs (explains why I couldnt tolerate wellbutrin, paxil, effexor, imipramine, etc etc..)

well there's more but yeah. it's neat to find the stuff to back up what i've been saing all along. i probably put "drug hpersensitivity" in my opening post. Of course my doctors think that I read patient pamphlets and go all hypochondriac, but this proves that they can all go to heck and do certain naughty things to themselves while there.

EDIT: ON FURTHER PERUSAL I SEE THAT ALMOST ALL OF THESE SAY THEY'RE AT "CYTOCHROME P450" - NO IDEA IF THIS IS IMPORTANT BUT WILL NOTE IT HERE IN CASE I EVER COME BACK TO DO MORE RESEARCH ON THIS

### Hope2Learn — 2015-02-22T17:00:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-156284>

that does make me feel at least a little less worried. it's just that she called me friday to apologize for sending me a letter saying my pap was fine, and saying she had just seen the results that showed i testd positive for that one kind of EBV that is high risk for cevical cancer. so i told her about my dna test results agreeing. she said all we can do is retest in one year instead of two, and to eat plenty of dark green and purple leafy vegetables and when i told her about the dna testing she also said i sound like a more "progressive" patient and there's only so much she can say as an allopathic doctor because they must operate under the FDA umbrella but that naturopathic remedies have been around for 3500 years and they work adn i might, as this type of patient, want to see about getting one of those doctors. i said i worry about cost and shes said yeah but.. it's your health. then i told her about my period starting on CD20 and she said to keep her informed of this kind of thing. that was before it just started AGAIN on feb 20... so i shot her an email,, but she warned me that those take a long time (weeks) to get to her because of going through so many people first. but whatever, nothing i can do.. she's away this week anyhow.

meanwhile the pharmacy says because she IS away this week and they diddn't get any rxs yet for me, they have nothing to fill, so it's hurry up and wait for my THIRD CD14 since trying to get these darn PG lozenges/trioches!!

do not get stressed by your particular genetic lotery. It is not your fate, but rTher a base to work with. your irregular cycle may mean many things and in between them your state of mind. When I found JK blog and started to introduce the changes. i got obcessed and my healht got worse at first. I think I stressed myself too much. i disagree that ignorance is a bless, but sometimes a lighter version is a big truth, you have to filter what comes to you and as a number one cultivate your soul and peace of mind. just recently I realized that behind every obcession is a big empty hole. When you remove the obcession there is nothing but emptiness and boredom, because the obcession was all your "food" and everything you invested in. It is particulary easy to get obsessed if you are determined person and you have a strong motivation to fight (for example for your health). Maybe ia m completely wrong, I just wanted to say it here and maybe you may get any use of it.... Hugs.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-05T15:39:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-157513>

Thanks Kovita.  \*hugs\*.... so true about obsessing about something and then removing it and finding an empty hole there. That's why I always have something I'm working on or obsessing about, BUT I try not to let it be about me whenever possible. I do genealogy research, I'm very into music, etc. and try to spend as much time (or more) doing things that are NOT about me, rather than constantly thinking about and focusing on my own health.

Here's a question for you and anyone else who may be reading this (and I don't know if you might know the answer or not)... now that I have these progesterone troches, and I am supposed to take/use one of them twice daily, I wonder if there is any optimum times to be using them. Obviously it is best to try to use them 12 hous apart but is using one upon waking better than waiting a few hours, or worse, for example... anyone know? I almost think that using one about half an hour after waking has helped me to wake up more quickly and to be in a slightly better mood, but I'm not sure just yet. Thanks!

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-09T14:22:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-157975>

ultrasound scheduled for 10am tomorrow. 26 hours notice isn't bad at all.

but they said to drink a quart / 32 ounces of clear fluid at least an HOUR before the ultrasound which they said will last up to an hour. heh. yeahright. The hour that goes by without me having to pee will be hte hour I sprout wings and fly, ESPECIALLY if I have to drink four 8-oz glasses of water all at once AND THEN HOLD IT FOR UP TO 2 HOURS.

hehe they better just plan on putting a bucket under the table cuz somebody's gonna leak. LMAO. hey it is what it is. maybe they'll realize how serious I"ve always been about figuring out the excessive urgent pee problem!

(ps - it's safe to say the progesterone is having some effect on my body at this point for sure, because the hot flashes/sweating in bed happened again last night. Also yesterday morning for hours after taking the morning dose I felt overheated... I think instead of taking them at 730 and 730, I will take them at 6 and 6. Maybe then I can avoid the sleep-sweating.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-11T15:36:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-158202>

Hot flashes at night continue. This morning I'm being hit with them as well. Definitely because of the progesterone. I'll assume this is a good sign since normally I'm always cold. Yesterday I took my coat off in the car even though the car thermometer said it was "only" 42 out. Felt warm to me but probably because that's a full 60 degees warmer than it has been the past 6-8 weeks here in upstate NY.

Also, I had stopped taking a multivitamin for a few weeks then restarted and now I can definitively state that they stop my cravings in the middle of the day, regulate my digestion, fix the digestive issue that ibuprofen causes (don't wanna go with TMI right now), and kickstart weight loss. Last time I took them for a few weeks I found myself here posting a thread asking why I was losing weight. Right after that I stopped taking it and tried to continue the new diet and instantly failed.

Definitely gonna stay on the women's centrum each morning along with the vitamin d3 from now on because for me the craving-killing nature of it combined with the digestion-facilitating nature absolutely positively causes a double-positive-whammy in my fight to lose winter weight (okay like 5 years' worth).

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-12T13:28:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nowhere-to-go-but-up.12501/post-158344>

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=158343)
