### Josh — 2015-01-15T05:11:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152802>

I was trying to explain the webinar to my partner. When I explained that a nanosecond=1 billionth of a second which means there are a billion of them in a second and that an error of this size in the atomic clock of a GPS satellite could cause a 1 foot error on Earth, she started to see how even the few feet between the organs and SCN or the altitude that the body lives at could have very real effects in a body where distances are much smaller than between the Earth and an orbiting satellite. It seems to me that we are working with micrometer and nanometer distances between cells and organelles such as mitochondria reckoned against the feet/up to a meter distances between the SCN and the organs. The ratios seem similar to the surface of the Earth and the orbiting GPS satellites.

Let us say 400 miles above the Earth=2,112,000 feet

1 nanosecond error at sea level = approximately 1 foot/30cm error

ratio=1/2,112,000

Let us say 1 meter from adrenal gland to SCN=1,000,000 micrometers

Average diameter of a mitochondria=0.75 micrometer

ratio=1/1,333,333

This is rough to demonstrate that the effects may well be of similar magnitude

### Da-mo — 2015-01-15T05:15:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152803>

Following the introduction of altitude affected time variation . . . lots of questions . . .

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitational\_time\_dilation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitational_time_dilation)

Add to this that the Earth is 3 million miles closer (3%) to the sun in January than in July due to elliptical orbit.

[http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT\_DAY/elliptical.htm](http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT_DAY/elliptical.htm)

> The time for the Earth to turn this small angle is about four minutes. This little difference would cause no concern if it were always the same, but it is not! Recalling that the Earth moves in an elliptical path (much exaggerated in Fig. 1) around the Sun, rather than a circular path, it turns out that the Earth is nearer to the Sun in January than in July. The difference is about three million miles (out of an average distance of ninety-three million miles).

[http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT\_DAY/elliptical.htm](http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT_DAY/elliptical.htm)

Remember that while this difference may seem small, the intensity of all of the fields suspected of causing time dilation follow an inverse square law with distance.

>
> The divergence of a [vector field](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vector_field) which is the resultant of radial inverse-square law fields with respect to one or more sources is everywhere proportional to the strength of the local sources, and hence zero outside sources. [Newton's law of universal gravitation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_law_of_universal_gravitation) follows an inverse-square law, as do the effects of[electric](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electricity), [magnetic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetism), [light](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light), [sound](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sound), and [radiation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radiation) phenomena.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inverse-square\_law](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inverse-square_law)

Not only does our altitude with respect to the sun change over the year (with possible gravitational time dilation) but our speed through space changes as well.

> The speed of the Earth in its orbit increases as it gets nearer to the Sun. Since the Earth is closest to the Sun in January and furthest in July, it follows that the Earth is moving more rapidly in its orbit in January than in July!

[http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT\_DAY/elliptical.htm](http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT_DAY/elliptical.htm)

So what are the effects on quantum time of a change in velocity when moving through:

-   gravitational fields?
-   magnetic fields?
-   relative to incoming light?

As the Earth moves closer to the sun, even though its line of travel is tangential to incident light:

-   would this add/subtract to the angle of incidence of electromagnetic radiation - must the same way as apparent wind direction is affected by the speed of a sailboat?
-   would this also cause a slight redshift or blueshift depending whether out altitude was increasing or decreasing?
-   would the angular momentum of the spin of the planet be additive to decreasing solar altitude in the morning and subtractive from decreasing solar altitude in the afternoon . . . and conversely be subtractive to increasing solar altitude in the morning and additive to increasing solar altitude in the afternoon?
-   if all of the above could affect redshift and blueshift - what would be the effects on quantum timing?

Finally . . . the elliptical orbit (change in solar altitude and observed size of the sun), axial tilt, acceleration and deceleration of orbital speed imply that solar radiation on Earth is more intense and longer in duration during summer (and less intense/shorter duration during winter) in the Southern hemisphere than the Northern hemisphere.

-   Could we therefore also extrapolate that the Southern hemisphere experiences more seasonal time dilation compared with the Northern?
-   Could we then take advantage of these time dilation effects by seasonally changing location between hemispheres?
-   Do seasonally migratory animals take advantage of these time dilation effects to re-calibrate their quantum clock speeds and increase longevity?

Dr. K has often said that zip code is very important . . . .

-   does the ideal location change with the seasons due to quantum time variation?
-   has evolution selected for migratory patterns where this aspect is a factor?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-01-15T05:25:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152807>

Light has a universal speed limit at 186,000 miles an hour. Light travels 30 centimeters in one nanosecond. The only way to increase its energy is to increase its frequency. If timing is off distant signals will also be awry. This very same feature is why GPS clocks orbiting the Earth must run faster than clocks on the ground to navigate properly. They are 38 microseconds faster. In 38 microseconds at the speed of light, if this clock difference did not exist, GPS devices would be off by over ten kilometers a day. When this happens in our brains’ mitochondria the inaccurate timing results in things like neuro-degeneration, T2D, and autoimmunity. Add to this that the Earth is 3 million miles closer (3%) to the sun in January than in July due to elliptical orbit.

So what are the effects on quantum time of a change in velocity when moving through:

gravitational fields?

magnetic fields?

relative to incoming light?

These are the things mitochondria and water pay deep attention to so you don’t have too.

It is also why ancestral health, paleo, and medicine are so off track; because it is not food that matters most but timing, especially circadian timing and meal timing that do..........inconvenient truth for sure...........but the truth is not diminished by the number of people who believe it.

### Josh — 2015-01-15T06:01:23+0200

It struck me as I was falling asleep last night that while we had covered that pregnenolone and all hormones start with production on the inner mitochondrial membrane, I really had no idea how this goes. Looking at it, I notice the P450SCC enzyme that must rely on proton tunneling that is dependent on distances, mass equivalence and such. Regulation of these would probably require a good clock as even small time irregularities would change the distances, proton tunneling and synthesis rates. I realize that sensing of seasonal changes and the adjustment of hormone levels for breeding at optimal time and in optimal conditions might indeed be more or less dependent upon this as well. Extrapolate this out to girls menstruating at 10 and under and so on...... Perhaps their blue light saturated bodies have an SCN that has essentially told them that they are already 15 due to changes in clock speed....

### Josh — 2015-01-15T06:17:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152813>

I realize that we have focused on the electron transport chain as if it is the only thing happening in a mitochondria. I am trying to fit it together with Pregnenolone synthesis to create a functioning image to play with. So far I have not found the right one. I am discovering that many more things happen in a mitochondria besides energy production through the electron transport chain such as: GABA synthesis, heme synthesis and more. I found an article on diurnal function in mitochondria suggesting that things are timed shall we say...

[http://www.mcponline.org/content/9/10/2125.full.pdf+html](http://www.mcponline.org/content/9/10/2125.full.pdf+html)

Fig. 5.
Metabolism scheme illustrating enzymes in mitochondrial primary metabolism with altered abundance or activity during diurnal cycle. A dark-enhanced phase (1–8 h; _red_), a midnight to early morning phase (10–14 h;_orange_), a day-enhanced phase (14–20 h; _green_), and a late-day phase (18–24 h; _purple_) are shown. One enzyme changed in dark-to-light and light-to-dark transitions (_blue_). _BCAT_, branched-chain amino acid aminotransferase; _E-CoAH_, enoyl-CoA hydratase; _FUM_, fumarase; _GABA-T_, GABA transaminase; _S-CoA-L_, succinyl-CoA ligase; _IVD_, isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase; _SSA_, succinic semialdehyde; _ETF_, electron transfer flavoprotein; _MDH_, malate dehydrogenase; _SDH_, succinate dehydrogenase; _2-OGDH_, 2-oxoglutarate dehydrogenase.

### Josh — 2015-01-15T17:53:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152880>

I also noticed that sulfated pregnenolone is a big deal in the brain. I honestly had not even noticed that there was a sulfated form. Contemplating the importance of sulfur combined with hormones in light of all the new things we have learned about shall we say the "kingdom of sulfur"......

Need to look through this, part of a pretty good sex hormone review from someone who still knows precious little about such things free on our beloved internet:

[http://www.intechopen.com/books/ste...e/the-biological-roles-of-steroid-sulfonation](http://www.intechopen.com/books/steroids-from-physiology-to-clinical-medicine/the-biological-roles-of-steroid-sulfonation)

**FIGURE 1.**
Sulfate levels need to be maintained for sulfonation reactions to function effectively. _(A)_ In the kidneys, filtered sulfate is reabsorbed through epithelial cells in the proximal tubule via NaS1 on the apical membrane and then by SAT1 on the basolateral membrane. _(B)_ Intracellular sulfate is obtained from extracellular sources via sulfate transporters, and is derived from the metabolism of methionine and cysteine. Sulfate and ATP are converted to the universal sulfonate donor, PAPS. Both (_1_) sulfonation and (_2_) de-sulfonation reactions are active within intracellular metabolism. Sulfonated molecules are transported across the plasma membrane of cells via ATP binding cassette (ABC) proteins, sodium-dependent organic anion transporter (SOAT) and organic anion transporter polypeptides (OATPs), where they provide a circulating reservoir for cellular uptake and intracellular de-sulfonation. R-O-SO3 represents sulfonated substrates, including steroids.

**FIGURE 3.**
Steroid sulfonation and de-conjugation pathways play an important role in steroid metabolism, as well as regulating steroid half-life and activity. (_A_) Steroid sulfatase (STS) converts cholesterol sulfate to cholesterol, which is then transported into mitochondria for conversion to pregnenolone, and downstream adrenal products including DHEA and DHEA sulfate. (_B_) DHEA sulfate serves as the precursor molecule for synthesis of the non-adrenal steroid hormones, including testosterone and estrogens. In most cases, sulfonation decreases the biological activity of steroids by preventing binding to steroid receptors.

> Pregnenolone and its sulfate, like [DHEA](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dehydroepiandrosterone) and its [sulfate](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DHEA_sulfate) and progesterone, belong to the group of [neurosteroids](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuroactive_steroid) that are found in high concentrations in certain areas of the brain, and are synthesized there. Neurosteroids affect synaptic functioning, are [neuroprotective](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuroprotective), and enhance [myelinization](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Myelin). Pregnenolone and its sulfate ester are under investigation for their potential to improve cognitive and [memory](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Memory) functioning.[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid11744095-3) Pregnenolone is also being considered as a potential treatment for[schizophrenia](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schizophrenia).[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid21756978-1)
>
> Interestingly, unlike pregnenolone, [pregnenolone sulfate](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone_sulfate) is a [negative allosteric modulator](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Negative_allosteric_modulator) of the GABAA receptor[\[4\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid3138576-4) as well as a positive allosteric modulator of the[NMDA receptor](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NMDA_receptor).[\[5\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid1654510-5)[\[6\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid1387199-6) In addition, it has been shown to activate the [transient receptor potential M3](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TRPM3) (TRPM3) [ion channel](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ion_channel) in [hepatocytes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hepatocyte) and [pancreatic islets](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islets_of_Langerhans) causing[calcium](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calcium) entry and subsequent [insulin](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insulin) release.[\[7\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid18978782-7)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone)

As I recall, sulfur has a lot to do with our ability to handle light, especially higher energy/frequency forms such as the sun...

### Josh — 2015-01-15T20:31:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152897>

Interesting article which I have only skimmed, seems we have reviewed the implications of "mitofilin depleted mitochondria" before....

> The inner mitochondrial membrane is where the action of life happens. It is covered by some unusual proteins. [Mitofilin proteins](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23109542) are crucial organizers of mitochondrial architecture. They are located in the inner mitochondrial membrane and interact with several protein complexes of the outer membrane, thereby generating contact sites between the two membrane systems of mitochondria. Here you can see how proteins undergo elastic changes as electrons are added and subtracted to its structure. When electrons are abundant, the mitofilin works to maintain ETC. When it is not, the result is autophagy or apoptosis. Within the inner membrane, mitofilins are part of hetero-oligomeric protein complexes that have been termed as the mitochondrial inner membrane organizing system (MINOS). MINOS integrity is required for the maintenance of the characteristic morphology of the inner mitochondrial membrane where ETC occurs. The inner boundary region is closely apposed to the outer membrane and cristae membranes, which form large tubular invaginations that protrude into the mitochondrial matrix and harbor the enzyme complexes of the oxidative phosphorylation machinery. MINOS deficiency comes along with a loss of crista junction structures and the detachment of cristae from the inner boundary membrane. MINOS has been conserved in evolution from unicellular eukaryotes to humans. Alterations of MINOS subunits are associated with multiple pathological conditions.

[http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-6-mitochondrial-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-6-mitochondrial-rx/)

### Josh — 2015-01-15T20:52:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152901>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=152808)
>
> Yes and it is magnified in water.

Do we have vestiges of the dual system that seals and whales have for hearing in air and in water?

> Conceptually, hearing is a relatively simple chain of events: sound energy is converted by neuro-mechanical
> transducers (middle and inner ear) into electrical signals (neural impulses) that provide a central processor (brain)
> with a window on the world. The view through that window differs dramatically for every species and, in part,
> reflects the selection pressures that shaped its ear. Structurally", the picture is more complex. Mammalian ears are
> elegant structures, packing over 75,000 mechanical and electrochemical components into an average area of I cm3.
> Structural variations in the components are essential elements in species differences in hearing abilities; therefore,
> comparing ears from species with different hearing ranges and habitats can provide useful insights into both specific
> and basic hearing mechanisms.
> Marine mammals, and whales in particular, are good candidates for such analyses. "Marine mammal" is a broad
> categorization for over 150 species from four orders. Extant groups of marine mammals include the Pinnipedia,
> which includes the true seals (phocids), sea lions (otariids), and walrus (odobenids); marine Fissipedia (sea otters);
> Sirenia (manatees and dugongs); and Cetacea, which comprise the baleen or "moustached" (mysticete) whales and
> echo locating, toothed ( odontocete) whales, porpoises, and dolphins. The degree of aquatic adaptation varies for each
> group. Many pinnipeds are amphibious, while all cetaceans and sirenians are aquatic obligates, unable to move,
> reproduce, or feed on land.
> All marine mammals, including whales, evolved from land-based animals with air-adapted ears (1). Over the next
> 50 million years, as their body shapes evolved to withstand enormous pressures, preserve heat, and move efficiently
> in the comparatively dense, cold environment of the seas, their ears evolved in tandem to effectively processes
> underwater sound. Water, with a density nearly 800-fold that of air (r=1.03 glee vs. 0. 0013 glee), has a sonic
> velocity that is on average 4.5 times faster than in air (c=l 530m/sec vs. c=340rn/sec). To examine the sensory
> implications of these values, consider a hypothetical mammal, that hears equally well in water and in air. An
> animal with an intensity based ear would require the same acoustic power/unit area in water as in air to have an
> equal sound percept, or (lair= !water):
> lair= Pai/1(0.442 g-rn/sec-cc) = Pwate/1(1575.g-rn/sec-cc) =!water (1)
> which implies the sound pressure in water must be 59.7 times that required in air to produce the same intensity and
> therefore the same sensation in this hypothetical perfectly matched amphibian ear. The units of intensity are, of
> course, watts/m 2, but to put this into the context of conventional hearing measures, recall that intensity is related to
> the mean square pressure of the sound wave over time. Most hearing studies report hearing thresholds in effective
> sound pressure level (SPL), expressed in decibels (dB), which in turn are complicated by arbitrary reference
> pressures. For air-borne sound measures, the reference is generally dB SPL or dB re 20 JlPa rms; for underwater
> sound measures, the reference pressure is dB re 1 JlPa. Thus, underwater sound pressure levels numerically are
> 61.5 dB greater than sound pressure levels in air for an equal intensity. In any case, whether viewed in terms of
> pressure or intensity, there are substantial differences for an equal percept
> Marine mammal ears have solved this problem, as well as all the attendant differences in other acoustic cues; e.g.
> shortened interaural arrival times. Equally important, underwater acuity, and the level of structural adaptation that
> underlie these abilities, do vary among marine mammals. Some, like the sea lion, still hear better in air, while
> others, like the bottlenosed dolphin, are fully aquatic, that is, they are exceptionally acute processors of waterborne
> sound but are probably incapable of hearing in air in any normal functional sense. Therefore, teasing out differences
> among these ears and comparing them to those of land mammal provides an opportunity to explore how structural
> elements in mammal ears relate to media dependent cues important for hearing.

And the post by @[Da-mo](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9712/) is great too! -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/#post-152803](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/#post-152803)

\----

Anyway, let's attempt to put things together from both a high-level and low-level perspective.

At the high level: What is the goal of the cell in the first place?

Getting back to my Gedanken high-and-mighty creator-of-everything perspective: The goal of the cell is to read and react to environmental stressors using as little energy as possible; keep as many constituent particles and wave stationary, receiving information, and not needing to be advanced forward to their next state

Note: everything about wakefulness is a "stress" that requires a response

I also tend to like me Computer-Science oriented State Machine analogy, we can also call this "collapsing the waveform"​

\----

At the low-level, the goal is to focus as much information to the measurement device as possible. In this case, the measurement devices will be the SCN and the PVN.

I personally think that the PVN is placed alongside the Third Ventricle, which has a concentrating vortex during wakefulness, so that "memory" from the water in that ventricle is transmitted to the PVN.

I think this is a great way of measuring time effects using the natural Josephson Junction that occurs along the thin lining of axons between the third ventricle and the hypothalamus (provided that the axons are adequately myelinated) -- [http://www.riken.jp/en/research/rikenresearch/highlights/5682](http://www.riken.jp/en/research/rikenresearch/highlights/5682)

\----

Intuitively, I think that the greater a positive time dilation differences in timing information between the SCN+PVN and the body, the greater the error in measurement. Meaning that time passes faster for the SCN than the rest of the body => less window of opportunity for measuring things accurately.

Flipping that around is beneficial. Example: greater magnetic field and/or greater gravitational effect upon the SCN => greater time dilation effects => larger window of opportunity for accurate measurement

In that sense, I think more time for the SCN+PVN relative to the rest of the body is a good thing. I would also then think that greater overall time dilation effects in the early part of the year is beneficial thing to have while in a waking state.

(Note that if my logic behind a concentrating vortex also concentrating information is wrong, then the conclusions are flipped. ie: if a dissipative vortex helps the PVN get more info, then it's probably better to have greater time dilation effects in a sleep state, which is favoured during a circadian winter time)

### Josh — 2015-01-16T18:45:34+0200

So the dive reflex causes a shift to and or activation of whatever vestiges of this remain? This must cause time dilation as well via alteration of the PVN outputs.

> **6.** If you use just air to adapt to cold thermogenesis it will take a lot longer, but there is one thing I should mention to you. Try to slowly remove clothing as you proceed over time. As you remove clothing, there is a specific way you should pick the clothes to remove. You want to expose your face and head to cold as soon as possible. Remember in number 1, we begin cold water adaptation with our face? This is because all mammals have a reflex called a dive reflex that is built in because we all were formed in a fluid filled placenta. When we expose ourselves to cold on our face first, we stimulate slowing of our heart rate. This is soon followed by vasoconstriction of blood flow in our extremities. When we continue to dive deep (think about dive in the movie the Abyss) we force blood and water to pass through our organs and endothelium to fill our air filled cavities like our chest. This has been experimentally shown even in humans with deep water cold dives in adults. We actually drown in pulmonary fluids but we can still survive! As a physician, I see this problem clinically daily in our ICU’s in patients with ARDS. Sadly, we do not treat them as I think we should, given what we know about the mammalian dive reflex but that is another blog. Do not worry, I do not plan to use this adaptation in my reset in the near future! I’d love to try it but I hear it takes years to perfect from my research on it.

[http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/)

This must be what slows the heart rate?

Possibly relevant:

> These studies have shown that RVLM
> neurones are not alone in directly controlling sympathetic
> vasomotor outflow. Using the hypothalamic PVN as an
> example, I have tried to illustrate that the tonic activity
> of vasomotor nerves allows the PVN to initiate nonuniform
> patterns of sympathetic nerve activation, so that
> delivery of blood can be directed to organs where it is most
> needed. I show how this has led to the concept of
> ‘command neurones’ and that the new understanding
> has directed interest to changes in the brain as a cause
> of alterations in sympathetic activity in cardiovascular
> disorders such as hypertension and congestive heart
> failure.

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/expphysiol.2006.035378/pdf](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/expphysiol.2006.035378/pdf)

**Figure 4. Major pontine and medullary regions involved in the cardiovascular and respiratory responses to peripheral chemoreflex activation**. Aortic and carotid bodies and supra-medullary pathways are not shown. Observe that peripheral chemoreflex activation recruits several brainstem nuclei in series and in parallel in order to produce a patterned and integrated cardiorespiratory response to hypoxia, which is characterized by (1) bradycardia, represented here as a decrease in instantaneous heart rate; (2) sympathoexcitation, demonstrated here as increases in the amplitude of thoracic sympathetic nerve activity; (3) tachypnea, show as an increase in the frequency of phrenic nerve bursts; and (4) forced expiration, here seen as an increase in the activity of the abdominal muscles during the expiratory phase of the respiratory cycle. NTS, nucleus tractus solitarii; NA, nucleus ambiguous; RTN/pFRG, retrotrapezoid nucleus and the parafacial respiratory group; BötC, Bötzinger Complex; preBötC, pre-Bötzinger Complex; rVRG, rostral ventral respiratory group; cVRG, caudal ventral respiratory group; RVLM, rostral-ventrolateral medulla; CVLM, caudal-ventrolateral medulla.

[http://journal.frontiersin.org/Journal/10.3389/fphys.2014.00302/full](http://journal.frontiersin.org/Journal/10.3389/fphys.2014.00302/full)

> **Evolution and physiology of neural oxygen sensing**
>  [Kauê M. Costa](http://www.frontiersin.org/people/u/55856)†, [ Daniela Accorsi-Mendonça](http://www.frontiersin.org/people/u/171967), [ Davi J. A. Moraes](http://www.frontiersin.org/people/u/167128) and [ Benedito H. Machado](http://www.frontiersin.org/people/u/28833)\*

They ask this question:

> But what kind of situations in the life of a mammal could cause hypoxia?

and there is no: changes in altitude, changes in temperature, changes in magnetic and electrical fields on their list. THeir conclusion is that chemoreflexes are for acute environmental changes that may cause pathology.

### Blue30 — 2015-01-14T21:47:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oscillating-hormones.12613/post-152755>

Hi everyone! I'm new to the forum and looking for a little help with oscillating hormones (in particular, T). Sometimes my T is right in the middle on the labs, but more often than not it's right at the lower end. It's been oscillating like this for several years. I've tried running every lab test I can think of (CBC, liver enzymes, cortisol, leptin, thyroid, vitamin D, heavy metal, CRP, gut/food allergy, hair mineral analysis, SpectraCell, etc.), but I have not been able to identify the cause. When I first started to notice it, my blood work reflect vitamin D levels below normal. I've since supplemented orally, but I understand that may not be doing much good. Also, my TT3 is just below normal (67\* ng/dL (ref 76-181)), but FT3 is normal (3.2 pg.mL (ref 2.3-4.2)). rT3 is a little on the high side, but still in range (19.4 ng/dL (ref 9.2-24.1)). I thought it might be a leptin issue, but CRP was very low (0.09 mg/L, Ref: 0.0-3.0).

Anyway, I'm hoping for a little input on where to look or what to do next - I'm running out of ideas. If it's helpful, I have a ton more labs. Also, I have psoriasis-like skin issues, if that is relevant.

Thank you all for the help!

### Shijin13 — 2015-01-15T20:05:54+0200

@[Blue30](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/12419/)

looking at your cortisol test - you have a conversion issue going on. You going to want to run a set of labs the capture Lutenizing Hormone (LH) and Folicular stimulating Hormone (FSH), to see what your hormone cascade for testosterone looks like. You also need to see what your LDL, HDL, Trigs, Pregnenolone, TSH, FT3, FT4, rT3, Estradiol, Progesterone, and Total Testosterone, and Free Testosterone look like. You also need an ASI with melatonin panel. Prolactin levels, Serotonin levels, IGF-1, hsCRP, 25OH (D3) and SHBG (Sex Hormone Binding Globulin) . You really need the Estradiol and SHGB to see what's going with the estrogens in your body.

The other thing that I picked up on - is your sleeping 8hr, not dreaming and you're not waking up rested. That screams OSA. Have you done a sleep study? OSA is the result of your brains inability to sense nnEMF to allow autophage at night for cellular repair. You can have an awesome 6 pack, yet if you're not getting quality sleep - you're leptin resistant

Do the optimal reset - Jack's got lots of great folks on there.

### Da-mo — 2015-01-16T03:33:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oscillating-hormones.12613/post-152942>

> [Blue30 said:](/goto/post?id=152939)
>
> JanSz, I've attached my SpectraCell report.
>
> Also, why should fish oil and krill oil be avoided? Too much of a change in the omega 6 to omega 3 ratio?

More in the DHA to EPA ratio - most fish/krill oils have the ratio backwards.

Also - DHA needs to be in the SN2 position to be taken into the cell. DHA is protected against degradation by iodine (much of which is removed from supplements). DHA from seafood (as opposed to supplements) will have all the necessary co-factors packaged up with it as well.

### Da-mo — 2015-01-16T04:34:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oscillating-hormones.12613/post-152962>

I see in your ZRT labs DHEAS good - so tracing it through the chart JanSz posted it seems the pathway is ok that far on the way to T. So looking downstream of that point might help . . . .

### Da-mo — 2015-01-16T04:56:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oscillating-hormones.12613/post-152965>

Digging a little further, you mention both a stressful time and psoriasis like skin issues. DHEAS is good yet Testosterone is low.

Comparing to JanSZ's chart downstream of testosterone we see DHT. Quick google seems to tie stress, psoriasis and elevated DHT together. . . .

> **Stress** - A surge in stress levels causes high levels of DHT circulating in the body, and triggers an increase in sebum production which can irritate the skin and give yeast more to feed upon. [http://www.psoriasisrx.com/seborrhoeic\_dermatitis.asp](http://www.psoriasisrx.com/seborrhoeic_dermatitis.asp)

Could it be possible that your testosterone generation is working fine but much of it is getting converted DHT and causing the skin issues?

Hopefully the others will know how to check for this - initially it would seem that if this is the case a 5ar inhibitor might have an effect but I think there are risks with going down that path.

Just a bit more to give a little background . . .

> **While androgens (the general class of male hormones which includes testostrone and DHT)** levels may be similar in men who have male pattern baldness and those who have hair, people with more 5-alpha reductase or a greater density of DHT receptors may be more vulnerable to the effects of DHT. Surprisingly, men with male pattern baldness have, **on average, significantly lower levels of total testosterone, though they did not have significantly lower levels of unbound androgens in their blood**.\[3\]
>
> **Spiking androgen levels, caused by intense weight training,** sudden weight loss, taking anabolic steroids or other synthetic androgens and other causes can promote the balding process. The vast majority of anabolic steroids contribute to hair loss, **since most anabolic compounds break down to form DHT at some point. [http://www.psoriasiscafe.org/baldness-definition.htm](http://www.psoriasiscafe.org/baldness-definition.htm)**

Da-mo, interesting ideas both with the zinc uptake and the conversions. I do eat a lot of nuts so the phytates could be causing an issue with absorption. ### Da-mo — 2015-01-16T07:54:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oscillating-hormones.12613/post-152987>

Interesting article on Testosterone and DHT showing how dropping T levels and increasing DHT can be protective for the prostate against BPH [http://www.bodybuilding.com/fun/reform8.htm](http://www.bodybuilding.com/fun/reform8.htm)

> As I started to explain before, DHT is a strong androgen that will signal the pituitary to decrease the production of gonadotropins. The decrease in gonadotropins will then cause less testosterone to be produced which will in turn cause the estrogen levels to drop. The resulting change in the hormonal milieu (high DHT, low estrogen) then apparently results in a regression of BPH. The clinical application of this theory is discussed in US patent 5,648,350 Dihydrotestosterone for use in androgenotherapy.

### JanSz — 2015-01-17T19:35:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oscillating-hormones.12613/post-153173>

Things to stay away from or risk damage to androgen/estrogen converting enzymes and receptors
or at least measure your DHT level and do not let it drop to less than mid-range.

propecia--5aR inhibitor
proscar -- 5aR inhibitor
Avodart 5aR and 5bR inhibitor
Accutane (isotretinoin) inhibits one of the above
///////////////////////////////////

TSH 1.98 mIU/L (ref 0.4-4.5)
TT4 6.0 mcg.dL (ref 4.5-12)
TT3 67\* ng/dL (ref 76-181) ---> out of range

both TT4 and TT3 could be higher

To make sure that you have plenty of iodine/iodide consider using Lugol's solution on top of seaweed and seafood
note ---- we need both forms, iodine & iodide
Spectracel indicates sufficient selenium=78 (>74%)
1 dropperfull/day=48mg/day=0.8cc
Lugol's Iodine Family Pack -- 6 (2 fl. oz.) bottles
[http://www.amazon.com/Lugols-Iodine-Family-Pack-bottles/dp/B001RRP3FW](http://www.amazon.com/Lugols-Iodine-Family-Pack-bottles/dp/B001RRP3FW)

thyroid gland holds 50mg iodine
whole body holds 1500mg iodine

Dr Jorge D. Flechas, MD mentions sick symporter that may prevent proper iodide transport
he is saying that symporter can be helped but does not say how.

The Sodium-Iodide Symporter
[http://arbl.cvmbs.colostate.edu/hbooks/pathphys/endocrine/thyroid/nai\_symport.html](http://arbl.cvmbs.colostate.edu/hbooks/pathphys/endocrine/thyroid/nai_symport.html)

The most important stimulator of symporter gene and protein expression
is thyroid-stimulating hormone, similar to what is observed with other important
thyroid proteins such as thyroglobulin and thyroid peroxidase.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16954431](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16954431)
Enhancement of sodium/iodide symporter expression

The sodium/iodide symporter (NIS) mediates iodide uptake in the thyroid gland

Retinoic acid has been used to stimulate NIS expression in both thyroid and breast cancer.

Your Spectracel indicate sufficient levels of vit A (Retinol).

If this is proper line of research, possibly someone on this board have suggestions
on how to raise TT4 & TT3.
//////////////////////////////////

Note that you have psoriasis
psoriasis home treatment is to paint it with lugol's

I guess that you may have one or both
low iodine/iodide
or
slow symporter

//////////////////////////////////
pantethine
Pantetheine
pantothenic acid

You have a low Pantothenate

My Pantothenate was originally low, it took number of Spectracel tests and 4/day
Nutricology Pantothenic Acid, Vegicaps, 90-Count
to barely get me over to better levels.
Now I am trying: Pantethine
wish me luck

[http://www.amazon.com/Allergy-Research-Nutricology-Pantothenic-capsules/dp/B000M8OMMW/ref=sr\_1\_2?ie=UTF8&qid=1403534679&sr=8-2&keywords=pantethine nutricology](http://www.amazon.com/Allergy-Research-Nutricology-Pantothenic-capsules/dp/B000M8OMMW/ref=sr_1_2?ie=UTF8&qid=1403534679&sr=8-2&keywords=pantethine+nutricology)
Allergy Research (Nutricology) - Pantothenic Acid (as calcium Pantotheate), 500 mg, 90 capsules

[http://www.nutricology.com/Pantethine-60-Caps-p-16549.html](http://www.nutricology.com/Pantethine-60-Caps-p-16549.html)
Pantethine is a derivative of pantothenic acid (vitamin B5) and a key constituent of coenzyme A,
which is involved in the metabolism of amino acids, carbohydrates, and lipids.\*
Contains Pantesin®, a high quality, pharmaceutical grade brand of pantethine.
Price: $38.21

//////////////////////////////////
zinc and B12 are borderline, may want to tweak your diet and/or suplement them.

//////////////////////////////////

Get from LabCorp or Mayo clinic:

Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum

Make sure that all of them are within +-25% from mid-range of their respective ranges.
Status=((C852-((E852+D852)/2))/(E852-D852))\*100

Pay closer attention to

ALA, LA, EPA tend to be too high (watch out for nuts, seeds, oils, fish/krill oils)
DHA, AA, GLA, palmitoleic acid(O7), tend to be low

/////////////////////////////////////

There is
BodyBio Wellness Report
available from:
[http://www.bodybio.com/BodyBioReport.aspx?Test=Yes](http://www.bodybio.com/BodyBioReport.aspx?Test=Yes)
That is, it is from dr Patricia Kane (fatty acids guru).
It have a different approach than Spectracell Micronutrient Analysis but
does similar assesment.
You have to bring your own blood test, price is for post-processing only.
Look up the sample report:

Any ideas why SHBG is so high?
\-----------------------------------------------------------
Good level of SHBG is 20-25

Level of SHBG is mostly generally correlated with good health.
Observed correlations are with

estrogens, raising increases SHBG
testosterone, raising decreases SHBG
better thyroid hormone panel influence SHBG, up or down
good Growth Hormone influences SHBG, up or down
\------------------------------------------------------------

both TT4 and TT3 could be higher >>>>????? Sodium-Iodide Symporter
\------------------------------------------------------------

When monitoring GH, or making decision about supplementing with GH
dr Hertoghe looks mostly at

IGF-1
and
IGFBP3

caveat:
he insist that the testing is done using RIA (manual) methodology.
He specifically warns against the (most commonly used) methodology, chemoluminessence.
Using RIA methodology to have sufficient GH on need to have:

IGF-1(300-350)ug/dl, deficient when less than 250
IGFBP3(3000)ug/dL, deficient when >4000
\===========================================================

For my self, I have done at LabCorp tests two different ways and got dramatically different results.

Tests at LabCorp that dr Hertohge warns against
Insulin-like Growth Factor 1 (IGF-1)Test Number: 010363 CPT Code: 84305
Insulin-like Growth Factor-binding Protein 3 (IGFBP-3) Test Number: 140152 CPT Code: 83520
\--

Tests that dr Hertoghe would hopefully approve:
Growth Assessment Test Group X (Growth Hormone, IGF-I, IGF-II, IGFBP-3)
Test Code 500901 ... CPT Code 83003, 84305, 83519, 83519

PS- I got my urinalysis results back yesterday as well... I have trace ketones and red blood cells in there. So those and this are all that's new. Keep in mind I have been looked at for lymphoma (lymph system/blood cancer) by an oncologist/hematologist since September and recently two new nodes popped up.. they're all on both sides of my neck directly adjacent to my thyroid. Makes me wonder....

I was also just diagnosed with early Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma in September. This is probably not related though it's hard to say because I am one of the ONLY people in my family to have this.. well the ONLY ONE in fact, but I have a brother and an aunt who have high IOPs... but out of 5 siblings and 14 aunts/uncles on both sides, I am the only person wiht glaucoma. And I am the youngest of all of them... and to be diagnosed with glaucoma at my age is super rare, only like 0-5% of glaucoma sufferers are under age 45 and I was diagnosed at 41.

Makes me wonder, again, if things are not connected someplace.

So....thoughts, anyone?

I eat the standard american diet, or did up until 3 days prior to this test sample being taken. High carbs, low protein, moderate fat, few vegetables.I'm a huge fan of snacking on pretzels up til bedtime - salt is one of my biggest cravings and has been all of my life. (Interesting since salt is iodized and hashis has a lack of iodine as a component if I recall correctly.. maybe my body has been craving sodium because of the iodine? Interesting!)

I think the ketones are because I had fasted for 12 hours before they took my urine sample, because I also had a lipid profile done that day and it required fasting.

I'm more concerned about the trace amounts of red blood cells right now (compared to the ketones). But more than either of those I'm now fascinated by this antibody result!

So, I just sent a note to my oncologist to let him know of the thyroid and urinalysis findings so at least we're all on the same page. Today is my 42nd birthday and I am just wanting answers for a gift. It fees like maybe I'm getting that, so I'm pleased.

Jack, you have no idea how happy you just made me. I wouldn't know about this if it wasn't for you. I HAVE AN ANSWER!!

Knowledge is Power! Now I can make a plan and DO SOMETHING!

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-15T15:50:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-peroxidase-antibody-i-have-lots.12624/post-152857>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=152854)
>
> Anyone here of cases of people reversing their **Hashimoto's**? How about reversing other auto-immune conditions? I think Terry Wahl did it, right ...

Funny thing is, I have a peculiar suspicion tha of all the things a human can reverse that goes wrong with their body, autoimmune conditions should be the most do-able.

It's entirely based within the body after all. And that is one thing we can control more than anything else, IMO.

Doesn't mean I know how.

Just means I believe I can, and I believe you put information out there to the masses that holds the first key to how this can be done.

The same way that my favorite part of my life so far has been going to college each time because of the way it challenges, educates, and empowers me with knowledge is the same way that I suspect this next year will invigorate and empower me... I love to take in information, synthesize it, and spit it back out. This time I can do it in a way that may actually change my life. I love that challenge!

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-15T15:56:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-peroxidase-antibody-i-have-lots.12624/post-152858>

Now I'm going to put a ridiculous question out there.

I see the word "oxidizing" when reading about Hashis.

This immediately reminds me of the conversation I just had with my new hair dresser who specializes in cancer patients and other serious hair issues. We were talking about my irregular hairline from some inexplicable loss I've had over recent years, and also discussing the way my hair color changes from roots to tips. She said it's normal for hair to OXIDIZE as it gets older (so, longer) and that's why most people with long hair have that ombre look to the color, where the ends are lighter than the tips. She remarked that mine oxidizes pretty fast, it gets a LOT lighter without so much length in between.

Does hair embody what's going on inside the body? To an extent that over-oxidation of hair COULD indicate an oxidizing disease process inside the body?

This is probably just a silly co-inky-dink. But had to put it out there, because it's how I am!

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-15T16:57:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-peroxidase-antibody-i-have-lots.12624/post-152869>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=152868)
>
> loss of hair is an oxidizing event which is why chemo cause hair loss

That would seem to imply that oxidation targets cells with faster turnover time then, since that is what chemo targets and destroys (that's what cancer cells are... that what mucosal lining is.. that's what hair is... etc.. hence hair fall out, sores in the mouth, etc)...

I was more interested in what she told me about hair color lightening from root to tip being a sign of oxidation. I was wondering if this could be soemthing a person could note about themselves and consider a sign to look further. Years ago my hair color was the same from root to tip but now it goes from brown to almost blonde.. due to (over/excessive/rapid) oxidation according to my hair dresser. I found it quite interesting.

### angie-s — 2015-01-15T17:00:51+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2015-01-16T07:34:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-peroxidase-antibody-i-have-lots.12624/post-152985>

Because if your cells are bad GH stimulate bad cells to grow and they do bad things. If your cells have a good redox potential then GH causes good growth.........it is not GH that determines the rules of the game. It is the redox potential in cells and in your extracellular matrix.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-16T13:19:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-peroxidase-antibody-i-have-lots.12624/post-153000>

I've been reading all I can since this time yesterday (tricky since it was my birthday and we did have some things to do outside the home.. Happy Birthday, I got me Hashimoto's! LOL).. and the first thing that I notice is that things going back even, perhaps, to childhood are now explained. It's rather frustrating that the doctors had all hte signs and symptoms there in front of them for years and nobody picked up on it, and I ultimately had to find it myself via pro-actively asking my doctor to run the recommended labs on this website.

I surely already had this in 2003 when I had preeclampsia during pregnancy with my daughter. That pregnancy was different in every way from my others. I think perhaps this came on after I had my boys in 93 and 95, and before my daughter in 03.

I vaguely remember trying a thyroid medicine of some kind at one time after I had one of the boys but that was 20 years ago now at least and I do not recall what or why, only that I had a female resident/doctor who I liked due to her being forward-thinking and open-minded. I'm not sure why she had me try it, or why I was taken back off it when I finished all my other postpartum meds (iron for anemia, etc).

But I mean,think of the hundreds of thousands of taxpayer dollars spent on my testing, since I was on state funded insurance as a young mother, for example....

I had all the autoimmune testing done TWICE by two different rheumatologists. Meanwhile I was diatnosed with Raynaud's and osteoarthritis in my hands, feet, and right hip. I was told hte right hip was chronically inflamed. I went through three pregnancies iwth no stretch marks in sight but in a couple of months my entire wasitline to bottom of the thighs are covered front and back with stripes from the swelling.

They sent me for physical therapy. The therapist put electrodes on my lower back and turned it on and then pushed the machine closer to me and said I could adjust it as I wanted to. I turned it up. IT was too far, it felt like everything changed that day. I found out later they can't let patients do that, it can be dangerous.. I've felt like something switched from positive to negative inside my whole body that day, I have been in a bad mood - and feeling unwell - ever since. That was 2006. I've always wondered if my magnetics got switched literally from positive to negative or something from that. Crazy, I know.

My lower legs gave out on me. I had to pull myself up and down stairs by the railings and eventually nearly stopped using hte steps at all, spending my time downstairs only. At this point I had brain scans looking for MS.

I had EMG testing to try to understand the peripheral neuropathy.

I had CT scans looking for cancer.

I had neurology appointments with a doctor who repeated many of the tests, and sent me for memory testing due to me becoming so foggy that I was "losing time" while out and about, sometimes spending so long standing and staring at items on the shelf in aisles that people would ask if I was okay. One day I went home after dark with almost nothing after spending all day at Walmart without my list and unable to recall what I was there for. I started to think I was losing my mind.

I was medicated for depression and anxiety for eleven years with so many medicines that it would make your head spin. I discovered I had drug hypersensitivity. Everything seemed to just be getting worse. They started prescribing other medicines to fix the side effects of the first medicines, then new ones to fix the cascading effects of the second batch... I became a mindless zombie full of chemicals and distrust for medicine.

Eventually my mother passed away and for the first time in my life I added alcohol into my diet. Things tanked HARD. Everything went to crap, my hair my skin my nails my joints my mood, my ability to think. I stayed like that for a couple of years. When 2008 came and I took alcohol back out of my life and looked into the mirror, I had aged myself at least 10 years in less than 5.

February 2009 I couldn't take the weakness anymore in my legs and the fatigue. The feeling like I couldn't even open my mouth to reply to my kids becasue I felt so buried deep inside myself it was too much physical effort to open my lips to try to make my vocal chords find the energy to move to form words. IT was too tough. I withdrew more. At this point my doctor had e try adderall 10mg daily - off label for fatigue since I do not have ADHD, though I was complaining of brain fog by then and we both thought maybe it would help me. After all, one of my sons seemed to have ADHD too.. he was so difficult at times... (could he have Hashi's? Now I wonder..).. I'm still on this medicine to this day.

By now my sex drive had been completely and utterly 100% gone for years. YEARS.

My GP was at his wit's end. After last ditch lyme and lupus retesting for the umpteenth time, there were no more specialists to send me to. He asked if I wanted a psychiatrist. I left his office in tears, 20 years of trust and respect severely damaged between us. He thought I was a hypochondriac. No wonder the last several doctors seemed exasperated to even meet me and seemed to be blowing me off. Something was put in my file to say I have some sort of "healthy hypervigilance" - nice way of saying she's paranoid she's sick, she's a hypo. She's nuts. She's a weak whiney female. Uterus = hysteria.

Which is funny because next thing you know my female gynocologist is saying she should refer me to her fellow surgical gyn.. I would be good to get my ladyparts yanked. I don't need them anymore, I've had my tubes tied. and since I keep having irregular and heavy periods I should just remove it.

Well. I could go on. But it's all here in my personal medical files, on my computer, in a spreadsheet, as well as in my doctor's files and so on.

I had Hashi's all along and because of it not being diagnosed I was literally on suicide watch for depression and anxiety at one point for about a year, during which I had agorophobia... couldn't handle talking to anyone, dealing with any one... stress puts me out of my mind STILL to thisday.. I avoid all family gatherings.. my inlaws barely KNOW me despite living in the same town for our 20+++ year marriage..

I nearly lost my daughter twice during my pregnancy with her, and preeclampsia made us both risk seizures and death. She had to be born so early that she was UNDER five pounds when we brought her home from the hospital.

EVERYTHING... WAS.... HASHI'S.

My extended family has been recently overwhelmed with fibromyalgia diagnoses. The ladies of my family are having terrible issues. Their children get ADHD and autism/asperger's diagnoses (I personally have felt for five years now that I must have both because why else would I act and feel and think like this? I do not fit in. My brain and body aren't right.IT WAS THE HASHI'S.)

Anyway like i said... I could go on... but it's safe to say that taxpayers covered a whole lot of stuff listed above that could've been avoided by testing my thyroid antibodies. They just covered that test for me too. I think they'd be happy this time to do so.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-16T13:31:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-peroxidase-antibody-i-have-lots.12624/post-153001>

The next question is, do I keep on track with my BHRT plans.

I came here with no sex drive, wanting to feel like myself again. I had found this forum by searching google for info on BHRT. Now weeks later I finally am on appointment day - I see my ladyparts doc today and its supposed to be all in preparation for going on BHRT soon.

But is that even safe now? Yes, lots of Hashi's women go on BHRT but I only am just unofficially diagnosed. So I dont know ... anything. I don't know if now I should see a doctor for this.. if they are going to put me on meds... if I can wean off the adderall... if I need to get all this straight to see how my stuff bounces back on its own before I start adding BHRT back in...

but since I self referred for the blood work I think I will havee to go from her on my own. I can ask my doc to refer me to an endocrinologist or whomever else would tend to my thyroid, I guess. Or I can ask him to prescribe something to me for it himself, and knowing him he surely will if I can back it up with facts.

But... now I'm lost. My whole gameplan has a wrench in it, and I leave in a couple of hours for my gyn appointment. Not even sure if I Should tell her about this or not. I sort of don't want to, because I feel like it's opening a can of worms that essentially amounts to "you failed for not seeing this ahead of time" which I don't want her to feel like I'm implying.

hm.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-16T14:04:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-peroxidase-antibody-i-have-lots.12624/post-153002>

Reading this long thread that Dr. K. posted above still... [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/) .... and I am getting a feeling from reading it that I am going to have to change some things about my life with this being a likely diagnosis for me. People are talking like it's a death sentence, or as bad as cancer or heart disease or glaucoma (yeah I either have, have had, probably have, or will have all three along with Hashi's so maybe that's why this isn't phasing me yet..?). People are avoiding certain foods and syaing if they have ANY of ANY of them, then they will have a flare.

How do I even know what that is anymore when I've lived "like this" for at least a decade, if not longer? What constitutes a notable flare?

I'm not being facetious or difficult, not being snarky or intentionally obtuse.. I just don't know the answers because this is all being thrown at me like a pie in the face. Similarly, it's kinda humorous considering how easy the diagnosis could've been as complared to all I had to go through to get to this point, but overall it's just making a huge mess for me. I'm wondering what's the first step in cleaning it up. What does a non-Krusian do at this point? Get a prescription filled and go on with their life?

For all I know, the fact that my body is popping up lymph nodes that have me seeing an oncologist while simultaneously developing glaaucoma \*IS\* a flare. I'm sure everyone here would say so. But what is a MEASURABLE flare? When your antibodies go up higher than your personal "norm"? Is there a personal "norm" that you all base subsequent testing results on? Do you always aim for "less antibodies are better" or for total elimination of antibodies (pretty sure that's not possible now. Nah, i \*knew\* that's not possible now. But will my 74 of them be reduced? Can I then take some sort of antigen killing substance cleverly designed to induce mimicry to fool my antibodies to latch onto them and then be killed? No anti-vaccine yet? Yes, I know that question was asked in the thread above but it was also answered back then as "nope, just don't introduce the antigens/triggerfoods".... well just thinking out loud as I learn.)

Suppose one doesn't modify their diet, what other options do they have?
Has anyone measured their telomeres right at Hashi's diagnosis, then again a year later after following an AI diet for a year?

If not, then I'll do that if I can afford it. Not sure what that would cost but I love this stuff. It's too late for me to go back to school for yet ANOTHER degree, but I can "practice" medicine / science / healthful practices on myself and see what happens, which I enjoy. Without motivation like documentation for purposes of eventual information gathering, I will find it difficult to change anything and keep it changed, honestly...


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Haley's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/haleys-journal.12626/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 75
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Haley — 2015-01-16T01:36:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/haleys-journal.12626/post-152927>

Hello Everyone!

I have appreciated all of the gracious help I have received thus far since being on the thread. I often feel very "out of the loop", being intimidated by the QUANTUMness...but realized today I am grasping things already more than I would of thought. So i'm going to go ahead and start a journal

Context: I am a 22 year old, newly Colorado-an, horse lover, avid health researcher. I became aware of my health when I was 15 and went from hating any form of a curve on my body to anorexic, then bulimic, then a binger, then back to anorexia/orthorexia..oh then bulimia/binging and back to anorexia/orthorexia..okay you get it. But back at age 17 I found out the antidepressants I was forced to be on were indeed NOT the answer and I also stumbled upon holistic nutrition and the blogosphere. Over time I developed a strict paleo template and have continued to manipulate my diet/supplements/lifestyle based off of who's webinar/blog i hear/read last. Let's just say by now I have tried not only every diet but every version of every diet

I'll say I officially started implementing Dr K's advice about 10 days ago. I have already been a low carb paleo-er for 5 years (with times of modification). I suspect I am leptin resistant since I do have about 10-20lbs I could lose (would love to loose 30 though) and have had my go with eating disorders. So I have begun...
\* my BABs. Created awareness to the amount of EMFs. Continuing to do my best to stay in sync with sunlight/darkness. Reduce my cortisol. Eat almost all my protein from seafood. Taken my 6-8c of non starchy veggies to about 1-2c/day. Removed the 15g carbs I was getting from winter squash. And continuing coconut oil along with EVOO on fish. Trying to get my D3 from sun only. Exercise is walking as much as I can with 3-4x/wk 10 min weights. Added raw seaweed. Drink more water. Stopped the herbal antibiotics from my candida cleanse. Eat 3x day without snacking. Don't turn car heat on. Leave house in the 50s. Cool-er showers. And I've done a lot more I cannot think of right now!

I've already been on a very low carb diet for months but once I cut that cup of winter squash ketosis really kicked in (as it always has) and my sleep immediately became disrupted. Prior I was sleeping at least 9 hrs/night now Im awake most of the night...and have been for 10 days and still haven't crashed. Driving me crazy. I say ketosis kicked in, but I'm still weary as I know Im eating a lot of protein. And all of the gurus say too much keeps you from being in ketosis. But my stomach has shrunk (at least deflated from water) so Im thinking I'm in ketosis (?). Also need to mention I still have a lot of fear regarding ketosis as almost every guru I follow has suggested one not doing it for several of the reasons I experienced the first time I did it 2 years ago.

Lately I wake up and eat my BAB with no problem...still actually hungry after. For example this morning i ate cod (50g protein worth) and a few collard leaves with a T of EVOO. And once again at 11 am I was hungry already! I wolfed down some beef shank and coconut oil and collards. Im trying to not freak out about calories being that high by 11am. (Context: I do count calories...a part of the brain I am trying to rewire..but they seem to make a difference in my weight regardless. I have been eating under 1500 cals/day for 7 months now and did so for 3 years prior...and then i binged for 1 month and gained 50 lbs). So I still keep my calls around 1300 even on ketosis but I find that my hunger has come back. And I'm not quite sure what to do with it. As it has not been my friend in the past. Anyway-should I eat more protein for my BAB since I am getting hungry (although so weird cuz i used to eat less for breakfast)??

I also have a lot of questions regarding gut issues. I have many many food sensitivies, autoimmune conditions and believe to have a slow thyroid. As well as may have a gut infection/parasite/virus...something that has cause me bloating/dig issues for YEARS. Don't have money to test so i have done every cleanse/protocol/supplement and can't seem to get to the root. Ive just completed 10 weeks of christ a orecchios candida cleanse which consisted of a low carb paleo diet and herbal antibiotics as well as sacromycees bollardi. And now that i feel i have "wiped out" my gut I'm worried I need to hurry and replenish it with good guys..but not quite sure how to start, effectively.

Okay, that felt good. Enough for today. I commend anyone who read that. Hoping for inspiration and community. My real goal is to help others one day. Ok. Thank you friends!

### caroline — 2015-01-16T01:50:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/haleys-journal.12626/post-152931>

Hi Haley - and welcome!

A couple things come to mind ... Did you actually have 50gr. of cod for your BAB? That would be a lot of cod!

I used to need around 80gr to knock out the hunger and cravings. If you are still hungry - you need to eat more. What about fat? grass fed butter? etc.

A proper big BAB would be about 4 eggs and 6 slices of bacon and maybe a pork chop or two! and lots of fat too.

I used to find it harder to fill up on seafood for my BAB .....but salmon works because it has more fat. Maybe try a mixture?

Have you joined the optimal reset? lots of great guests to answer your questions and then there is the live Q&A with Dr. kruse....

### Lahelada — 2015-01-16T02:09:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/haleys-journal.12626/post-152934>

Read ya,applaud ya now stop counting calories. Now this is said with the utmost respect . With time you will find all your answers here. Keep reading keep asking.
A bit of a headsup calories in calories out is a dead concept here. My
suspicion is that your sleep is disrupted. as your body is "screaming " "where is it". In other words it is not getting enough of what it was used to and not enough of what you wantitto get used to. In other words by not going with the
flow you are creating an emergency. When trying yo enter ketosis you need to indulge your whims. I understand that this initially feels as if you are trying to rationalise a "Death by chocolate" eating contest. The fall off in hunger will come later though. First of all you need to convince your system that you are not planning for the great shutdown!
Practically speaking I would not increase protein but do two things. Shjft your protein intake to seafood and pork and maybe include a bulletproof coffee or tea in your BAB. Secondly i would implement some easy raw stuff,like salami,olives that kind of thing to introduce some new immigrants to your gut population.
You could post your BaB here and we could suggest alternatives.
Mental food: Keep reading even if you think you do not understand. Something always sticks as you will find out as you go along.

Welcome to thjs upwardly slippery slope. It is excruciatjngly,maddeningly,confusingly,suspensefully interesting.

### Linz — 2015-01-16T10:56:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/haleys-journal.12626/post-152999>

Use a hot water bottle ( or two) at night so your face/breathing benefits from the cold while you stay comfortable. Cold face dunks and then ice packs will help start uncoupling so you gradually start generating heat and you should find you no longer feel cold at night. It takes time to adapt but waking up your UCPs will give you all kinds of benefit. Ketosis works best with cold (but you need to adapt to the cold carefully). It can also take up to two years to become an efficient fat burner although you should have started on the right road with your previous paleo diets.
Bacon and pork are a source of cysteine which is important component of collagen. You may well need extra vitamin K2 (mk 4) if you don't eat any raw dairy and your gut bacteria are trashed (good gut flora makes K2). It also helps teeth enormously so you can actually see the benefits quite fast.

### Linz — 2015-01-16T17:26:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/haleys-journal.12626/post-153019>

Haley, just use the bottle anywhere. I think if you can't generate free heat the damage from losing sleep is greater than the benefit of cold. But sleeping in a cold room is great. Your lifestyle is a good start but heat transfer is much faster with water than air so face dunks (just need a basin of water) building up to more immersion is far better. You will know when uncoupling starts.

The Korean probiotic Kimchi also has high K2 content but not sure how easy it is to make. Here (uk) we hardly see raw milk but unpasteurised cheese is quite common. I am very careful with it as I have ai issues too. Prebiotic veggies such as artichoke and horseradish might be useful. I haven't had much success with supplemental probiotics and they caused me considerable problems when I ate normal sad (but mostly unprocessed). Lactobacillus plantarum has been recommended as a good one here. Raw fish may be a better option and I think it promotes estriol production (safest form of oestrogen) in the gut.

From a quick skim I think todays blog emphasises everything seasonal and at the right time above all.

### Haley — 2015-01-25T01:19:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/haleys-journal.12626/post-153786>

Hi all! Wanted to give a quick update as I haven't been following the forums all week.

I have been adapting to what I think is ketosis rather well. I no longer wake up at night but sleep a solid 9hrs with many dreams. I don't crave carbs like I did before and am able to eat much less of them (about a few cups of leafy greens a day). I've been very satisfied with the reduced bloating I have experienced with this. The only time I have experienced bloating & felt fluid retention is right now which I believe is due to me being in the luteal phase of my cycle and an increased consumption of salt the last few days.

I am a bit frustrated with my lack of weight loss though. Ketosis seemed to have helped with bloating but I'm not noticing any real weight loss. I have been eating more total calories throughout the day, still lower than should be but I'm trying to increase gradually and see the effects. I'm worried I'm not active enough as i have just started school online. I walk about 6000-8000 steps a day, and do three 10 minute weight training sessions a week without stopping. But I do feel rather weak lately.

I have had bouts of nassau since starting the protocol and usually it happens after eating coconut butter at night. I had one episode this week where I had to throw up. And I'm not sure if it was the coconut butter or homemade gelatin I tried.

Despite my easing into this high seafood diet, ketosis way of life I have been struggling with depression. I have a history with it and usually it comes when I am not working and have structure. And my [Dermatillomania](http://www.xojane.com/healthy/%20http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dermatillomania) (obsessive cuticle picking) comes with it. I found this article: **[http://www.stopskinpickingcoach.com/2013/vitamin-d](http://www.stopskinpickingcoach.com/2013/vitamin-d)** suggesting Vitamin D deficiency is likely the culprit behind it..and maybe part of the reason for my depression too.
\*\*\*\*I have read on the blog that vitamin D through supplementation may not be effective. I try and get in the sun a couple times a day but I know I'm not getting very enough, especially for someone with a tan complexion. What do you guys recommend for getting more during the winter months???

All for now! Thanks for reading!

### Inger — 2015-01-25T14:28:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/haleys-journal.12626/post-153807>

Haley coconut butter have made me nauseous too. I would suggest to ditch it from your diet.
Do butter or other animal fat instead. Or pure raw coconut oil (can make some people nauseous too, never made me, but coconut butter did! Many times)

For depression;
GO OUTSIDE.
Every day. You must do this, even if you do not feel like it.... it will help huge.. take a slow walk in the woods... for a hour or longer if you can. Get daylight into your eyes. IMHO it is not as much about the vitD from sun as it is to get real daylight. When it is so gray outside, and I go out anyways.. i look up into the sky and it is super bright!!!! This is what you need. You need to go out rainy or snowy or whatever and look up into the sky. Do not think about it like exercise, juts be there... walk slowly... stop... look up into the sky.. suck in the bright light. Spend time with the nature. Do not watch down... lift your eyes.
In the evening... just keep is at dark as it gets.
These two things will help for sure. CT helps depression too. Just go on with the protocol, do not give up! Do short face dunks, open the windows.. especially at night. Cover up so you do not freeze, but keep it fresh and cold in the bedroom.

>

But my Hidradenitis Superativa (painful boils around groin area) definitely flares up. Makes me so frustrated! This made me look into **histamines** and I realized that along with chocolate, sardines and other seafood are also high in histamines. The symptoms of "flushing, insomnia, abdominal cramps, accelerated heart rate, difficulty regulating body temp, nausea" and more ([http://www.mindbodygreen.com/0-11175/everything-you-need-to-know-about-histamine-intolerance.html](http://www.mindbodygreen.com/0-11175/everything-you-need-to-know-about-histamine-intolerance.html)) are all things I have been experiencing often. And according to that article leaky gut/flora issues cause these foods to spark the histamine reaction. Found this very very interesting after my experience yesterday with eating so many sardines.....wondering what else i can do to accelerate the healing of my gut as I have been working at it for many years now..

Haley…sorry I didn't get around to reading this until this morning….there is a long thread here on the forum about histamine…it has been a big issue for me. Look into Histame…you can take it when you are eating high histamine foods that are good for you, like sardines. Fermented food are full of histamine, so that is another roadblock for building good gut flora, but fresh bone broth and Vitk will work and raw foods too. This can take a long time….just saying. Don't expect things to change overnight.

Three cans of sardines would do me in, but I eat one can regularly. Fresh frozen seafood has less histamine than stuff sitting around in a shop for a few days….it's just a matter of finding the stuff that works for you.

Also very familiar with depression, although this winter I've had none…(it's been getting better for the past three years) High cortisol was a big part of a stressed nervous system for me….Vit D is key, but only get it through light, not supplements. Inger's advise is perfect. Do you have a tanning salon nearby? Worth a try. Also some of the light machines that go up your nose might help…(again N=1, this was too much for me)

CT is the key around which all these other changes revolve….it starts everything working differently….so again, don't give up and just do what you can. Are you taking curcumin for your AI condition? Have a look at Biotics Research phenatropic….might help with anxiety/depression.

And, best medicine of all…..hang out with your horse!

PS…coconut manna or oil also made me nauseous unless it was my main fat of the day….you can overload your fat intake as your capacity to use it changes

### Haley — 2015-01-25T22:55:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/haleys-journal.12626/post-153829>

SeaHorse- thanks for sharing!

Im interested to hear there are products to help with histamine intolerance, but like other things, is it really the problem? Do you have any insight on what the deeper issue is that could be addressed so that intolerance is no longer an issue? What are your experiences with homemade gelatin? I attempted to make broth with all knuckle bones and ended up with gelatin & tallow. And im weary of the gelatin as I got sick afterward. I have read things about the free glutamates being too much for some people and to ease in slowly.

I feel my cortisol must be going down since i just quit my job last month and now am doing school full time online. So i have mornings to relax,etc. We do have a couple tanning salons and one has a bed that has higher UVB but still some UVA. Does that matter? I have been wanting to go since I love the feeling they give me but wonder if that would not be congruent with the season I am in?

I know I need to explore CT more but I just hate being cold-and I gave in about a week ago and started using more heat in my car, bed etc. Sleep is just worth it to me. I do finish my showers off cold but thats about it. Typical day here is between 30-40 f..so im exposed to cold often.

I am taking about 1,000mg a day of curcumin through food and supplements.

Do you mean that coconut manna gave you issues only when you combined it with other fats? That may be the problem. I tend to have EVOO, coconut butter & tallow all in one day because im trying to make sure im not eating too much protein. Do you find too much protein takes you out of ketosis? Im really actually not sure im in ketosis even though I eat under 20g carbs a day.

And you are right....walking my horse is the best part of my day

\----
Caroline- typically I eat 1/2 salmon with skin with some greens/seaweed. I keep it between 300-450 calories. I know i know i know i need to stop counting..again i am trying but habits are hard! And to be honest with you all I am having a very hard time allowing myself to increase calories on this protocol because I am trying to loose weight as part of getting healthier. I have been eating much more during the day than I used to and this has been giving me anxiety. Like right now i just finished lunch and I'm still hungry

\----
Nicld- thanks for showing me, I will look into it! My best (affordable) option is probably the sun itself..so i just need to suck it up and get cold and stop studying several times a day!

>

I tend to have EVOO, coconut butter & tallow all in one day because im trying to make sure im not eating too much protein. Do you find too much protein takes you out of ketosis? Im really actually not sure im in ketosis even though I eat under 20g carbs a day.

This is my take on histamine….it will get better when everything else gets better, but in the meantime, don't aggravate the issue by flooding your body with histamines….it messes up your water/collagen structures (more blogs to read) and irritates the lining of your gut etc. etc. Glutamates from broths and gelatine can definitely be another irritant….particularly those with mood/mental challenges need to watch it. As for tanning, I would give it a go….see how your mood responds. It may be too much energy coming in or it may just be wonderful….only you can tell. It is a mismatch with the seasons, but depending on how much chaos we have created, sometimes we need to go outside the "perfect" set up and work with what we need at the moment…eventually you might be able to handle winter as an optimal mammal, but not yet…..just keep working on getting there…it's all a grand experiment, so keep that in mind with all this advise…

As for coconut manna and oil, it just seemed to tip me over the edge unless it was the only fat I ate. I can't combine it and now I don't eat it in the winter much….at first I did. Too much protein was a problem for me as I converted it to sugar, but I couldn't burn fat properly either for a long time….still not totally there, but it's all getter better. Try to get properly hydrated what ever you do…that was a big issue for me.

Ketosis is slippery subject….it's again a process to find what works best and then realize that it will change throughout the year….all the best

(I can’t believe I would do it…but i’ve come a long way in my trying new things!). I hope I could get it down! Still cannot eat a second bite of liver…

**caroline**\- right. My assumption would be that regaining leptin sensitivity would result in weight loss though, no? not making my efforts about weight loss but rather hoping that would be an effect of getting healthier.

**Yewwei.tan-**I will be sending you back a private message with more detail. Thank you for putting time in to help me. But I will say…your post is a bit discouraging.
